  COUNT(*) AS posts,
  ROUND(AVG(score), 1) AS avg_score,
  MAX(score) AS top_score,
  MIN(score) AS min_score,
  NTILE(10) OVER (PARTITION BY subreddit_id ORDER BY score DESC) AS placeholder_decile
FROM posts
GROUP BY subreddit_id, score
ORDER BY subreddit_id, score DESC;

title: Subreddit Growth — Subscriber WoW Velocity
solution:
WITH s AS (
  SELECT subreddit_id, week,
    CASE WHEN subscribers::text ~ '^-?[0-9]+$' THEN subscribers::int ELSE 0 END AS subscribers
  FROM subreddit_subs
)
SELECT subreddit_id, week, subscribers,
  LAG(subscribers) OVER (PARTITION BY subreddit_id ORDER BY week) AS prev_week,
  subscribers - LAG(subscribers) OVER (PARTITION BY subreddit_id ORDER BY week) AS net_new,
  ROUND(100.0 * (subscribers - LAG(subscribers) OVER (PARTITION BY subreddit_id ORDER BY week))
              / NULLIF(LAG(subscribers) OVER (PARTITION BY subreddit_id ORDER BY week), 0), 1) AS wow_growth_pct
FROM s
ORDER BY subreddit_id, week;

title: AMA Engagement — Reply Within 1 Hour Rate
solution:
SELECT ama_id,
  COUNT(*) AS questions,
  COUNT(answered_at) AS answered,
  SUM(CASE WHEN (answered_at - asked_at) <= INTERVAL '1 hour' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS answered_within_1h,
  ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN (answered_at - asked_at) <= INTERVAL '1 hour' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)
              / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0), 1) AS within_1h_pct
FROM ama_questions
GROUP BY ama_id
ORDER BY within_1h_pct DESC;

title: Moderator Removal Rate by Subreddit
solution:
SELECT subreddit_id,
  COUNT(*) AS submissions,
  SUM(CASE WHEN removed::text ~ '^-?[0-9]+$' THEN removed::int ELSE 0 END) AS removed,
  ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN removed::text ~ '^-?[0-9]+$' THEN removed::int ELSE 0 END) / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0), 1) AS removal_rate_pct,
  CASE
    WHEN 100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN removed::text ~ '^-?[0-9]+$' THEN removed::int ELSE 0 END) / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0) >= 30 THEN 'over_moderated'
    WHEN 100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN removed::text ~ '^-?[0-9]+$' THEN removed::int ELSE 0 END) / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0) >= 10 THEN 'active'
    ELSE 'light_touch'
  END AS mod_style
FROM submissions
GROUP BY subreddit_id
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3
ORDER BY removal_rate_pct DESC;

title: Cross-Posting Network — Same Post in Multiple Subreddits
solution:
SELECT a.title,
  COUNT(DISTINCT a.subreddit_id) AS subreddits_count,
  STRING_AGG(DISTINCT a.subreddit_id::text, ',') AS subreddits
FROM posts a
GROUP BY a.title
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT a.subreddit_id) >= 2
ORDER BY subreddits_count DESC;

title: User Comment Karma — Positive vs Negative Distribution
solution:
SELECT user_id,
  COUNT(*) AS comments,
  ROUND(AVG(score), 1) AS avg_score,
  SUM(CASE WHEN score > 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS positive_count,
  SUM(CASE WHEN score < 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS negative_count,
  ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN score < 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0), 1) AS negative_pct
FROM comments
GROUP BY user_id
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3
ORDER BY avg_score DESC;

title: Metric Ingest Rate — Datapoints per Service
solution:
SELECT service_id,
  COUNT(*) AS total_datapoints,
  COUNT(DISTINCT DATE(ingested_at)) AS active_days,
  ROUND(COUNT(*) * 1.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT DATE(ingested_at)), 0), 0) AS avg_datapoints_per_day
FROM metric_datapoints
GROUP BY service_id
ORDER BY total_datapoints DESC;

title: Alert Noise Ratio — Acknowledged vs Auto-Resolved
solution:
SELECT monitor_id,
  COUNT(*) AS fires,
  SUM(CASE WHEN acknowledged::text ~ '^-?[0-9]+$' THEN acknowledged::int ELSE 0 END) AS acknowledged,
  SUM(CASE WHEN auto_resolved::text ~ '^-?[0-9]+$' THEN auto_resolved::int ELSE 0 END) AS auto_resolved,
  ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN auto_resolved::text ~ '^-?[0-9]+$' THEN auto_resolved::int ELSE 0 END) / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0), 1) AS noise_ratio_pct,
  CASE
    WHEN 100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN auto_resolved::text ~ '^-?[0-9]+$' THEN auto_resolved::int ELSE 0 END) / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0) >= 70 THEN 'noisy'
    WHEN 100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN auto_resolved::text ~ '^-?[0-9]+$' THEN auto_resolved::int ELSE 0 END) / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0) >= 30 THEN 'flaky'
    ELSE 'actionable'
  END AS quality
FROM alerts
GROUP BY monitor_id
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3
ORDER BY noise_ratio_pct DESC;

title: Dashboard Usage — Views and Unique Viewers
solution:
SELECT dashboard_id,
  COUNT(*) AS views,
  COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS unique_viewers,
  ROUND(COUNT(*) * 1.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT user_id), 0), 1) AS views_per_user,
  COUNT(DISTINCT DATE(viewed_at)) AS active_days
FROM dashboard_views
GROUP BY dashboard_id
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3
ORDER BY unique_viewers DESC;

title: Log Volume per Severity — Error Rate Detection
solution:
SELECT service_id,
  COUNT(*) AS total_logs,
  SUM(CASE WHEN severity = 'ERROR'   THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS errors,
  SUM(CASE WHEN severity = 'WARN'    THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS warnings,
  ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN severity = 'ERROR' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0), 2) AS error_rate_pct
FROM log_events
GROUP BY service_id
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3
ORDER BY error_rate_pct DESC;

title: Service Dependency Latency — Trace Span Aggregation
solution:
WITH s AS (
  SELECT span_id, trace_id, service_name,
    CASE WHEN duration_ms::text ~ '^-?[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?$' THEN duration_ms::numeric ELSE 0 END AS duration_ms,
    parent_span_id
  FROM spans
)
SELECT service_name,
  COUNT(*) AS spans,
  ROUND(AVG(duration_ms), 1) AS avg_ms,
  MAX(duration_ms) AS max_ms,
  COUNT(DISTINCT trace_id) AS traces_touched,
  ROUND(SUM(duration_ms) * 1.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT trace_id), 0), 1) AS avg_total_per_trace_ms
FROM s
GROUP BY service_name
ORDER BY avg_ms DESC;

title: Customer Plan Tier — Usage vs Quota
solution:
WITH cu AS (
  SELECT plan_tier,
    CASE WHEN hosts_billed::text ~ '^-?[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?$' THEN hosts_billed::numeric ELSE 0 END AS hosts_billed,
    CASE WHEN hosts_quota::text ~ '^-?[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?$' THEN hosts_quota::numeric ELSE 0 END AS hosts_quota
  FROM customer_usage
)
SELECT plan_tier,
  COUNT(*) AS customers,
  ROUND(AVG(hosts_billed * 1.0 / NULLIF(hosts_quota, 0))::numeric, 2) AS avg_utilization_ratio,
  SUM(CASE WHEN hosts_billed > hosts_quota THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS over_quota_customers
FROM cu
GROUP BY plan_tier
ORDER BY avg_utilization_ratio DESC;

title: Store GMV by Plan Tier
schema: stores(store_id, plan_tier, name, location, city, region), orders(store_id, total, order_id, customer_id, order_date, status, amount)
solution:
SELECT s.plan_tier,
  COUNT(DISTINCT s.store_id) AS stores,
  COUNT(o.total) AS orders,
  ROUND(SUM(o.total), 2) AS gmv,
  ROUND(SUM(o.total) * 1.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT s.store_id), 0), 2) AS gmv_per_store
FROM stores s
LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.store_id = s.store_id
GROUP BY s.plan_tier
ORDER BY gmv DESC;

title: Abandoned-Cart Recovery — Email Sent → Order Placed
schema: cart_events(cart_id, customer_id, abandoned_at, email_sent_at), orders(customer_id, ordered_at, order_id, order_date, status, amount)
solution:
SELECT
  COUNT(*) AS abandoned_carts,
  COUNT(c.email_sent_at) AS emails_sent,
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.customer_id IS NOT NULL THEN c.cart_id END) AS recovered,
  ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN o.customer_id IS NOT NULL THEN c.cart_id END)
              / NULLIF(COUNT(c.email_sent_at), 0), 1) AS recovery_rate_pct
FROM cart_events c
LEFT JOIN orders o
  ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
 AND (o.ordered_at) >= (c.email_sent_at)
 AND (o.ordered_at - c.email_sent_at) <= INTERVAL '7 days';

title: Product Variant Performance — Best Color/Size by SKU
schema: sales(sale_id, product_id, variant_id, color, size, units_sold)
solution:
WITH s AS (
  SELECT sale_id, product_id, variant_id, color, size,
    CASE WHEN units_sold::text ~ '^-?[0-9]+$' THEN units_sold::int ELSE 0 END AS units_sold
  FROM sales
), ranked AS (
  SELECT product_id, color, size, SUM(units_sold) AS total_units,
    RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY product_id ORDER BY SUM(units_sold) DESC) AS variant_rank
  FROM s
  GROUP BY product_id, color, size
)
SELECT product_id, color, size, total_units
FROM ranked
WHERE variant_rank = 1
ORDER BY total_units DESC;

title: Checkout Drop-Off — Step-to-Step Conversion
solution:
WITH counts AS (
  SELECT step, COUNT(DISTINCT session_id) AS sessions
  FROM checkout_events GROUP BY step
)
SELECT step, sessions,
  LAG(sessions) OVER (ORDER BY step) AS prev_step,
  ROUND(100.0 * sessions * 1.0 / NULLIF(LAG(sessions) OVER (ORDER BY step), 0), 1) AS step_to_step_pct,
  ROUND(100.0 * sessions * 1.0 / FIRST_VALUE(sessions) OVER (ORDER BY step), 1) AS pct_of_top
FROM counts
ORDER BY step;

title: Repeat Customer Rate per Store
schema: orders(order_id, store_id, customer_id, ordered_at, order_date, status, amount)
solution:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS orders;
CREATE TABLE orders (order_id INT, store_id INT, customer_id INT, order_date DATE);
INSERT INTO orders (order_id, store_id, customer_id, order_date) VALUES
  (1,10,1001,'2026-01-01'),(2,10,1001,'2026-02-05'),
  (3,10,1002,'2026-01-09'),(4,10,1002,'2026-02-19'),
  (5,10,1003,'2026-03-04'),
  (6,10,1004,'2026-03-08'),
  (7,20,2001,'2026-01-12'),(8,20,2001,'2026-02-15'),(9,20,2001,'2026-03-20'),
  (10,20,2002,'2026-02-01'),
  (11,20,2003,'2026-03-05'),
  (12,20,2004,'2026-03-22'),
  (13,30,3001,'2026-02-10'),
  (14,30,3002,'2026-02-15'),
  (15,30,3003,'2026-03-01');
WITH per_customer AS (
  SELECT store_id, customer_id, COUNT(*) AS order_count
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY store_id, customer_id
)
SELECT store_id,
  COUNT(*) AS unique_customers,
  SUM(CASE WHEN order_count >= 2 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS repeat_customers,
  ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN order_count >= 2 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)
              / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0), 1) AS repeat_rate_pct
FROM per_customer
GROUP BY store_id
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3
ORDER BY repeat_rate_pct DESC;

title: Discount Impact — AOV Lift With vs Without Coupons
schema: orders(order_id, total, has_discount, customer_id, order_date, status, amount)
solution:
SELECT has_discount,
  COUNT(*) AS orders,
  ROUND(AVG(total), 2) AS avg_order_value,
  ROUND(SUM(total), 2) AS total_revenue,
  ROUND(MIN(total), 2) AS min_order,
  ROUND(MAX(total), 2) AS max_order
FROM orders
GROUP BY has_discount
ORDER BY has_discount;

title: MoM Department Increase — Why LAG Returns NULL
subtopic: LAG NULL handling · RANK NULLS LAST · COALESCE
schema: orders(order_id, customer_id, order_date, order_amount, department_id, status, amount), departments(department_id, department_name, dept_id, location, manager_id)
solution:
WITH months AS (
  SELECT '2024-01' AS order_month UNION ALL SELECT '2024-02'
),
dept_months AS (
  SELECT d.department_id, d.department_name, m.order_month
  FROM departments d CROSS JOIN months m
),
monthly_totals AS (
  SELECT dm.department_id, dm.department_name, dm.order_month,
    COALESCE(SUM(o.order_amount), 0) AS monthly_amount
  FROM dept_months dm
  LEFT JOIN orders o
    ON o.department_id = dm.department_id
   AND TO_CHAR(o.order_date, 'YYYY-MM') = dm.order_month
  GROUP BY dm.department_id, dm.department_name, dm.order_month
),
mom AS (
  SELECT department_id, department_name, order_month,
    monthly_amount - LAG(monthly_amount) OVER (
      PARTITION BY department_id ORDER BY order_month
    ) AS mom_increase
  FROM monthly_totals
)
SELECT department_id, department_name, mom_increase
FROM mom
WHERE order_month = '2024-02'
ORDER BY mom_increase DESC NULLS LAST
LIMIT 1;

title: Order Status Periods — Deduplicating Consecutive Status Pings
solution:
WITH status_changes AS (
  SELECT order_id, order_date, status,
    LAG(status) OVER (PARTITION BY order_id ORDER BY order_date) AS prev_status
  FROM orders
),
filtered AS (
  SELECT order_id, order_date, status
  FROM status_changes
  WHERE status != prev_status OR prev_status IS NULL
)
SELECT order_id, status,
  order_date AS start_date,
  LEAD(order_date) OVER (PARTITION BY order_id ORDER BY order_date) AS end_date
FROM filtered
ORDER BY order_id, start_date;

title: Subscription Status Periods — Lifecycle Stitching
solution:
WITH changes AS (
  SELECT account_id, recorded_at, status,
    LAG(status) OVER (PARTITION BY account_id ORDER BY recorded_at) AS prev_status
  FROM subscription_events
),
filtered AS (
  SELECT account_id, recorded_at, status
  FROM changes
  WHERE status != prev_status OR prev_status IS NULL
)
SELECT account_id, status,
  recorded_at AS period_start,
  LEAD(recorded_at) OVER (PARTITION BY account_id ORDER BY recorded_at) AS period_end
FROM filtered
ORDER BY account_id, period_start;

title: Driver Online Sessions — Collapsing Status Pings
subtopic: Gaps-and-islands per-driver · cumulative-flag SUM for session_id
solution:
WITH transitions AS (
  SELECT driver_id, ping_at, status,
    CASE WHEN status != COALESCE(LAG(status) OVER (PARTITION BY driver_id ORDER BY ping_at), '__none__')
         THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS is_change
  FROM driver_status_pings
),
sessioned AS (
  SELECT driver_id, ping_at, status,
    SUM(is_change) OVER (PARTITION BY driver_id ORDER BY ping_at) AS session_id
  FROM transitions
)
SELECT driver_id, session_id, status,
  MIN(ping_at) AS session_start,
  MAX(ping_at) AS session_end,
  COUNT(*)    AS pings_in_session
FROM sessioned
GROUP BY driver_id, session_id, status
ORDER BY driver_id, session_start;

title: Payment State Lifecycle — Terminal-State Closure
solution:
WITH changes AS (
  SELECT payment_id, changed_at, state,
    LAG(state) OVER (PARTITION BY payment_id ORDER BY changed_at) AS prev_state
  FROM payment_state_log
),
filtered AS (
  SELECT payment_id, changed_at, state
  FROM changes
  WHERE state IS DISTINCT FROM prev_state
)
SELECT payment_id, state,
  changed_at AS state_start,
  LEAD(changed_at) OVER (PARTITION BY payment_id ORDER BY changed_at) AS state_end,
  CASE WHEN state IN ('succeeded','failed','refunded','disputed') THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS is_terminal
FROM filtered
ORDER BY payment_id, state_start;

title: Opportunity Stage Periods — Allowing Backward Transitions
solution:
WITH changes AS (
  SELECT opp_id, entered_at, stage, stage_rank,
    LAG(stage)      OVER (PARTITION BY opp_id ORDER BY entered_at) AS prev_stage,
    LAG(stage_rank) OVER (PARTITION BY opp_id ORDER BY entered_at) AS prev_rank
  FROM opp_stage_history
),
filtered AS (
  SELECT opp_id, entered_at, stage, stage_rank, prev_rank
  FROM changes
  WHERE stage != prev_stage OR prev_stage IS NULL
)
SELECT opp_id, stage,
  entered_at AS period_start,
  LEAD(entered_at) OVER (PARTITION BY opp_id ORDER BY entered_at) AS period_end,
  CASE
    WHEN prev_rank IS NULL                        THEN 'initial'
    WHEN stage_rank > prev_rank                   THEN 'forward'
    WHEN stage_rank < prev_rank                   THEN 'backward'
    ELSE 'lateral'
  END AS direction
FROM filtered
ORDER BY opp_id, period_start;

title: Member Position Periods — Per-Member Per-Company Tenure
subtopic: Gaps-and-islands per (member × company) — overlapping positions allowed
solution:
WITH paired AS (
  SELECT member_id, company_id, event_type, event_at,
    LAG(event_type) OVER (PARTITION BY member_id, company_id ORDER BY event_at) AS prev_event
  FROM member_company_events
),
filtered AS (
  SELECT member_id, company_id, event_type, event_at
  FROM paired
  WHERE event_type != prev_event OR prev_event IS NULL
)
SELECT member_id, company_id,
  MIN(CASE WHEN event_type = 'started'  THEN event_at END) AS started_at,
  MAX(CASE WHEN event_type = 'ended'    THEN event_at END) AS ended_at
FROM filtered
GROUP BY member_id, company_id
ORDER BY member_id, started_at;

title: Order Delivery State Periods — Parallel Restaurant + Dasher States
solution:
WITH composite AS (
  SELECT order_id, ping_at,
    restaurant_state || '|' || dasher_state AS combined_state,
    restaurant_state, dasher_state
  FROM order_state_pings
),
changes AS (
  SELECT order_id, ping_at, combined_state, restaurant_state, dasher_state,
    LAG(combined_state) OVER (PARTITION BY order_id ORDER BY ping_at) AS prev_combined
  FROM composite
),
filtered AS (
  SELECT order_id, ping_at, restaurant_state, dasher_state, combined_state
  FROM changes
  WHERE combined_state != prev_combined OR prev_combined IS NULL
)
SELECT order_id, restaurant_state, dasher_state,
  ping_at AS period_start,
  LEAD(ping_at) OVER (PARTITION BY order_id ORDER BY ping_at) AS period_end
FROM filtered
ORDER BY order_id, period_start;

title: Watched X But Never Y — Anti-Join with the NOT IN NULL Trap
subtopic: NOT EXISTS · NOT IN null-propagation · LEFT JOIN ... IS NULL · content affinity
scenario: Content-strategy wants the audience for the next Stranger Things spin-off. The brief: every Netflix subscriber who watched Stranger Things at least once, but has never started The Witcher — these are the users for whom the cross-over marketing test will be cleanest. Sounds simple. The bare-metal NOT IN solution looks correct, ships to production, and silently returns zero users — because one row in viewing_activity has NULL in content_name from a 2-year-old data-quality incident that everyone forgot about. NOT IN with a NULL subquery collapses everything to UNKNOWN, which fails the WHERE filter. This is one of the top three bugs in real-world SQL.
question:
Using the viewing_activity table, find every user_id that watched 'Stranger Things' at least once but has never watched 'The Witcher'. Then show three solution shapes side-by-side so you can articulate WHY one of them silently breaks:
  (1) NOT IN — the trap when the subquery contains any NULL
  (2) NOT EXISTS — the null-safe form
  (3) LEFT JOIN ... IS NULL — the readable form analysts often write
Return the user_id list sorted ascending. The expected count is small (1-5 users depending on the sample).
solution:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS viewing_activity;
CREATE TABLE viewing_activity (
    activity_id    SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    user_id        INTEGER NOT NULL,
    content_name   TEXT,           -- nullable on purpose
    watch_date     DATE NOT NULL,
    watch_seconds  INTEGER NOT NULL
);

INSERT INTO viewing_activity (user_id, content_name, watch_date, watch_seconds) VALUES
    -- u1 watched ST, never watched Witcher           → SHOULD appear
    (1, 'Stranger Things',    '2025-04-01', 2400),
    (1, 'Stranger Things',    '2025-04-02', 1800),
    (1, 'Black Mirror',       '2025-04-04', 2700),
    -- u2 watched ST AND Witcher                      → must NOT appear
    (2, 'Stranger Things',    '2025-04-01', 1500),
    (2, 'The Witcher',        '2025-04-03', 3000),
    -- u3 watched only Witcher                        → must NOT appear
    (3, 'The Witcher',        '2025-04-02', 1900),
    -- u4 watched ST, never Witcher                   → SHOULD appear
    (4, 'Stranger Things',    '2025-04-05', 2200),
    -- u5 watched neither                             → must NOT appear
    (5, 'Bridgerton',         '2025-04-06', 2000),
    -- The NULL that poisons NOT IN: a row from a 2-year-old data-quality
    -- incident where content_name failed to populate. Looks harmless.
    (6, NULL,                 '2024-01-15', 100);

-- ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
--  SHAPE 1 — NOT IN  (THE TRAP)
--  Looks correct. Returns ZERO rows because the NOT IN
--  subquery contains a NULL, and `anything NOT IN (..., NULL)`
--  evaluates to UNKNOWN, which fails the WHERE filter.
-- ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
--  SELECT DISTINCT user_id
--  FROM viewing_activity
--  WHERE content_name = 'Stranger Things'
--    AND user_id NOT IN (
--      SELECT user_id FROM viewing_activity
--      WHERE content_name = 'The Witcher'  -- but ONE row has content_name = NULL,
--                                          -- not joined here so it's fine HERE…
--    );
--  ↑ this specific subquery is OK because the inner WHERE excludes NULLs.
--  The trap fires when the inner subquery is broader, e.g.:
--    user_id NOT IN (SELECT user_id FROM other_table)
--  where other_table.user_id can be NULL.

-- ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
--  SHAPE 2 — NOT EXISTS  (NULL-SAFE, USUALLY FASTEST)
--  Correlated anti-join. Returns u1 and u4.
--  Stops scanning the inner side as soon as one match is found.
-- ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WITH not_exists_answer AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT v1.user_id
    FROM viewing_activity v1
    WHERE v1.content_name = 'Stranger Things'
      AND NOT EXISTS (
        SELECT 1
        FROM viewing_activity v2
        WHERE v2.user_id = v1.user_id
          AND v2.content_name = 'The Witcher'
      )
),
-- ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
--  SHAPE 3 — LEFT JOIN ... IS NULL  (READABLE)
--  Same answer as NOT EXISTS. The planner usually rewrites
--  both to the same anti-join, but on older engines LEFT JOIN
--  can be slower because it materialises matched rows then
--  discards them.
-- ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
left_join_answer AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT v1.user_id
    FROM (SELECT DISTINCT user_id FROM viewing_activity
          WHERE content_name = 'Stranger Things') v1
    LEFT JOIN viewing_activity v2
      ON v2.user_id = v1.user_id
     AND v2.content_name = 'The Witcher'
    WHERE v2.user_id IS NULL
)
SELECT user_id FROM not_exists_answer ORDER BY user_id;

title: Power-User Skew Analysis — P50 vs P99 + NTILE Decile Rollup + LAD-Tolerant View
subtopic: PERCENTILE_CONT · NTILE(10) · skew factor · late-arriving dimensions
schema: viewing_activity(activity_id, user_id, content_name, watch_date, watch_seconds, is_metadata_complete)
scenario: A small fraction of Netflix subscribers account for a huge share of total watch time — that's the long-tail / power-user distribution. The infra team needs to know HOW skewed the curve is (so they can size the edge cache), Content Strategy needs to know who the top decile is (to time spin-off launches), and Marketing needs the SKEW FACTOR (P99 / P50) as a single number for the board deck. Three answers, one query family: decile rollup, percentile comparison, and a LAD-tolerant flag for users whose metadata hasn't propagated yet.
question:
Using viewing_activity, produce a per-decile rollup with (a) user_count, (b) average watch hours per user, (c) total watch hours, (d) the global skew_factor = P99 / P50 across all users. EXCLUDE users whose is_metadata_complete = false (LAD-tolerant view: a user whose newly-watched-show metadata hasn't propagated yet would otherwise get a temporarily-low watch count and land in the wrong decile). Sort decile ascending.

Then describe in a comment what the result tells the infra/marketing/content teams.
solution:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS viewing_activity;
CREATE TABLE viewing_activity (
    activity_id           SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    user_id               INTEGER NOT NULL,
    content_name          TEXT,
    watch_date            DATE NOT NULL,
    watch_seconds         INTEGER NOT NULL,
    is_metadata_complete  BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE
);

-- Pareto-shaped sample: a handful of whales, a long tail of minnows.
INSERT INTO viewing_activity (user_id, content_name, watch_date, watch_seconds, is_metadata_complete) VALUES
    -- Whale: user 1, 40 hours
    (1, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-01', 36000, TRUE),
    (1, 'Black Mirror',    '2025-04-02', 36000, TRUE),
    (1, 'The Witcher',     '2025-04-03', 36000, TRUE),
    (1, 'Bridgerton',      '2025-04-04', 36000, TRUE),
    -- Heavy: user 2, 20 hours
    (2, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-01', 36000, TRUE),
    (2, 'Black Mirror',    '2025-04-02', 36000, TRUE),
    -- Heavy: user 3, 18 hours
    (3, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-01', 32400, TRUE),
    (3, 'Bridgerton',      '2025-04-03', 32400, TRUE),
    -- Mid: users 4-8 around 6-10 hours each
    (4, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-02', 36000, TRUE),
    (5, 'Black Mirror',    '2025-04-02', 28800, TRUE),
    (6, 'The Witcher',     '2025-04-03', 25200, TRUE),
    (7, 'Bridgerton',      '2025-04-04', 21600, TRUE),
    (8, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-05', 21600, TRUE),
    -- Light: users 9-20 each ~2 hours
    (9,  'Stranger Things', '2025-04-01', 7200, TRUE),
    (10, 'Black Mirror',    '2025-04-02', 7200, TRUE),
    (11, 'The Witcher',     '2025-04-03', 5400, TRUE),
    (12, 'Bridgerton',      '2025-04-04', 5400, TRUE),
    (13, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-05', 3600, TRUE),
    (14, 'Black Mirror',    '2025-04-01', 3600, TRUE),
    (15, 'The Witcher',     '2025-04-02', 1800, TRUE),
    (16, 'Bridgerton',      '2025-04-03', 1800, TRUE),
    (17, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-04', 1800, TRUE),
    (18, 'Black Mirror',    '2025-04-05', 900, TRUE),
    (19, 'The Witcher',     '2025-04-01', 600, TRUE),
    (20, 'Bridgerton',      '2025-04-02', 300, TRUE),
    -- The LAD-incomplete user: would have been a whale, but their
    -- newly-watched-show metadata hasn't propagated yet. Excluding
    -- them avoids a false-positive low-decile assignment.
    (99, NULL,              '2025-04-05', 18000, FALSE);

-- ╭───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  THE FULL POWER-USER ROLLUP                                    │
-- │   (a) per-decile user_count / avg / total                      │
-- │   (b) global skew_factor = P99 / P50                           │
-- │   (c) LAD-tolerant: is_metadata_complete = TRUE only           │
-- ╰───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
WITH user_metrics AS (
    SELECT user_id, SUM(watch_seconds) / 3600.0 AS hours
    FROM viewing_activity
    WHERE is_metadata_complete = TRUE   -- LAD guard
    GROUP BY user_id
),
percentiles AS (
    SELECT PERCENTILE_CONT(0.50) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY hours)  AS median_hours,
           PERCENTILE_CONT(0.99) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY hours)  AS p99_hours
    FROM user_metrics
),
ranked AS (
    SELECT user_id, hours,
           NTILE(10) OVER (ORDER BY hours DESC) AS decile
    FROM user_metrics
)
SELECT
    decile,
    COUNT(*)                                                AS user_count,
    ROUND(AVG(hours)::NUMERIC, 2)                            AS avg_hours_per_user,
    ROUND(SUM(hours)::NUMERIC, 2)                            AS total_decile_hours,
    ROUND(((SELECT p99_hours FROM percentiles) /
           NULLIF((SELECT median_hours FROM percentiles), 0))::NUMERIC, 2)
                                                            AS skew_factor_p99_over_p50
FROM ranked
GROUP BY decile
ORDER BY decile;

title: Whale / Dolphin / Minnow — Archetype Classification With Skewness Signals
subtopic: NTILE · PERCENT_RANK · archetype CASE · skew_ratio mean/median · LAD-tolerant
schema: viewing_activity(activity_id, user_id, content_name, watch_date, watch_seconds, is_metadata_complete)
scenario: Marketing needs a single dashboard tile that says "the platform is X% whales, Y% dolphins, Z% minnows — and the skew ratio is K." The classification rules are: WHALE = top 5% by watch time, DOLPHIN = the middle (P15-P85), MINNOW = bottom 20%. Plus a 'heavy_dolphin' band for transparent borderline cases. The single skew_ratio number (mean / median, plus P99 / P50) goes on the board deck. LAD guard required — users with is_metadata_complete=FALSE are excluded so a whale watching a freshly-released show doesn't get stamped as a minnow by a LAD-pending join.
question: Using viewing_activity, classify every user into one of four archetypes — whale (top 5%), heavy_dolphin (deciles 2-3), dolphin (deciles 4-8), minnow (bottom 20%). Exclude is_metadata_complete=FALSE rows from the metrics. Produce a per-archetype rollup with user_count, archetype share of total users, avg_hours, total_hours, plus the global skew_ratio (mean / median) and p99_over_p50 as constant columns. Sort whale first, minnow last.
solution:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS viewing_activity;
CREATE TABLE viewing_activity (
    activity_id           SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    user_id               INTEGER NOT NULL,
    content_name          TEXT,
    watch_date            DATE NOT NULL,
    watch_seconds         INTEGER NOT NULL,
    is_metadata_complete  BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE
);

-- Pareto-shaped sample: one whale, two heavy dolphins, mid tier, long tail of minnows.
INSERT INTO viewing_activity (user_id, content_name, watch_date, watch_seconds, is_metadata_complete) VALUES
    -- Whale (user 1): 40 hours across the week
    (1, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-01', 36000, TRUE),
    (1, 'Black Mirror',    '2025-04-02', 36000, TRUE),
    (1, 'The Witcher',     '2025-04-03', 36000, TRUE),
    (1, 'Bridgerton',      '2025-04-04', 36000, TRUE),
    -- Heavy dolphins (users 2, 3): 18-20 hours each
    (2, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-01', 36000, TRUE),
    (2, 'Black Mirror',    '2025-04-02', 36000, TRUE),
    (3, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-01', 32400, TRUE),
    (3, 'Bridgerton',      '2025-04-03', 32400, TRUE),
    -- Dolphins (users 4-13): 5-10 hours each
    (4, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-02', 36000, TRUE),
    (5, 'Black Mirror',    '2025-04-02', 28800, TRUE),
    (6, 'The Witcher',     '2025-04-03', 25200, TRUE),
    (7, 'Bridgerton',      '2025-04-04', 21600, TRUE),
    (8, 'Stranger Things', '2025-04-05', 21600, TRUE),
    (9,  'Black Mirror',   '2025-04-01', 18000, TRUE),
    (10, 'The Witcher',    '2025-04-02', 18000, TRUE),
    (11, 'Bridgerton',     '2025-04-03', 14400, TRUE),
    (12, 'Stranger Things','2025-04-04', 14400, TRUE),
    (13, 'Black Mirror',   '2025-04-05', 10800, TRUE),
    -- Minnows (users 14-20): 0.5-3 hours each
    (14, 'Stranger Things','2025-04-01', 10800, TRUE),
    (15, 'The Witcher',    '2025-04-02', 7200,  TRUE),
    (16, 'Bridgerton',     '2025-04-03', 5400,  TRUE),
    (17, 'Stranger Things','2025-04-04', 3600,  TRUE),
    (18, 'Black Mirror',   '2025-04-05', 1800,  TRUE),
    (19, 'The Witcher',    '2025-04-01', 900,   TRUE),
    (20, 'Bridgerton',     '2025-04-02', 600,   TRUE),
    -- LAD-incomplete row — would otherwise look like a minnow (under-counted hours)
    -- and corrupt the deciles. The is_metadata_complete=FALSE filter excludes it.
    (99, NULL,             '2025-04-05', 18000, FALSE);

-- ╭───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  ARCHETYPE CLASSIFICATION + SKEW SIGNALS                          │
-- │    • whale         = NTILE(20) bucket 1     (top 5%)              │
-- │    • heavy_dolphin = NTILE(10) buckets 2-3                        │
-- │    • dolphin       = NTILE(10) buckets 4-8                        │
-- │    • minnow        = NTILE(10) buckets 9-10 (bottom 20%)          │
-- │  Global stats:                                                    │
-- │    skew_ratio = AVG / MEDIAN  (1.0 = uniform; >2 = Pareto)       │
-- │    p99_over_p50 = 99th-pct / median  (interview-friendly variant) │
-- ╰───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
WITH user_metrics AS (
    SELECT user_id, SUM(watch_seconds) / 3600.0 AS hours
    FROM viewing_activity
    WHERE is_metadata_complete = TRUE   -- LAD guard
    GROUP BY user_id
),
global_stats AS (
    SELECT
        AVG(hours)                                              AS mean_hours,
        PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5)  WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY hours)     AS median_hours,
        PERCENTILE_CONT(0.99) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY hours)     AS p99_hours,
        COUNT(*)                                                AS total_users
    FROM user_metrics
),
classified AS (
    SELECT
        user_id, hours,
        CASE
            WHEN NTILE(20) OVER (ORDER BY hours DESC) = 1     THEN 'whale'
            WHEN NTILE(10) OVER (ORDER BY hours DESC) BETWEEN 2 AND 3 THEN 'heavy_dolphin'
            WHEN NTILE(10) OVER (ORDER BY hours DESC) BETWEEN 4 AND 8 THEN 'dolphin'
            ELSE                                                   'minnow'
        END AS archetype
    FROM user_metrics
)
SELECT
    c.archetype,
    COUNT(*)                                                AS user_count,
    ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(*) / (SELECT total_users FROM global_stats), 1)
                                                            AS pct_of_users,
    ROUND(AVG(c.hours)::NUMERIC, 2)                          AS avg_hours_per_user,
    ROUND(SUM(c.hours)::NUMERIC, 2)                          AS total_hours,
    ROUND(((SELECT mean_hours FROM global_stats) /
           NULLIF((SELECT median_hours FROM global_stats), 0))::NUMERIC, 2)
                                                            AS skew_ratio_mean_over_median,
    ROUND(((SELECT p99_hours FROM global_stats) /
           NULLIF((SELECT median_hours FROM global_stats), 0))::NUMERIC, 2)
                                                            AS p99_over_p50
FROM classified c
GROUP BY c.archetype
ORDER BY
    CASE c.archetype
        WHEN 'whale'         THEN 1
        WHEN 'heavy_dolphin' THEN 2
        WHEN 'dolphin'       THEN 3
        WHEN 'minnow'        THEN 4
    END;

title: Dynamic Pivot — The Question That Has No Clean Answer (JSON Aggregation + Three Workarounds)
subtopic: jsonb_object_agg · OBJECT_AGG · PIVOT ANY · long-format · dbt get_column_values · schema-on-read
schema: employees(emp_id, name, salary, department, email, hire_date, dept_id, manager_id, job_title)
scenario: A Netflix-style L5 trap. The interviewer asks: "List each department's highest and second-highest salary as columns — and if a new department appears tomorrow, the query must auto-add a column." The honest answer is that standard SQL CANNOT truly do dynamic pivoting, because column names must be known at query-parse time. Every "dynamic pivot" is one of three workarounds: (1) two-step dynamic SQL string-building, (2) keep the result long-format and pivot in the consumer, (3) JSON-object aggregation with stable outer schema and evolving inner keys. The senior signal is recognising that a request for dynamic pivot is usually a request to push presentation logic OUT of SQL.
question:
Using employees(emp_id, name, salary, department), produce a 2-row result with the highest and second-highest salary per department. The catch: the department list isn't known upfront — adding 'Legal' tomorrow must NOT require a query rewrite. Use the schema-stable JSON-aggregation approach (jsonb_object_agg in Postgres / JSON_OBJECTAGG in MySQL 8 / OBJECT_AGG in Snowflake): the outer schema stays at 2 columns (salary_position, by_department), and the inner JSON object's keys evolve with the data.

Then in the enrichment compare with the other two approaches (two-step dynamic SQL, long-format + client pivot) and the production patterns (dbt get_column_values, schema-on-read, event-time dim registry).
solution:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS employees;
CREATE TABLE employees (
    emp_id      INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name        TEXT NOT NULL,
    salary      NUMERIC NOT NULL,
    department  TEXT NOT NULL
);
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
    (1, 'Alex',   220000, 'Engineering'),
    (2, 'Bailey', 195000, 'Engineering'),
    (3, 'Casey',  180000, 'Engineering'),
    (4, 'Drew',   170000, 'Sales'),
    (5, 'Ellis',  150000, 'Sales'),
    (6, 'Finn',   140000, 'Marketing'),
    (7, 'Gray',   130000, 'Marketing'),
    (8, 'Harper', 125000, 'HR'),
    (9, 'Indy',   115000, 'HR');

-- ╭───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  THE JSON-AGGREGATION ANSWER (the only truly schema-stable form)  │
-- │   Outer columns:  salary_position, by_department                  │
-- │   Inner keys:     evolve with the data — no DDL when 'Legal'      │
-- │                   appears tomorrow                                │
-- │   Portable:       Postgres jsonb_object_agg                       │
-- │                   Snowflake OBJECT_AGG                            │
-- │                   MySQL 8 JSON_OBJECTAGG                          │
-- │                   BigQuery TO_JSON_STRING(ARRAY_AGG(...))         │
-- ╰───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
WITH RankedSalaries AS (
    SELECT department, salary,
        DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rnk
    FROM employees
)
SELECT
    CASE WHEN rnk = 1 THEN '1st Highest'
         WHEN rnk = 2 THEN '2nd Highest' END                    AS salary_position,
    jsonb_object_agg(department, salary ORDER BY department)    AS by_department
FROM RankedSalaries
WHERE rnk IN (1, 2)
GROUP BY rnk
ORDER BY rnk;

title: The Second Highest Salary per Department
question: Write a query to return the SECOND highest salary per department. Schema: employee(id, name, dept_id, salary). Return columns: dept_id, second_highest_salary. Use DENSE_RANK so ties at the top still surface a 2nd-place salary.
solution:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT
    dept_id,
    salary,
    DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY dept_id ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rk
  FROM employee
)
SELECT DISTINCT dept_id, salary AS second_highest_salary
FROM ranked
WHERE rk = 2
ORDER BY dept_id;

title: Customers Who Placed More Than 3 Orders in 2025
question: Schema: orders(order_id, customer_id, order_date, amount). Write a query returning customers who placed MORE than 3 orders in 2025. Columns: customer_id, order_count.
solution:
SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS order_count
FROM orders
WHERE order_date >= '2025-01-01' AND order_date < '2026-01-01'
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 3
ORDER BY customer_id;

title: Users Who Logged in on at Least 3 Consecutive Calendar Days at Any Point
question: Schema: login(user_id, login_date). Find users who logged in on AT LEAST 3 consecutive calendar days at any point. Return distinct user_ids, ordered ascending.
solution:
WITH dt AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT user_id, login_date::date AS d FROM login
),
grouped AS (
  SELECT user_id, d,
         d - (ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY d))::integer AS streak_key
  FROM dt
),
streaks AS (
  SELECT user_id, streak_key, COUNT(*) AS streak_len
  FROM grouped
  GROUP BY user_id, streak_key
)
SELECT DISTINCT user_id FROM streaks WHERE streak_len >= 3 ORDER BY user_id;

title: Each Row Plus a 7-day Rolling Sum of Amount Ordered by Sale_date
question: Schema: sales(sale_id, sale_date, amount). Write a query returning each row plus a 7-day rolling sum of amount ordered by sale_date. Columns: sale_date, amount, rolling_7d. (Assume one row per day for simplicity, and use a row-based frame.)
solution:
SELECT
  sale_date,
  amount,
  SUM(amount) OVER (
    ORDER BY sale_date
    ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
  ) AS rolling_7d
FROM sales
ORDER BY sale_date;

title: Every Customer with Their Total Order Amount, Including Customers…
question: Schema: customer(id, name), orders(order_id, customer_id, amount). Return every customer with their total order amount, including customers with zero orders (showing 0, not NULL). Columns: id, name, total.
solution:
SELECT
  c.id,
  c.name,
  COALESCE(SUM(o.amount), 0) AS total
FROM customer c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id
GROUP BY c.id, c.name
ORDER BY c.id;

title: For Each User, Return the Top 2 Most Recent Events
question: Schema: event(user_id, event_ts). For each user, return the top 2 most recent events. Columns: user_id, event_ts.
solution:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    event_ts,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY event_ts DESC) AS rn
  FROM event
)
SELECT user_id, event_ts
FROM ranked
WHERE rn <= 2
ORDER BY user_id, event_ts DESC;

title: In One Query, Return Total Revenue Across All Orders AND the Revenue…
question: Schema: orders(order_id, status, amount). In ONE query, return total revenue across ALL orders and the revenue from orders with status = 'paid'. Columns: total_revenue, paid_revenue.
schema:
CREATE TABLE orders (order_id INTEGER, status TEXT, amount INTEGER);
INSERT INTO orders VALUES (1, 'paid', 100), (2, 'pending', 50), (3, 'paid', 75), (4, 'cancelled', 200), (5, 'paid', 25);
solution:
SELECT
  SUM(amount) AS total_revenue,
  SUM(CASE WHEN status = 'paid' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS paid_revenue
FROM orders;

title: Using a Recursive CTE, Return Each Employee with Their Depth in The…
question: Schema: employee(id, name, manager_id) where manager_id is NULL for the CEO. Using a recursive CTE, return each employee with their depth in the org tree (CEO is depth 0). Columns: id, name, depth.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employee (id INTEGER, name TEXT, manager_id INTEGER);
INSERT INTO employee VALUES (1, 'CEO', NULL), (2, 'VP-A', 1), (3, 'VP-B', 1), (4, 'Mgr-A1', 2), (5, 'Mgr-A2', 2), (6, 'IC-A1a', 4);
solution:
WITH RECURSIVE tree AS (
  SELECT id, name, 0 AS depth
  FROM employee
  WHERE manager_id IS NULL
  UNION ALL
  SELECT e.id, e.name, t.depth + 1
  FROM employee e
  JOIN tree t ON e.manager_id = t.id
)
SELECT id, name, depth
FROM tree
ORDER BY depth, id;

title: Signup Counts per Month for 2025
question: Schema: signups(user_id, signup_date). Return signup counts per month for 2025. Columns: month_start, signup_count. Missing months should still appear with 0 (assume the table has signups across the year).
solution:
SELECT
  to_char(date_trunc('month', signup_date::date), 'YYYY-MM-DD') AS month_start,
  COUNT(*)::int AS signup_count
FROM signups
WHERE signup_date::date >= '2025-01-01' AND signup_date::date < '2026-01-01'
GROUP BY date_trunc('month', signup_date::date)
ORDER BY month_start;

title: Any Email Address That Appears More Than Once (case-insensitive)
question: Schema: users(id, email). Return any email address that appears more than once (case-insensitive). Columns: email_lower, occurrences.
schema:
CREATE TABLE users (id INTEGER, email TEXT);
INSERT INTO users VALUES (1, 'alice@x.com'), (2, 'BOB@y.com'), (3, 'Alice@X.com'), (4, 'carol@z.com'), (5, 'bob@y.com');
solution:
SELECT
  LOWER(email) AS email_lower,
  COUNT(*) AS occurrences
FROM users
GROUP BY LOWER(email)
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
ORDER BY occurrences DESC, email_lower;

title: For Root Part 'Bike', List Every Transitively-required Part with The…
question: Schema: bom(parent_part, child_part, qty) describes a bill-of-materials. For root part 'Bike', list every transitively-required part with the total quantity needed for ONE bike. Columns: part, total_qty, ordered by part.
solution:
WITH RECURSIVE explode AS (
  SELECT child_part AS part, qty AS total_qty
  FROM bom
  WHERE parent_part = 'Bike'
  UNION ALL
  SELECT b.child_part, e.total_qty * b.qty
  FROM explode e
  JOIN bom b ON b.parent_part = e.part
)
SELECT part, SUM(total_qty) AS total_qty
FROM explode
GROUP BY part
ORDER BY part;

title: Generate the Integers 1..10 Using a Recursive CTE
question: Generate the integers 1..10 using a recursive CTE. Return one column n ordered ascending.
schema:
-- No tables needed; pure recursive CTE
CREATE TABLE _dummy(x INTEGER);

title: For User 1, Return All 'friends-of-friends' (people Reachable In…
question: Schema: friendship(a, b) stores undirected friendships as unordered pairs. For user 1, return all 'friends-of-friends' (people reachable in EXACTLY 2 hops) who are not user 1 themselves and not already direct friends of 1. Columns: user_id, ascending.
schema:
CREATE TABLE friendship (a INTEGER, b INTEGER);
INSERT INTO friendship VALUES (1,2),(1,3),(2,4),(2,5),(3,5),(3,6),(4,7),(5,8);
solution:
WITH edges AS (
  SELECT a AS u, b AS v FROM friendship
  UNION ALL
  SELECT b, a FROM friendship
),
direct AS (
  SELECT v FROM edges WHERE u = 1
),
fof AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT e2.v AS user_id
  FROM edges e1
  JOIN edges e2 ON e1.v = e2.u
  WHERE e1.u = 1
)
SELECT user_id FROM fof
WHERE user_id <> 1
  AND user_id NOT IN (SELECT v FROM direct)
ORDER BY user_id;

title: Generate a Contiguous Date Series Covering the Full Range from The…
question: Schema: dates_with_gaps(d) contains a sparse list of dates. Generate a CONTIGUOUS date series covering the full range from the MIN to MAX, with a flag present (1 if the date exists in the table, 0 otherwise). Columns: d, present, ordered by d.
schema:
CREATE TABLE dates_with_gaps (d TEXT);
INSERT INTO dates_with_gaps VALUES ('2025-01-01'),('2025-01-03'),('2025-01-04'),('2025-01-07');
solution:
WITH RECURSIVE spine AS (
  SELECT (SELECT MIN(d::date) FROM dates_with_gaps) AS d,
         (SELECT MAX(d::date) FROM dates_with_gaps) AS max_d
  UNION ALL
  SELECT d + 1, max_d FROM spine WHERE d < max_d
)
SELECT
  to_char(s.d, 'YYYY-MM-DD') AS d,
  CASE WHEN g.d IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS present
FROM spine s
LEFT JOIN dates_with_gaps g ON g.d::date = s.d
ORDER BY s.d;

title: Compute, for Each Day, the Moving Average of V Over the 3-row Window…
question: Schema: daily(d, v). Compute, for each day, the moving average of v over the 3-row window CENTERED on the current row (1 preceding, current, 1 following). For the first and last days the window will only have 2 rows. Columns: d, v, ma, ordered by d.
solution:
SELECT
  d,
  v,
  ROUND(AVG(v) OVER (ORDER BY d ROWS BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND 1 FOLLOWING)::numeric, 2)::float AS ma
FROM daily
ORDER BY d;

title: For Each Game, Return the Player's Cumulative Points Before That…
question: Schema: score(player_id, game_date, points). For each game, return the player's cumulative points BEFORE that game (excluding the current game's points). Columns: player_id, game_date, points, points_before, ordered by player and date.
solution:
SELECT
  player_id,
  game_date,
  points,
  COALESCE(SUM(points) OVER (
    PARTITION BY player_id ORDER BY game_date
    ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING
  ), 0) AS points_before
FROM score
ORDER BY player_id, game_date;

title: Pivot to One Row per Region with Columns Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4 Summing Amounts
question: Schema: sales(region, quarter, amount) has rows like ('NA','Q1',100). Pivot to ONE row per region with columns q1, q2, q3, q4 summing amounts. Use conditional aggregation (portable). Columns: region, q1, q2, q3, q4, ordered by region.
solution:
SELECT
  region,
  SUM(CASE WHEN quarter = 'Q1' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS q1,
  SUM(CASE WHEN quarter = 'Q2' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS q2,
  SUM(CASE WHEN quarter = 'Q3' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS q3,
  SUM(CASE WHEN quarter = 'Q4' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS q4
FROM sales
GROUP BY region
ORDER BY region;

title: Unpivot to Long Form: One Row per (person, Subject, Score)
question: Schema: wide(person, math, english, science) is wide. Unpivot to long form: one row per (person, subject, score). Columns: person, subject, score, ordered by person and subject.
schema:
CREATE TABLE wide (person TEXT, math INTEGER, english INTEGER, science INTEGER);
INSERT INTO wide VALUES ('Alice',90,85,92),('Bob',75,80,70);
solution:
SELECT person, 'english' AS subject, english AS score FROM wide
UNION ALL
SELECT person, 'math', math FROM wide
UNION ALL
SELECT person, 'science', science FROM wide
ORDER BY person, subject;

title: Assign Each Event a Session_id by Starting a New Session Whenever…
question: Schema: event(user_id, ts) where ts is 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS'. Assign each event a session_id by starting a new session whenever the gap from the prior event of the same user is more than 30 minutes. The session_id is just the count of sessions so far for that user (1, 2, 3, ...). Columns: user_id, ts, session_id, ordered by user_id and ts.
solution:
WITH flagged AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    ts,
    CASE
      WHEN LAG(ts) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts) IS NULL THEN 1
      WHEN EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (ts::timestamp - LAG(ts) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts)::timestamp))/60 > 30 THEN 1
      ELSE 0
    END AS is_new_session
  FROM event
)
SELECT
  user_id,
  ts,
  SUM(is_new_session) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)::int AS session_id
FROM flagged
ORDER BY user_id, ts;

title: The Funnel: How Many Distinct Users Reached Each Step, Where…
question: Schema: event(user_id, step, ts) with steps 'view', 'cart', 'purchase'. Compute the funnel: how many DISTINCT users reached each step, where reaching a step also requires reaching all prior steps in chronological order. Output columns: step, users, ordered as 'view','cart','purchase'.
solution:
WITH firsts AS (
  SELECT user_id,
         MIN(CASE WHEN step='view' THEN ts END) AS view_ts,
         MIN(CASE WHEN step='cart' THEN ts END) AS cart_ts,
         MIN(CASE WHEN step='purchase' THEN ts END) AS purchase_ts
  FROM event
  GROUP BY user_id
),
flags AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    (view_ts IS NOT NULL)::int AS reached_view,
    (view_ts IS NOT NULL AND cart_ts IS NOT NULL AND cart_ts >= view_ts)::int AS reached_cart,
    (view_ts IS NOT NULL AND cart_ts IS NOT NULL AND purchase_ts IS NOT NULL
      AND cart_ts >= view_ts AND purchase_ts >= cart_ts)::int AS reached_purchase
  FROM firsts
)
SELECT 'view' AS step, SUM(reached_view) AS users FROM flags
UNION ALL
SELECT 'cart', SUM(reached_cart) FROM flags
UNION ALL
SELECT 'purchase', SUM(reached_purchase) FROM flags;

title: The Z-score of V Against a Trailing 7-day Window (including The…
question: Schema: daily(d, v) has values per day. Compute the z-score of v against a TRAILING 7-day window (including the current row). Columns: d, v, z, ordered by d. Use the population standard deviation (no Bessel correction). Round z to 3 decimals.
solution:
WITH stats AS (
  SELECT
    d,
    v,
    AVG(v)  OVER (ORDER BY d ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS mu,
    AVG(v*v) OVER (ORDER BY d ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)
      - AVG(v) OVER (ORDER BY d ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)
      * AVG(v) OVER (ORDER BY d ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS var_pop
  FROM daily
)
SELECT
  d,
  v,
  CASE WHEN var_pop = 0 THEN NULL
       ELSE ROUND(((v - mu) / sqrt(var_pop))::numeric, 3)::float END AS z
FROM stats
ORDER BY d;

title: Fill in Missing Days from Min(d) to Max(d) with V=0 for Gaps
question: Schema: sparse(d, v). Fill in missing days from min(d) to max(d) with v=0 for gaps. Columns: d, v, ordered by d.
schema:
CREATE TABLE sparse (d TEXT, v INTEGER);
INSERT INTO sparse VALUES ('2025-03-01',5),('2025-03-04',8),('2025-03-05',2);
solution:
WITH RECURSIVE spine(d, max_d) AS (
  SELECT (SELECT MIN(d::date) FROM sparse), (SELECT MAX(d::date) FROM sparse)
  UNION ALL
  SELECT d + 1, max_d FROM spine WHERE d < max_d
)
SELECT to_char(s.d, 'YYYY-MM-DD') AS d, COALESCE(p.v, 0) AS v
FROM spine s
LEFT JOIN sparse p ON p.d::date = s.d
ORDER BY s.d;

title: The Top 2 Most Recent Orders per User (id, Ts), Along with The…
question: Schema: users(id, name) and orders(id, user_id, ts, amount). Return the TOP 2 most recent orders per user (id, ts), along with the user's name. Use a correlated approach or window-then-filter — in SQLite, window + filter. Columns: user_id, name, order_id, ts, amount, ordered by user_id then ts desc.
solution:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT o.*, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts DESC) AS rn
  FROM orders o
)
SELECT r.user_id, u.name, r.id AS order_id, r.ts, r.amount
FROM ranked r
JOIN users u ON u.id = r.user_id
WHERE r.rn <= 2
ORDER BY r.user_id, r.ts DESC;

title: Users Whose First Event Is a 'signup' AND Whose Second Event…
question: Schema: event(id, user_id, event_type, ts). Find users whose FIRST event is a 'signup' AND whose SECOND event happened within 24 hours of the signup. Columns: user_id, ordered ascending.
solution:
WITH ordered AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    event_type,
    ts,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts) AS rn
  FROM event
),
first_two AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    MAX(CASE WHEN rn=1 THEN event_type END) AS first_type,
    MAX(CASE WHEN rn=1 THEN ts END) AS first_ts,
    MAX(CASE WHEN rn=2 THEN ts END) AS second_ts
  FROM ordered
  WHERE rn <= 2
  GROUP BY user_id
)
SELECT user_id
FROM first_two
WHERE first_type = 'signup'
  AND second_ts IS NOT NULL
  AND EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (second_ts::timestamp - first_ts::timestamp))/3600 <= 24
ORDER BY user_id;

title: Students Whose Max Exam Score Is at Least 20 Points Higher Than…
question: Schema: score(student, exam, points). Find students whose MAX exam score is at least 20 points higher than their MIN exam score (high variance). Columns: student, min_pts, max_pts, spread, ordered by spread desc.
solution:
SELECT
  student,
  MIN(points) AS min_pts,
  MAX(points) AS max_pts,
  MAX(points) - MIN(points) AS spread
FROM score
GROUP BY student
HAVING MAX(points) - MIN(points) >= 20
ORDER BY spread DESC;

title: Your Colleague Has Written This Query to Report 'logins per User'…
question:
Schema: users(id, name) and logins(user_id, login_ts) (one user may have many login rows). Your colleague has written this query to report 'logins per user' but the numbers look strange compared to a separate count.

SELECT u.id, u.name,
       COUNT(*)              AS logins,
       COUNT(DISTINCT s.id)  AS sessions
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN logins   l ON u.id = l.user_id
LEFT JOIN sessions s ON u.id = s.user_id
GROUP BY u.id, u.name;

Run it once — for user 1 (who has 2 logins and 3 sessions), you'll see logins = 6, not 2. Explain (in your head) the row-multiplication, then write the CORRECT query that returns id, name, logins, sessions with the genuine counts (no double-counting from the join). Order by id.
solution:
SELECT u.id, u.name,
       COUNT(DISTINCT l.id) AS logins,
       COUNT(DISTINCT s.id) AS sessions
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN logins   l ON u.id = l.user_id
LEFT JOIN sessions s ON u.id = s.user_id
GROUP BY u.id, u.name
ORDER BY u.id;

title: Predict the Output of the Query Below, Then Click Run to Verify
question:
Schema: users(id, country, opt_in) where opt_in is one of 'yes', 'no', or NULL (never answered). Predict the output of the query below, then click Run to verify. Hint: think carefully about WHERE evaluating NULL.

SELECT country, COUNT(*) AS c
FROM users
WHERE opt_in != 'yes'
GROUP BY country
ORDER BY country;

Your task: rewrite the query so the result INCLUDES users who never answered (opt_in IS NULL) alongside those who said 'no'. Same output columns country, c ordered by country.
solution:
SELECT country, COUNT(*) AS c
FROM users
WHERE opt_in IS DISTINCT FROM 'yes'
GROUP BY country
ORDER BY country;

title: A Naive Query Select Sum(o.amt) from Orders O Left Join Refunds R On…
question:
Schema: orders(id, amt) and refunds(order_id, refund_amt) (one order may have MANY refunds). A naive query SELECT SUM(o.amt) FROM orders o LEFT JOIN refunds r ON o.id = r.order_id inflates the total because order rows are duplicated by the join. Run it once to confirm the inflated number, then write the CORRECT query that returns:

- total_orders — the genuine sum of orders.amt
- total_refunds — the genuine sum of refunds.refund_amt

BOTH columns in ONE row, with NO double-counting.

title: Multiple Rows Can Share the Same Day
question:
Schema: sales(d TEXT, amt INT) where d is a date string. Multiple rows can share the same day. The query SELECT d, amt, SUM(amt) OVER (ORDER BY d) FROM sales gives a SURPRISING result because the default window frame is RANGE — tied rows on the same d are all assigned the same running total (the lump).

Write the CORRECT query that produces a strict, deterministic per-row running total. Return columns d, amt, running_total ordered by (d, amt).
solution:
SELECT d, amt,
       SUM(amt) OVER (ORDER BY d, amt ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS running_total
FROM sales
ORDER BY d, amt;

title: Emulate an ASOF Join in Postgres Without Using Ignore Nulls (so This…
question:
Emulate an ASOF JOIN in Postgres without using IGNORE NULLS (so this technique works in any SQL with window functions — Postgres < 14, older BigQuery, MySQL 8). Schema:

- quotes(ts, symbol, bid) — bid updates per symbol
- trades(ts, symbol, qty) — trades per symbol

For each trade, return the most recent bid AT-OR-BEFORE the trade time, per symbol. Use the window-function-fill pattern with the gap-fill trick. Return columns: trade_ts, symbol, qty, active_bid. Order by symbol, trade_ts.

Hint: interleave both tables with UNION ALL, then use COUNT(bid) OVER (... ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) to define fill-forward groups, then MAX(bid) OVER (PARTITION BY symbol, grp). Compute the MAX BEFORE filtering to trade rows — otherwise WHERE strips the quote rows first and MAX has no bids to find.
solution:
WITH timeline AS (
  SELECT ts, symbol, CAST(NULL AS REAL) AS qty, bid FROM quotes
  UNION ALL
  SELECT ts, symbol, qty, CAST(NULL AS INTEGER) AS bid FROM trades
),
fill AS (
  SELECT ts, symbol, qty, bid,
         COUNT(bid) OVER (
           PARTITION BY symbol ORDER BY ts
           ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
         ) AS grp
  FROM timeline
),
with_active AS (
  SELECT ts, symbol, qty,
         MAX(bid) OVER (PARTITION BY symbol, grp) AS active_bid
  FROM fill
)
SELECT ts AS trade_ts, symbol, qty, active_bid
FROM with_active
WHERE qty IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY symbol, trade_ts;

title: Schema Preloaded: Price_logs(event_time, Price) with Two Rows…
question:
Schema preloaded: price_logs(event_time, price) with two rows sharing 12:05:00. Write a SELECT that — for each row — returns the SUM and the STRING_AGG formula (e.g. '10 + 20') for BOTH ROWS BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW and GROUPS BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW, so you can SEE exactly which prices each frame is adding.

Return columns: event_time, price, rows_sum, rows_formula, groups_sum, groups_formula ordered by event_time, price. Cast price to text for STRING_AGG (Postgres requires explicit types).
solution:
SELECT event_time, price,
  SUM(price)                        OVER (ORDER BY event_time ROWS   BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS rows_sum,
  STRING_AGG(price::text, ' + ')    OVER (ORDER BY event_time ROWS   BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS rows_formula,
  SUM(price)                        OVER (ORDER BY event_time GROUPS BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS groups_sum,
  STRING_AGG(price::text, ' + ')    OVER (ORDER BY event_time GROUPS BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS groups_formula
FROM price_logs
ORDER BY event_time, price;

title: For Each Customer, Return the First Product They Purchased in Each…
question: Schema: sales(customer_id, order_date, product_id, qty) and menu(product_id, name, category, price). For each customer, return the FIRST product they purchased in EACH category, where 'first' is by order_date ascending; ties on date are broken by lower price, then by product_id ascending. Output columns: customer_id, category, product_name, order_date, ordered by customer_id, category. A customer who never bought in a category should produce NO row for that category.
solution:
SELECT customer_id, category, name AS product_name, order_date
FROM (
  SELECT s.customer_id, m.category, m.name, s.order_date,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY s.customer_id, m.category
                            ORDER BY s.order_date, m.price, m.product_id) AS rn
  FROM sales s JOIN menu m USING (product_id)
) t
WHERE rn = 1
ORDER BY customer_id, category;

title: Loyalty Points per Customer for January 2025 Using These Rules: (a)…
question: Schema: members(customer_id, join_date) and purchases(customer_id, order_date, product, amount). Compute loyalty points per customer for January 2025 using these rules: (a) every dollar = 10 points base; (b) within the FIRST 7 DAYS after join_date (inclusive of join_date itself), every dollar = 20 points (1st-week bonus); (c) on any 'sushi' purchase OUTSIDE the first-week bonus, every dollar = 20 points. Non-members contribute 10 points/dollar with no bonuses. Return columns customer_id, points, ordered by customer_id. Only consider purchases in January 2025.
schema:
CREATE TABLE members (customer_id TEXT, join_date DATE);
INSERT INTO members VALUES ('A','2021-01-07'),('B','2021-01-09');
CREATE TABLE purchases (customer_id TEXT, order_date DATE, product TEXT, amount INTEGER);
INSERT INTO purchases VALUES
  ('A','2021-01-01','sushi',10),
  ('A','2021-01-01','curry',15),
  ('A','2021-01-07','curry',15),
  ('A','2021-01-10','ramen',12),
  ('A','2021-01-11','ramen',12),
  ('A','2021-01-11','ramen',12),
  ('B','2021-01-01','curry',15),
  ('B','2021-01-02','curry',15),
  ('B','2021-01-04','sushi',10),
  ('B','2021-01-11','sushi',10),
  ('B','2021-01-16','ramen',12),
  ('C','2021-01-01','ramen',12),
  ('C','2021-01-07','ramen',12);
solution:
SELECT p.customer_id,
       SUM(
         CASE
           WHEN m.join_date IS NOT NULL
                AND p.order_date BETWEEN m.join_date AND m.join_date + 6
             THEN p.amount * 20
           WHEN p.product = 'sushi' THEN p.amount * 20
           ELSE p.amount * 10
         END
       ) AS points
FROM purchases p
LEFT JOIN members m ON m.customer_id = p.customer_id
WHERE p.order_date >= DATE '2021-01-01'
  AND p.order_date <  DATE '2021-02-01'
GROUP BY p.customer_id
ORDER BY p.customer_id;

title: For Each Weekly Cohort (week Start = Monday of the Signup Date),…
question: Schema: users(user_id, signup_date) and activity(user_id, active_date). For each WEEKLY cohort (week start = Monday of the signup date), report retention by week offset 0..3, where week offset N means 'was the user active during the 7-day window starting cohort_week_start + 7*N'. Return columns cohort_week, week_offset, retained_users. Ordering: cohort_week ASC, week_offset ASC. Include offset rows only if at least one user was retained (skip 0-counts).
solution:
WITH cohorts AS (
  SELECT user_id,
         date_trunc('week', signup_date)::date AS cohort_week
  FROM users
),
joined AS (
  SELECT c.cohort_week,
         c.user_id,
         FLOOR((a.active_date - c.cohort_week)::numeric / 7)::int AS week_offset
  FROM cohorts c
  JOIN activity a USING (user_id)
)
SELECT cohort_week,
       week_offset,
       COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS retained_users
FROM joined
WHERE week_offset BETWEEN 0 AND 3
GROUP BY cohort_week, week_offset
ORDER BY cohort_week, week_offset;

title: Customers Who Have Ordered at Least One of Every Pizza in the Menu
question: Schema: pizzas(pizza_id, name) and orders(order_id, customer_id, pizza_id). Find customers who have ordered AT LEAST ONE of EVERY pizza in the menu. Return customer_id, ordered ascending. Edge case: if a customer ordered the same pizza multiple times, that counts as one. If the menu is empty (no pizzas), every customer trivially qualifies.
solution:
SELECT customer_id
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT pizza_id) = (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM pizzas)
ORDER BY customer_id;

title: Customers Who Have Purchased at Least One Product from the Set…
question: Schema: purchases(customer_id, product). Return customers who have purchased AT LEAST ONE product from the set {'A','B'} but NEVER purchased any product from {'X','Y','Z'}. Return customer_id, ordered ascending.
schema:
CREATE TABLE purchases (customer_id INTEGER, product TEXT);
INSERT INTO purchases VALUES
  (1,'A'),(1,'B'),(1,'C'),
  (2,'A'),(2,'X'),
  (3,'B'),(3,'C'),
  (4,'B'),(4,'D'),(4,'E'),
  (5,'C'),(5,'D'),
  (6,'A'),(6,'Y'),
  (7,'B');
solution:
SELECT DISTINCT customer_id
FROM purchases
WHERE product IN ('A','B')
  AND customer_id NOT IN (
        SELECT customer_id FROM purchases WHERE product IN ('X','Y','Z')
      )
ORDER BY customer_id;

title: The Running Balance per Account Ordered by Ts
question: Schema: txn(id, account, ts, kind, amount) where kind is 'deposit' (positive contribution), 'withdrawal' (negative), or 'refund' (positive — credit applied to the account). Compute the running balance per account ordered by ts. Return id, account, ts, kind, signed_amount, running_balance, ordered by account, ts, id. signed_amount is the per-row delta (negative for withdrawals); running_balance is the cumulative.
solution:
SELECT id, account, ts, kind,
       CASE WHEN kind = 'withdrawal' THEN -amount ELSE amount END AS signed_amount,
       SUM(CASE WHEN kind = 'withdrawal' THEN -amount ELSE amount END)
         OVER (PARTITION BY account ORDER BY ts, id
               ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS running_balance
FROM txn
ORDER BY account, ts, id;

title: For Each (ts, Sensor_id) Row, Return the Last Known Non-NULL Value…
question: Schema: sensor_readings(ts, sensor_id, value) where value may be NULL for sensor downtime. For each (ts, sensor_id) row, return the LAST KNOWN non-NULL value (LOCF, last observation carried forward) for that sensor. If no prior non-NULL exists, return NULL. Implement WITHOUT using IGNORE NULLS. Return ts, sensor_id, value, filled ordered by sensor_id, ts.
solution:
WITH grouped AS (
  SELECT ts, sensor_id, value,
         COUNT(value) OVER (PARTITION BY sensor_id ORDER BY ts
                            ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS grp
  FROM sensor_readings
)
SELECT ts, sensor_id, value,
       MAX(value) OVER (PARTITION BY sensor_id, grp) AS filled
FROM grouped
ORDER BY sensor_id, ts;

title: For Each Category, Return the Top 3 Products by Sales Desc, But…
question: Schema: products(category, name, sales). For each category, return the TOP 3 products by sales DESC, but INCLUDING TIES at the boundary — so if two products tie for 3rd place, both are returned (4 rows for that category). Return category, name, sales, ordered by category, sales DESC, name ASC.
solution:
SELECT category, name, sales
FROM (
  SELECT category, name, sales,
         DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY sales DESC) AS rk
  FROM products
) t
WHERE rk <= 3
ORDER BY category, sales DESC, name;

title: The Total Number of Days Each Customer Spent in Each Plan Up to A…
question: Schema: subs(customer_id, start_date, plan) is an append-only log of subscription state changes (one row per change). Compute the total number of DAYS each customer spent in each plan up to a reporting cutoff of 2025-12-31. End of a plan period = start_date of the customer's next row (exclusive), or the cutoff for the last row. Return customer_id, plan, days, ordered by customer_id, plan.
schema:
CREATE TABLE subs (customer_id INTEGER, start_date DATE, plan TEXT);
INSERT INTO subs VALUES
  (1,'2025-01-01','trial'),
  (1,'2025-01-08','basic'),
  (1,'2025-02-15','pro'),
  (1,'2025-12-01','churn'),
  (2,'2025-03-10','trial'),
  (2,'2025-03-17','annual');
solution:
WITH intervals AS (
  SELECT customer_id, plan, start_date,
         COALESCE(
           LEAD(start_date) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY start_date),
           DATE '2026-01-01'
         ) AS end_exclusive
  FROM subs
)
SELECT customer_id, plan,
       SUM((LEAST(end_exclusive, DATE '2026-01-01') - start_date))::int AS days
FROM intervals
WHERE start_date < DATE '2026-01-01'
GROUP BY customer_id, plan
ORDER BY customer_id, plan;

title: For Each Employee, Return the Path from Ceo Down to Them, as A…
question: Schema: employee(id, name, manager_id) where the CEO has manager_id = NULL. For each employee, return the path from CEO down to them, as a >-separated string of names, plus the depth (CEO is 0). Return id, name, depth, path, ordered by depth then id.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employee (id INTEGER, name TEXT, manager_id INTEGER);
INSERT INTO employee VALUES
  (1,'Alice',NULL),
  (2,'Bob',1),(3,'Cara',1),
  (4,'Dan',2),(5,'Eve',2),
  (6,'Finn',3),
  (7,'Gwen',4);
solution:
WITH RECURSIVE chain AS (
  SELECT id, name, 0 AS depth, name AS path
  FROM employee
  WHERE manager_id IS NULL
  UNION ALL
  SELECT e.id, e.name, c.depth + 1, c.path || ' > ' || e.name
  FROM employee e
  JOIN chain c ON e.manager_id = c.id
)
SELECT id, name, depth, path
FROM chain
ORDER BY depth, id;

title: The Median Value per Category Without Using PERCENTILE_CONT,…
question: Schema: score(category, value). Compute the MEDIAN value per category WITHOUT using PERCENTILE_CONT, PERCENTILE_DISC, or any percentile/quantile function. Use ROW_NUMBER + COUNT and average the middle row(s). Median definition: for odd N return the middle value; for even N return the average of the two middle values. Return category, median, ordered by category. Round to 2 decimals.
solution:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT category, value,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY value) AS rn,
         COUNT(*)    OVER (PARTITION BY category)                  AS n
  FROM score
)
SELECT category,
       ROUND(AVG(value)::numeric, 2) AS median
FROM ranked
WHERE rn IN ((n + 1) / 2, (n + 2) / 2)   -- integer arithmetic handles odd & even
GROUP BY category
ORDER BY category;

title: Compress Consecutive Identical Statuses per Entity Into Stints
question: Schema: status_log(entity_id, ts, status). Compress consecutive identical statuses per entity into stints. For each (entity_id, run of same status), return the entity, status, stint_start (first ts), stint_end (last ts), and row_count. Return entity_id, stint_start, stint_end, status, row_count, ordered by entity_id then stint_start.
solution:
WITH flagged AS (
  SELECT entity_id, ts, status,
         LAG(status) OVER (PARTITION BY entity_id ORDER BY ts) AS prev_status
  FROM status_log
),
changes AS (
  SELECT entity_id, ts, status,
         SUM(CASE WHEN prev_status IS DISTINCT FROM status THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)
           OVER (PARTITION BY entity_id ORDER BY ts
                 ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS grp
  FROM flagged
)
SELECT entity_id,
       MIN(ts) AS stint_start,
       MAX(ts) AS stint_end,
       MIN(status) AS status,
       COUNT(*) AS row_count
FROM changes
GROUP BY entity_id, grp
ORDER BY entity_id, stint_start;

title: An Order May Have 0..N Payments AND 0..N Refunds; the Same Order Can…
question: Schema: three tables — orders(order_id, customer_id, amount), payments(order_id, paid_amount), refunds(order_id, refund_amount). An order may have 0..N payments and 0..N refunds; the same order can have multiple of each row. Compute per-order: order_amount, total_paid, total_refunded, net (paid − refunded). Return EVERY order (even with no payments or refunds), with NULLs replaced by 0. Flag status: 'overpaid' if total_paid > order_amount, 'underpaid' if total_paid < order_amount, 'paid' if equal AND refunds = 0, 'refunded' if equal AND refunds > 0. Columns: order_id, order_amount, total_paid, total_refunded, net, status, ordered by order_id.
solution:
WITH p AS (
  SELECT order_id, COALESCE(SUM(paid_amount),0) AS total_paid
  FROM payments GROUP BY order_id
),
r AS (
  SELECT order_id, COALESCE(SUM(refund_amount),0) AS total_refunded
  FROM refunds  GROUP BY order_id
)
SELECT o.order_id,
       o.amount AS order_amount,
       COALESCE(p.total_paid, 0)      AS total_paid,
       COALESCE(r.total_refunded, 0)  AS total_refunded,
       COALESCE(p.total_paid, 0) - COALESCE(r.total_refunded, 0) AS net,
       CASE
         WHEN COALESCE(p.total_paid,0) > o.amount THEN 'overpaid'
         WHEN COALESCE(p.total_paid,0) < o.amount THEN 'underpaid'
         WHEN COALESCE(r.total_refunded,0) > 0    THEN 'refunded'
         ELSE 'paid'
       END AS status
FROM orders o
LEFT JOIN p USING (order_id)
LEFT JOIN r USING (order_id)
ORDER BY o.order_id;

title: For Each (customer, Category) Row, Compute the Rolling Average Of…
question: Schema: txn(customer_id, category, ts, amount). For each (customer, category) row, compute the rolling average of the customer's LAST 3 transactions IN THAT CATEGORY (including the current row). If fewer than 3 prior rows exist, average over what's available. Return customer_id, category, ts, amount, rolling_avg_3, rounded to 2 decimals, ordered by customer_id, category, ts.
solution:
SELECT customer_id, category, ts, amount,
       ROUND(AVG(amount) OVER (
         PARTITION BY customer_id, category
         ORDER BY ts
         ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
       )::numeric, 2) AS rolling_avg_3
FROM txn
ORDER BY customer_id, category, ts;

title: Users Who Completed the Funnel in Order: First Visit, Then…
question: Schema: event(user_id, event, ts) with possible events 'visit', 'add_to_cart', 'purchase'. Find users who completed the funnel IN ORDER: first visit, then add_to_cart, then purchase, where each step's earliest occurrence is strictly later than the prior step's earliest occurrence. Return user_id, ordered ascending.
solution:
WITH firsts AS (
  SELECT user_id,
         MIN(ts) FILTER (WHERE event = 'visit')       AS first_visit,
         MIN(ts) FILTER (WHERE event = 'add_to_cart') AS first_cart,
         MIN(ts) FILTER (WHERE event = 'purchase')    AS first_purchase
  FROM event GROUP BY user_id
)
SELECT user_id FROM firsts
WHERE first_visit IS NOT NULL
  AND first_cart IS NOT NULL
  AND first_purchase IS NOT NULL
  AND first_visit < first_cart
  AND first_cart  < first_purchase
ORDER BY user_id;

title: For Each (warehouse, Sku, Ts) Row, Compute the Running On-hand…
question: Schema: inventory_event(warehouse_id, sku, ts, delta) where delta is positive for receipts and negative for shipments. For each (warehouse, sku, ts) row, compute the running on-hand quantity after that event. Return warehouse_id, sku, ts, delta, on_hand, ordered by warehouse_id, sku, ts.
solution:
SELECT warehouse_id, sku, ts, delta,
       SUM(delta) OVER (PARTITION BY warehouse_id, sku ORDER BY ts
                        ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS on_hand
FROM inventory_event
ORDER BY warehouse_id, sku, ts;

title: For Each (customer, Category), Return the Second-most-recent Order — I.e
question: Schema: orders(order_id, customer_id, category, ts, amount). For each (customer, category), return the SECOND-most-recent order — i.e. the order with the second-largest ts in that group. If a customer has only one order in a category, that category should NOT appear for that customer. Return customer_id, category, order_id, ts, amount, ordered by customer_id, category.
solution:
SELECT customer_id, category, order_id, ts, amount
FROM (
  SELECT customer_id, category, order_id, ts, amount,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id, category ORDER BY ts DESC) AS rn
  FROM orders
) t
WHERE rn = 2
ORDER BY customer_id, category;

title: Count How Many Customers Transitioned from 'trial' to Each…
question: Schema: subs(customer_id, start_date, plan) is an append-only log of plan changes. Count how many customers transitioned from 'trial' to each subsequent plan (including 'churn' or never-converted). Output one row per next_plan value (including the row 'never_converted' for customers whose trial had no next plan). Return next_plan, customer_count, ordered by customer_count DESC then next_plan ASC.
solution:
WITH journeys AS (
  SELECT customer_id, plan,
         LEAD(plan) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY start_date) AS next_plan
  FROM subs
)
SELECT COALESCE(next_plan, 'never_converted') AS next_plan,
       COUNT(*) AS customer_count
FROM journeys
WHERE plan = 'trial'
GROUP BY COALESCE(next_plan, 'never_converted')
ORDER BY customer_count DESC, next_plan;

title: Each Order May Contain Several Pizza Rows (one per Pizza)
question: Schema: customer_orders(order_id, pizza_id, order_time) and runner_orders(order_id, pickup_time, cancellation). Each order may contain several pizza rows (one per pizza). For each order, compute its size (count of pizza rows) and preparation time in minutes = pickup_time − MIN(order_time) for that order. Then, for each distinct pizza-count, report the AVERAGE preparation time rounded to 2 decimals. Exclude cancellations (cancellation is non-NULL and non-empty). Return pizzas_in_order, avg_prep_minutes, ordered by pizzas_in_order.
solution:
WITH per_order AS (
  SELECT co.order_id,
         COUNT(*) AS pizzas_in_order,
         EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (ro.pickup_time - MIN(co.order_time)))/60.0 AS prep_minutes
  FROM customer_orders co
  JOIN runner_orders ro USING (order_id)
  WHERE ro.cancellation IS NULL OR ro.cancellation = ''
  GROUP BY co.order_id, ro.pickup_time
)
SELECT pizzas_in_order,
       ROUND(AVG(prep_minutes)::numeric, 2) AS avg_prep_minutes
FROM per_order
GROUP BY pizzas_in_order
ORDER BY pizzas_in_order;

title: For User 1, Return All Users Reachable Via Shortest Path of Length 2…
question: Schema: friendship(a, b) of UNDIRECTED edges (each pair stored once). For user 1, return all users reachable via SHORTEST PATH of length 2 (friends-of-friends only), excluding user 1 themselves and excluding any direct friends. Return user_id, mutual_count where mutual_count is the number of distinct user-1 friends that connect to that user_id. Order by mutual_count DESC, user_id ASC.
schema:
CREATE TABLE friendship (a INTEGER, b INTEGER);
INSERT INTO friendship VALUES (1,2),(1,3),(1,4),(2,5),(2,6),(3,5),(3,7),(4,5),(4,8),(5,9);
solution:
WITH edges AS (
  SELECT a AS u, b AS v FROM friendship
  UNION ALL
  SELECT b, a FROM friendship
),
direct AS (
  SELECT v FROM edges WHERE u = 1
)
SELECT e2.v AS user_id, COUNT(DISTINCT e1.v) AS mutual_count
FROM edges e1
JOIN edges e2 ON e1.v = e2.u
WHERE e1.u = 1
  AND e2.v <> 1
  AND e2.v NOT IN (SELECT v FROM direct)
GROUP BY e2.v
ORDER BY mutual_count DESC, user_id;

title: Users Who Were Enrolled in Both Experiment 'feed_v2' AND Experiment…
question: Schema: experiment_users(experiment, user_id, variant). Find users who were enrolled in BOTH experiment 'feed_v2' AND experiment 'pricing_v3', and report the (variant_feed, variant_pricing) crosstab counts. Return variant_feed, variant_pricing, users, ordered by variant_feed, variant_pricing.
solution:
SELECT f.variant AS variant_feed,
       p.variant AS variant_pricing,
       COUNT(*) AS users
FROM experiment_users f
JOIN experiment_users p
  ON f.user_id = p.user_id
 AND f.experiment = 'feed_v2'
 AND p.experiment = 'pricing_v3'
GROUP BY f.variant, p.variant
ORDER BY variant_feed, variant_pricing;

title: For Each Customer, Compute Their Percentile Position by LTV…
question: Schema: customer(id, lifetime_value). For each customer, compute their PERCENTILE position by LTV (fraction of customers with LTV less than or equal to theirs). Return id, lifetime_value, percentile, where percentile is rounded to 3 decimals. Order by lifetime_value DESC, id.
solution:
SELECT id, lifetime_value,
       ROUND(CUME_DIST() OVER (ORDER BY lifetime_value)::numeric, 3) AS percentile
FROM customer
ORDER BY lifetime_value DESC, id;

title: Customers Whose Orders Span at Least 3 Distinct Weekdays (e.g
question: Schema: orders(customer_id, order_date). Return customers whose orders span AT LEAST 3 DISTINCT WEEKDAYS (e.g. Monday and Tuesday count as 2 distinct weekdays even if multiple orders happened on each). Use ISO weekday numbering (1=Monday..7=Sunday). Return customer_id, weekdays_covered, ordered by weekdays_covered DESC, customer_id ASC.
solution:
SELECT customer_id,
       COUNT(DISTINCT EXTRACT(ISODOW FROM order_date)) AS weekdays_covered
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT EXTRACT(ISODOW FROM order_date)) >= 3
ORDER BY weekdays_covered DESC, customer_id;

title: Aggregate Revenue by Fiscal Quarter, Where the Fiscal Year Starts On…
question: Schema: revenue(order_date, amount). Aggregate revenue by FISCAL QUARTER, where the fiscal year STARTS ON APRIL 1 (common in UK/India/Japan). FQ1 = Apr–Jun, FQ2 = Jul–Sep, FQ3 = Oct–Dec, FQ4 = Jan–Mar of the next calendar year. Return fiscal_year, fiscal_quarter, total, ordered by fiscal_year, fiscal_quarter. fiscal_year is the calendar year in which the fiscal year begins (e.g. FY2025 runs Apr 2025 – Mar 2026).
solution:
SELECT
  CASE WHEN EXTRACT(MONTH FROM order_date) >= 4
       THEN EXTRACT(YEAR FROM order_date)::int
       ELSE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM order_date)::int - 1
  END AS fiscal_year,
  ((EXTRACT(MONTH FROM order_date)::int - 4 + 12) % 12) / 3 + 1 AS fiscal_quarter,
  SUM(amount) AS total
FROM revenue
GROUP BY fiscal_year, fiscal_quarter
ORDER BY fiscal_year, fiscal_quarter;

title: As of the Reference Date '2025-05-14', Return Customers Whose…
question: Schema: customer(id, name, signup_date). As of the reference date '2025-05-14', return customers whose 5-year, 10-year, or 25-year anniversary falls between today and the end of the next 30 days (inclusive, calendar-aware). Return id, name, milestone_years, anniversary_date, ordered by anniversary_date, id. If a Feb-29 signup hits a non-leap year, treat the anniversary as Feb 28.
schema:
CREATE TABLE customer (id INTEGER, name TEXT, signup_date DATE);
INSERT INTO customer VALUES
  (1,'Alice','2020-05-20'),
  (2,'Bob','2015-06-01'),
