  (3,'Cara','2000-06-10'),
  (4,'Dan','2020-02-29'),
  (5,'Eve','2019-05-14'),
  (6,'Finn','2010-04-01'),
  (7,'Gwen','2020-06-15');
solution:
WITH base AS (
  SELECT id, name, signup_date, m AS milestone_years
  FROM customer, (VALUES (5),(10),(25)) AS milestones(m)
),
anniversaries AS (
  SELECT id, name, milestone_years,
         (signup_date + (milestone_years * INTERVAL '1 year'))::date AS anniversary_date
  FROM base
)
SELECT id, name, milestone_years, anniversary_date
FROM anniversaries
WHERE anniversary_date BETWEEN DATE '2025-05-14' AND DATE '2025-05-14' + 30
ORDER BY anniversary_date, id;

title: The Average Number of Orders per Customer, Including Customers Who…
question: Schema: orders(order_id, customer_id). Compute the AVERAGE number of orders per customer, INCLUDING customers who have zero orders (use customer table). Return avg_orders_per_customer rounded to 2 decimals as a single-row, single-column result.
solution:
SELECT ROUND(AVG(order_count)::numeric, 2) AS avg_orders_per_customer
FROM (
  SELECT c.id, COUNT(o.order_id) AS order_count
  FROM customer c
  LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id
  GROUP BY c.id
) t;

title: For Each Product, Compute Its Share of Revenue Within Its Segment…
question: Schema: sales(product_id, segment, category, revenue). For each product, compute its share of revenue WITHIN its segment and ALSO within its category. Return product_id, segment, category, revenue, pct_of_segment, pct_of_category. Percentages are 0-100 rounded to 2 decimals. Order by category, segment, product_id.
solution:
SELECT product_id, segment, category, revenue,
       ROUND(100.0 * revenue / SUM(revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY segment)::numeric, 2) AS pct_of_segment,
       ROUND(100.0 * revenue / SUM(revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY category)::numeric, 2) AS pct_of_category
FROM sales
ORDER BY category, segment, product_id;

title: The Most Common Unordered 2-product Combination Across All Orders…
question: Schema: order_items(order_id, product_id). Find the MOST COMMON unordered 2-product combination across all orders (orders containing exactly 2 distinct products — or both products at least, regardless of size). The combination is the unordered pair {p1, p2} with p1 < p2. Return p1, p2, occurrences, ordered by occurrences DESC, p1, p2 — LIMIT to top 3 rows.
schema:
CREATE TABLE order_items (order_id INTEGER, product_id INTEGER);
INSERT INTO order_items VALUES
  (1,1),(1,2),
  (2,1),(2,2),(2,3),
  (3,2),(3,3),
  (4,1),(4,2),
  (5,1),(5,3),
  (6,2),(6,3),(6,4),
  (7,1),(7,4);
solution:
SELECT a.product_id AS p1, b.product_id AS p2, COUNT(*) AS occurrences
FROM order_items a
JOIN order_items b
  ON a.order_id = b.order_id
 AND a.product_id < b.product_id
GROUP BY a.product_id, b.product_id
ORDER BY occurrences DESC, p1, p2
LIMIT 3;

title: Pivot to a Quarterly Summary for 2025: One Row per Customer, Columns…
question: Schema: sales(customer_id, sale_date, amount). Pivot to a quarterly summary for 2025: one row per customer, columns q1, q2, q3, q4 summing the customer's sales in each calendar quarter. Customers with no sales in a quarter get 0 (not NULL). Skip customers with no sales in the year. Return customer_id, q1, q2, q3, q4, ordered by customer_id.
solution:
SELECT customer_id,
       COALESCE(SUM(amount) FILTER (WHERE EXTRACT(QUARTER FROM sale_date) = 1), 0) AS q1,
       COALESCE(SUM(amount) FILTER (WHERE EXTRACT(QUARTER FROM sale_date) = 2), 0) AS q2,
       COALESCE(SUM(amount) FILTER (WHERE EXTRACT(QUARTER FROM sale_date) = 3), 0) AS q3,
       COALESCE(SUM(amount) FILTER (WHERE EXTRACT(QUARTER FROM sale_date) = 4), 0) AS q4
FROM sales
WHERE sale_date >= DATE '2025-01-01' AND sale_date < DATE '2026-01-01'
GROUP BY customer_id
ORDER BY customer_id;

title: For Each Department (including All Ancestors), Compute the Sum Of…
question: Schema: dept(id, name, parent_id) and employee(id, dept_id, salary). For each department (including ALL ancestors), compute the SUM of employee salaries in that dept OR any descendant dept. Return dept_id, dept_name, total_salary, ordered by dept_id. Departments with no employees in their subtree get 0.
solution:
WITH RECURSIVE subtree AS (
  SELECT id AS root_id, id AS dept_id FROM dept
  UNION ALL
  SELECT s.root_id, d.id
  FROM dept d JOIN subtree s ON d.parent_id = s.dept_id
)
SELECT d.id AS dept_id,
       d.name AS dept_name,
       COALESCE(SUM(e.salary), 0) AS total_salary
FROM dept d
LEFT JOIN subtree s ON s.root_id = d.id
LEFT JOIN employee e ON e.dept_id = s.dept_id
GROUP BY d.id, d.name
ORDER BY d.id;

title: Month_start, Region, Revenue, Prior_month_revenue, Mom_growth_pct…
question: Schema: monthly_sales(month_start, region, revenue) (one row per region per first-of-month). Return month_start, region, revenue, prior_month_revenue, mom_growth_pct (this/prev - 1 in percent, 2 decimals). Ordering: region, month_start. If a month has no prior month for that region (gap or first observation), prior_month_revenue and mom_growth_pct should be NULL.
schema:
CREATE TABLE monthly_sales (month_start DATE, region TEXT, revenue NUMERIC);
INSERT INTO monthly_sales VALUES
  ('2025-01-01','NA',1000),
  ('2025-02-01','NA',1100),
  ('2025-03-01','NA',1320),
  ('2025-05-01','NA',1500),  -- April missing for NA
  ('2025-01-01','EU',800),
  ('2025-02-01','EU',900);
solution:
SELECT month_start, region, revenue,
       LAG(revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY region ORDER BY month_start) AS prior_month_revenue,
       CASE
         WHEN LAG(month_start) OVER (PARTITION BY region ORDER BY month_start)
              = (month_start - INTERVAL '1 month')::date
         THEN ROUND((revenue / LAG(revenue) OVER (PARTITION BY region ORDER BY month_start) - 1) * 100, 2)
         ELSE NULL
       END AS mom_growth_pct
FROM monthly_sales
ORDER BY region, month_start;

title: Customers Who Placed at Least One Order in 3 OR More Consecutive…
question: Schema: orders(order_id, customer_id, order_date). Find customers who placed AT LEAST one order in 3 OR MORE CONSECUTIVE calendar months. Return customer_id, ordered ascending.
schema:
CREATE TABLE orders (order_id INTEGER, customer_id INTEGER, order_date DATE);
INSERT INTO orders VALUES
  -- customer 1: Jan, Feb, Mar (streak of 3) → qualifies
  (1,1,'2025-01-05'),(2,1,'2025-02-15'),(3,1,'2025-03-01'),
  -- customer 2: Jan, Feb, then skip, Apr May → max streak = 2
  (4,2,'2025-01-01'),(5,2,'2025-02-01'),(6,2,'2025-04-01'),(7,2,'2025-05-01'),
  -- customer 3: Jan, Mar, May → no consecutive
  (8,3,'2025-01-01'),(9,3,'2025-03-01'),(10,3,'2025-05-01'),
  -- customer 4: Jul, Aug, Sep, Oct (streak of 4) → qualifies
  (11,4,'2025-07-01'),(12,4,'2025-08-01'),(13,4,'2025-09-01'),(14,4,'2025-10-01');
solution:
WITH months AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT customer_id,
         date_trunc('month', order_date)::date AS m
  FROM orders
),
numbered AS (
  SELECT customer_id, m,
         (m - (ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY m) || ' months')::interval)::date AS streak_key
  FROM months
),
streaks AS (
  SELECT customer_id, streak_key, COUNT(*) AS streak_len
  FROM numbered
  GROUP BY customer_id, streak_key
)
SELECT DISTINCT customer_id
FROM streaks
WHERE streak_len >= 3
ORDER BY customer_id;

title: For Each Customer, Return Their Most Purchased Product(s) — If There…
question: Schema: sales(customer_id, product). For each customer, return their MOST PURCHASED product(s) — if there is a tie for most-purchased, return ALL tied products. Return customer_id, product, purchase_count, ordered by customer_id, product.
solution:
WITH counts AS (
  SELECT customer_id, product, COUNT(*) AS purchase_count
  FROM sales
  GROUP BY customer_id, product
),
ranked AS (
  SELECT *, RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY purchase_count DESC) AS rk
  FROM counts
)
SELECT customer_id, product, purchase_count
FROM ranked
WHERE rk = 1
ORDER BY customer_id, product;

title: Each Customer's 2 Most-recent Events
question: Schema: customers(id, name) and events(id, customer_id, ts, kind). Return each customer's 2 most-recent events. Use the LATERAL pattern (Postgres). If a customer has fewer than 2 events, return what they have; if they have none, omit them from the result. Columns: customer_id, name, event_id, ts, kind, ordered by customer_id, ts DESC.
solution:
SELECT c.id AS customer_id, c.name, e.id AS event_id, e.ts, e.kind
FROM customers c
CROSS JOIN LATERAL (
  SELECT id, ts, kind
  FROM events
  WHERE customer_id = c.id
  ORDER BY ts DESC
  LIMIT 2
) e
ORDER BY customer_id, ts DESC;

title: Group Events Into Sessions Using a 30-minute Inactivity Gap per User
question: Schema: event(user_id, ts, event_type). Group events into sessions using a 30-minute inactivity gap per user. Then, for each session, return ONLY the FIRST event in chronological order. Return user_id, session_id, ts, event_type, ordered by user_id, session_id. session_id is the 1-based ordinal within each user.
solution:
WITH flagged AS (
  SELECT user_id, ts, event_type,
         CASE
           WHEN LAG(ts) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts) IS NULL THEN 1
           WHEN EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (ts - LAG(ts) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts)))/60 > 30 THEN 1
           ELSE 0
         END AS is_new_session
  FROM event
),
sessioned AS (
  SELECT user_id, ts, event_type,
         SUM(is_new_session) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts
                                    ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS session_id
  FROM flagged
),
first_in_session AS (
  SELECT user_id, session_id, ts, event_type,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id, session_id ORDER BY ts) AS rn
  FROM sessioned
)
SELECT user_id, session_id, ts, event_type
FROM first_in_session
WHERE rn = 1
ORDER BY user_id, session_id;

title: A Booking Overlaps Another If [start, End) Intervals Intersect
question: Schema: booking(id, room_id, start_ts, end_ts). A booking overlaps another if [start, end) intervals intersect. Find pairs of OVERLAPPING bookings for the SAME room. Return each pair ONCE (with id_a < id_b). Return room_id, id_a, id_b, ordered by room_id, id_a, id_b.
solution:
SELECT a.room_id, a.id AS id_a, b.id AS id_b
FROM booking a
JOIN booking b
  ON a.room_id = b.room_id
 AND a.id < b.id
 AND a.start_ts < b.end_ts
 AND b.start_ts < a.end_ts
ORDER BY a.room_id, id_a, id_b;

title: For the Date Range 2025-01-01
question: Schema: subscription(user_id, start_date, end_date) where each row is one user's active subscription interval (half-open: end_date exclusive). For the date range 2025-01-01 .. 2025-01-05 inclusive, return how many DISTINCT users were active each day. A user is active on day D if start_date <= D < end_date. Return d, active_users, ordered by d. Include every day in the range, even with 0 users.
schema:
CREATE TABLE subscription (user_id INTEGER, start_date DATE, end_date DATE);
INSERT INTO subscription VALUES
  (1,'2025-01-01','2025-01-04'),   -- active Jan 1, 2, 3
  (2,'2025-01-02','2025-01-06'),   -- active Jan 2, 3, 4, 5
  (3,'2025-01-03','2025-01-04'),   -- active Jan 3 only
  (3,'2025-01-05','2025-01-10'),   -- same user, second interval (active Jan 5..)
  (4,'2024-12-30','2025-01-02');   -- active Jan 1 only (within range)
solution:
SELECT d, COUNT(DISTINCT s.user_id) AS active_users
FROM GENERATE_SERIES(DATE '2025-01-01', DATE '2025-01-05', INTERVAL '1 day') AS gs(d)
LEFT JOIN subscription s ON d >= s.start_date AND d < s.end_date
GROUP BY d
ORDER BY d;

title: Groups of Contacts That Are Duplicates After Normalization: Trim…
question: Schema: contacts(id, email, name). Find groups of contacts that are duplicates after normalization: trim whitespace, lowercase, and collapse any internal whitespace runs in the name to a single space. Return one row per normalized duplicate group containing AT LEAST 2 contacts. Columns: normalized_name, normalized_email, ids, n where ids is a comma-sorted string of IDs, n is the group size. Ordering: n DESC, normalized_email, normalized_name.
schema:
CREATE TABLE contacts (id INTEGER, email TEXT, name TEXT);
INSERT INTO contacts VALUES
  (1,'alice@x.com','Alice Smith'),
  (2,'Alice@X.COM','alice  smith '),     -- whitespace + case dup of 1
  (3,'  ALICE@x.com ','ALICE SMITH'),    -- triple dup
  (4,'bob@y.com','Bob'),
  (5,'BOB@y.com','bob'),
  (6,'carol@z.com','Carol');
solution:
WITH n AS (
  SELECT id,
         LOWER(TRIM(email)) AS normalized_email,
         LOWER(REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(name), '\s+', ' ', 'g')) AS normalized_name
  FROM contacts
)
SELECT normalized_name, normalized_email,
       STRING_AGG(id::text, ',' ORDER BY id) AS ids,
       COUNT(*) AS n
FROM n
GROUP BY normalized_name, normalized_email
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 2
ORDER BY n DESC, normalized_email, normalized_name;

title: For Every User Who Posted on at Least Two Distinct Dates During…
question: Schema: posts(user_id INT, post_date DATE). For every user who posted on at least two distinct dates during 2025, compute the number of WHOLE days between their FIRST and LAST post of 2025 (last_date - first_date in days). Output columns: user_id, hiatus_days. Order by user_id ascending. Ignore users with only one posting day in 2025.
solution:
SELECT user_id,
       (MAX(post_date) - MIN(post_date)) AS hiatus_days
FROM posts
WHERE post_date >= DATE '2025-01-01' AND post_date < DATE '2026-01-01'
GROUP BY user_id
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT post_date) >= 2
ORDER BY user_id;

title: For Purchases Made During 2025, Return the Top Two Highest-grossing…
question: Schema: purchases(transaction_id INT, category TEXT, product TEXT, spend NUMERIC, purchase_ts TIMESTAMP). For purchases made during 2025, return the TOP TWO highest-grossing products WITHIN EACH category by total spend. If there is a tie for second place, INCLUDE ALL tied products. Output columns: category, product, total_spend. Order by category ASC then total_spend DESC then product ASC. Round total_spend to the nearest integer.
schema:
CREATE TABLE purchases (transaction_id INT, category TEXT, product TEXT, spend NUMERIC, purchase_ts TIMESTAMP);
INSERT INTO purchases VALUES
  (1,'appliance','refrigerator',1200,'2025-02-01 09:00'),
  (2,'appliance','refrigerator', 800,'2025-04-15 09:00'),
  (3,'appliance','washer',       900,'2025-06-01 09:00'),
  (4,'appliance','washer',       900,'2025-08-20 09:00'),
  (5,'appliance','toaster',      150,'2025-03-12 09:00'),
  (6,'appliance','blender',      400,'2025-07-08 09:00'),
  (7,'electronics','tv',        1500,'2025-05-10 09:00'),
  (8,'electronics','headphones', 250,'2025-05-11 09:00'),
  (9,'electronics','headphones', 250,'2025-09-21 09:00'),
 (10,'electronics','laptop',     900,'2024-12-31 09:00'),
 (11,'electronics','laptop',     900,'2025-11-30 09:00');
solution:
WITH yr AS (
  SELECT category, product, SUM(spend) AS total_spend
  FROM purchases
  WHERE purchase_ts >= TIMESTAMP '2025-01-01' AND purchase_ts < TIMESTAMP '2026-01-01'
  GROUP BY category, product
),
rk AS (
  SELECT category, product, total_spend,
         DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY total_spend DESC) AS rnk
  FROM yr
)
SELECT category, product, ROUND(total_spend)::int AS total_spend
FROM rk
WHERE rnk <= 2
ORDER BY category, total_spend DESC, product;

title: You Have Activities(user_id Int, Activity_type Text, Time_spent…
question: You have activities(user_id INT, activity_type TEXT, time_spent NUMERIC) and user_age(user_id INT, age_bucket TEXT). Considering ONLY the 'send' and 'open' activity types, for each age_bucket return the percentage of total time spent on each of the two activity types. Output one row per (age_bucket, activity_type) with columns: age_bucket, activity_type, percentage where percentage is rounded to 2 decimal places. Order by age_bucket then activity_type.
solution:
WITH tot AS (
  SELECT u.age_bucket,
         a.activity_type,
         SUM(a.time_spent) AS act_time
  FROM activities a
  JOIN user_age u ON u.user_id = a.user_id
  WHERE a.activity_type IN ('send','open')
  GROUP BY u.age_bucket, a.activity_type
),
bk AS (
  SELECT age_bucket, SUM(act_time) AS bucket_time FROM tot GROUP BY age_bucket
)
SELECT t.age_bucket,
       t.activity_type,
       ROUND(100.0 * t.act_time / b.bucket_time, 2) AS percentage
FROM tot t
JOIN bk b USING (age_bucket)
ORDER BY t.age_bucket, t.activity_type;

title: Profile IDS of 'power Creators' Whose Personal Follower Count…
question: Schema: personal_profile(profile_id INT, follower_count INT), employment(profile_id INT, company_id INT), company_page(company_id INT, follower_count INT). Return profile IDs of 'power creators' whose PERSONAL follower count strictly exceeds the follower count of EVERY company they work at. (Some profiles have multiple employments — the rule must hold for each one.) Profiles with no recorded employment should NOT appear. Output column: profile_id. Order ascending.
solution:
SELECT p.profile_id
FROM personal_profile p
WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM employment e WHERE e.profile_id = p.profile_id)
  AND NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT 1
    FROM employment e
    JOIN company_page c ON c.company_id = e.company_id
    WHERE e.profile_id = p.profile_id
      AND c.follower_count >= p.follower_count
  )
ORDER BY p.profile_id;

title: Identify the Top 2 Senders by Message Volume in August 2025
question: Schema: messages(message_id INT, sender_id INT, recipient_id INT, sent_at TIMESTAMP). Identify the TOP 2 senders by message volume in August 2025. Break ties by lower sender_id first. Output columns: sender_id, message_count. If there is a tie at rank 2, return ALL tied users.
solution:
WITH cnt AS (
  SELECT sender_id, COUNT(*) AS message_count
  FROM messages
  WHERE sent_at >= TIMESTAMP '2025-08-01' AND sent_at < TIMESTAMP '2025-09-01'
  GROUP BY sender_id
),
rk AS (
  SELECT sender_id, message_count,
         DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY message_count DESC) AS r
  FROM cnt
)
SELECT sender_id, message_count
FROM rk
WHERE r <= 2
ORDER BY message_count DESC, sender_id;

title: For Each User-ticker Pair, Compute a Running Realized PnL Using The…
question: Schema: trade(trade_id INT, user_id INT, ticker TEXT, action TEXT, qty INT, price NUMERIC, executed_at TIMESTAMP) where action is 'buy' or 'sell'. For each user-ticker pair, compute a RUNNING realized PnL using the simple cash-flow definition: cash_out = qty*price for a buy (positive outflow), cash_in = qty*price for a sell (positive inflow). Running PnL after each row = cumulative (cash_in - cash_out). Output columns user_id, ticker, executed_at, running_pnl for every trade row in chronological order per (user, ticker). Round running_pnl to 2 decimals. Sort by user_id, ticker, executed_at.
solution:
SELECT user_id, ticker, executed_at,
       ROUND(
         SUM(CASE WHEN action='sell' THEN qty*price ELSE -qty*price END)
           OVER (PARTITION BY user_id, ticker
                 ORDER BY executed_at
                 ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW),
         2) AS running_pnl
FROM trade
ORDER BY user_id, ticker, executed_at;

title: A Payment Is Considered a Duplicate If There Exists an Earlier…
question: Schema: payment(payment_id INT, merchant_id INT, credit_card_id INT, amount NUMERIC, paid_at TIMESTAMP). A payment is considered a DUPLICATE if there exists an earlier payment by the SAME credit card to the SAME merchant for the SAME amount within 10 minutes (600 seconds) before it. Count the number of duplicate payments per merchant — i.e. exclude the first occurrence of each cluster. Output columns: merchant_id, duplicate_count. Only include merchants whose count is > 0, ordered by merchant_id.
solution:
WITH lagged AS (
  SELECT merchant_id, credit_card_id, amount, paid_at,
         LAG(paid_at) OVER (PARTITION BY merchant_id, credit_card_id, amount ORDER BY paid_at) AS prev_paid_at
  FROM payment
)
SELECT merchant_id, COUNT(*) AS duplicate_count
FROM lagged
WHERE prev_paid_at IS NOT NULL
  AND paid_at - prev_paid_at <= INTERVAL '10 minutes'
GROUP BY merchant_id
ORDER BY merchant_id;

title: For Each (product, Year), Compute the Total Spend AND the Prior-year…
question: Schema: txn(transaction_id INT, product_id INT, spend NUMERIC, occurred_at TIMESTAMP). For each (product, year), compute the total spend and the prior-year spend. Then compute YoY growth = ROUND((curr - prev) / prev * 100, 2). Output columns: year, product_id, curr_year_spend, prev_year_spend, yoy_growth. Rows with no prior-year history should still appear, with prev_year_spend and yoy_growth set to NULL. Order by year, product_id.
solution:
WITH yearly AS (
  SELECT EXTRACT(YEAR FROM occurred_at)::int AS year,
         product_id,
         SUM(spend) AS curr_year_spend
  FROM txn
  GROUP BY EXTRACT(YEAR FROM occurred_at), product_id
)
SELECT year, product_id, curr_year_spend,
       LAG(curr_year_spend) OVER (PARTITION BY product_id ORDER BY year) AS prev_year_spend,
       ROUND(
         (curr_year_spend - LAG(curr_year_spend) OVER (PARTITION BY product_id ORDER BY year))
         / NULLIF(LAG(curr_year_spend) OVER (PARTITION BY product_id ORDER BY year), 0) * 100,
         2) AS yoy_growth
FROM yearly
ORDER BY year, product_id;

title: For Each Card, Return the Number of Cards Issued in Its Launch Month…
question: Schema: card_issuance(card_name TEXT, issue_year INT, issue_month INT, issued_cards INT). For each card, return the number of cards issued in its LAUNCH MONTH — defined as the earliest (year, month) row for that card. Output columns: card_name, launch_issued. Sort by launch_issued DESC then card_name.
schema:
CREATE TABLE card_issuance (card_name TEXT, issue_year INT, issue_month INT, issued_cards INT);
INSERT INTO card_issuance VALUES
  ('SapphireGo',2023, 1, 12000),
  ('SapphireGo',2023, 2, 15000),
  ('SapphireGo',2023, 3, 20000),
  ('VelocityX',  2024, 6,  8000),
  ('VelocityX',  2024, 7, 11000),
  ('AmberOne',   2023,12,  4500),
  ('AmberOne',   2024, 1,  9000),
  ('NorthLite',  2025, 3, 12000);
solution:
SELECT card_name, launch_issued FROM (
  SELECT DISTINCT ON (card_name)
         card_name,
         issued_cards AS launch_issued,
         issue_year, issue_month
  FROM card_issuance
  ORDER BY card_name, issue_year, issue_month
) t
ORDER BY launch_issued DESC, card_name;

title: The Median Number of Searches Across the Implied Population of Users
question: Schema: search_frequency(searches INT, num_users INT) — each row says 'num_users users each performed exactly searches searches last year' (a histogram, NOT a raw event log). Compute the MEDIAN number of searches across the implied population of users. Return one row with column median rounded to 1 decimal place.
solution:
WITH expanded AS (
  SELECT s.searches
  FROM search_frequency s,
       generate_series(1, s.num_users)
)
SELECT ROUND(PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY searches)::numeric, 1) AS median
FROM expanded;

title: For 2025, Build a Histogram of Users-per-tweet-count
question: Schema: tweet(tweet_id INT, user_id INT, tweet_date DATE). For 2025, build a histogram of users-per-tweet-count. That is, for each value K = number of tweets a user posted in 2025, output the number of users who posted exactly K tweets that year. Users with zero tweets are not in the table and should NOT appear in the output. Columns: tweet_count, users. Order by tweet_count.
solution:
WITH per_user AS (
  SELECT user_id, COUNT(*) AS tweet_count
  FROM tweet
  WHERE tweet_date >= DATE '2025-01-01' AND tweet_date < DATE '2026-01-01'
  GROUP BY user_id
)
SELECT tweet_count, COUNT(*) AS users
FROM per_user
GROUP BY tweet_count
ORDER BY tweet_count;

title: Users Who Confirmed (signup_ack = True) Exactly on the Calendar Day…
question: Schema: signups(user_id INT, signup_ts TIMESTAMP) and text_messages(msg_id INT, user_id INT, signup_ack BOOLEAN, sent_ts TIMESTAMP). Find users who confirmed (signup_ack = TRUE) EXACTLY on the calendar day AFTER they signed up — i.e. (sent_date - signup_date) = 1. A user may receive multiple texts; if ANY of their TRUE confirmations falls on day +1, the user qualifies. Output columns: user_id. Order ascending.
solution:
SELECT DISTINCT s.user_id
FROM signups s
JOIN text_messages t ON t.user_id = s.user_id
WHERE t.signup_ack = TRUE
  AND (t.sent_ts::date - s.signup_ts::date) = 1
ORDER BY s.user_id;

title: Define a 'monthly Active User' as a User Who Performed Any Action…
question: Schema: events(user_id INT, action TEXT, event_date DATE) where action is one of 'sign-in', 'like', 'comment', 'post'. Define a 'monthly active user' as a user who performed any action other than 'post' in that month. Find the count of users who were monthly-active in BOTH July 2025 AND June 2025 (i.e. retained users). Return one row: month, mau, retained. month should be the integer 7, mau the count active in July, retained the count active in both July and June.
solution:
WITH active AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT user_id,
         DATE_TRUNC('month', event_date)::date AS month_start
  FROM events
  WHERE action <> 'post'
)
SELECT 7 AS month,
       COUNT(DISTINCT j.user_id) AS mau,
       COUNT(DISTINCT j.user_id) FILTER (WHERE j.user_id IN (SELECT user_id FROM active WHERE month_start = DATE '2025-06-01')) AS retained
FROM active j
WHERE j.month_start = DATE '2025-07-01';

title: Page IDS for Pages with Zero Recorded Likes, Including Any Pages…
question: Schema: pages(page_id INT, page_name TEXT) and page_likes(user_id INT, page_id INT, liked_at TIMESTAMP). Return page IDs for pages with ZERO recorded likes, including any pages whose only page_likes rows have a NULL page_id (those are not real matches). Output page_id, ordered ascending. Solve this WITHOUT using NOT IN to avoid the NULL-IN gotcha.

title: For Each User, Find the Longest Gap (in Whole Minutes) Between The…
question: Schema: app_session(user_id INT, started_at TIMESTAMP, ended_at TIMESTAMP). For each user, find the longest GAP (in whole minutes) between the end of one session and the start of the NEXT session. Ignore overlapping sessions (treat any negative gap as 0). Users with only one session should NOT appear. Output columns: user_id, longest_gap_minutes. Sort by longest_gap_minutes DESC, then user_id.
solution:
WITH gaps AS (
  SELECT user_id,
         LAG(ended_at) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY started_at) AS prev_end,
         started_at
  FROM app_session
),
clamped AS (
  SELECT user_id,
         GREATEST(EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (started_at - prev_end))/60, 0) AS gap_min
  FROM gaps
  WHERE prev_end IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT user_id, MAX(gap_min)::int AS longest_gap_minutes
FROM clamped
GROUP BY user_id
ORDER BY longest_gap_minutes DESC, user_id;

title: Songs_weekly(user_id Int, Song_id Int, Played_at Date) Records…
question: Schema: songs_history(user_id INT, song_id INT, play_count INT) records historical plays up to and including 2025-08-03. songs_weekly(user_id INT, song_id INT, played_at DATE) records individual plays on or after 2025-08-04 (one row per play). Compute the cumulative play count for every (user, song) pair THROUGH AND INCLUDING 2025-08-04. Include pairs that appear in either table. Output user_id, song_id, cumulative_plays, sorted by cumulative_plays DESC then user_id, song_id.
solution:
WITH new_plays AS (
  SELECT user_id, song_id, COUNT(*) AS play_count
  FROM songs_weekly
  WHERE played_at <= DATE '2025-08-04'
  GROUP BY user_id, song_id
)
SELECT user_id, song_id, SUM(play_count)::int AS cumulative_plays
FROM (
  SELECT user_id, song_id, play_count FROM songs_history
  UNION ALL
  SELECT user_id, song_id, play_count FROM new_plays
) all_plays
GROUP BY user_id, song_id
ORDER BY cumulative_plays DESC, user_id, song_id;

title: For Each User, Return Their Third Transaction (chronologically)
question: Schema: transaction(txn_id INT, user_id INT, spend NUMERIC, occurred_at TIMESTAMP). For each user, return their THIRD transaction (chronologically). If a user has fewer than 3 transactions, do not include them. Output user_id, spend, occurred_at. Order by user_id.
solution:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT user_id, spend, occurred_at,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY occurred_at) AS rn
  FROM transaction
)
SELECT user_id, spend, occurred_at
FROM ranked
WHERE rn = 3
ORDER BY user_id;

title: For Each Driver, Return the Rider Who Tipped Them the Most in Total…
question: Schema: trip(trip_id INT, driver_id INT, rider_id INT, tip_amount NUMERIC, started_at TIMESTAMP). For each driver, return the rider who tipped them the MOST IN TOTAL (sum across all their trips). If two riders tie, return the rider with the SMALLEST rider_id. Output columns: driver_id, top_rider, total_tip. Sort by driver_id. Round total_tip to 2 dp.
solution:
WITH per_pair AS (
  SELECT driver_id, rider_id, SUM(tip_amount) AS total_tip
  FROM trip
  GROUP BY driver_id, rider_id
),
ranked AS (
  SELECT driver_id, rider_id, total_tip,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY driver_id ORDER BY total_tip DESC, rider_id) AS rn
  FROM per_pair
)
SELECT driver_id, rider_id AS top_rider, ROUND(total_tip, 2) AS total_tip
FROM ranked
WHERE rn = 1
ORDER BY driver_id;

title: For Each Driver, Compute (a) the Average Delivery Duration In…
question: Schema: delivery(driver_id INT, customer_id INT, order_placed TIMESTAMP, delivered_at TIMESTAMP). For each driver, compute (a) the average delivery duration in minutes per CALENDAR DAY and (b) the overall average across all days. Output columns: driver_id, day, daily_avg_min, overall_avg_min. Both averages rounded to 2 decimal places. Sort by driver_id, day.
solution:
WITH dur AS (
  SELECT driver_id,
         order_placed::date AS day,
         EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (delivered_at - order_placed))/60 AS minutes
  FROM delivery
),
daily AS (
  SELECT driver_id, day, AVG(minutes) AS daily_avg_min
  FROM dur
  GROUP BY driver_id, day
)
SELECT driver_id, day,
       ROUND(daily_avg_min::numeric, 2) AS daily_avg_min,
       ROUND(AVG(daily_avg_min) OVER (PARTITION BY driver_id)::numeric, 2) AS overall_avg_min
FROM daily
ORDER BY driver_id, day;

title: For Each User, Return the Number of Products Bought on Their Most…
question: Schema: user_txn(user_id INT, products_bought INT, txn_date DATE). For each user, return the number of products bought on their MOST RECENT transaction date. If a user has multiple transactions on the same most-recent date, SUM the products across them. Output columns: user_id, latest_date, total_products. Order by user_id.
solution:
WITH latest AS (
  SELECT user_id, MAX(txn_date) AS latest_date
  FROM user_txn
  GROUP BY user_id
)
SELECT u.user_id, u.txn_date AS latest_date, SUM(u.products_bought)::int AS total_products
FROM user_txn u
JOIN latest l ON l.user_id = u.user_id AND l.latest_date = u.txn_date
GROUP BY u.user_id, u.txn_date
ORDER BY u.user_id;

title: For Each Calendar Month in 2025 for Which There Is Data, Identify…
question: Schema: watch(user_id INT, started_at TIMESTAMP, watched_minutes INT). For each calendar month in 2025 for which there is data, identify the TOP 3 users by total watched minutes. If users tie on total, prefer SMALLER user_id. Output columns: month_start, user_id, watched_minutes. Sort by month_start then descending watched_minutes then user_id.
solution:
WITH monthly AS (
  SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', started_at)::date AS month_start,
         user_id,
         SUM(watched_minutes) AS watched_minutes
  FROM watch
  WHERE started_at >= TIMESTAMP '2025-01-01' AND started_at < TIMESTAMP '2026-01-01'
  GROUP BY 1, 2
),
ranked AS (
  SELECT month_start, user_id, watched_minutes,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY month_start ORDER BY watched_minutes DESC, user_id) AS rn
  FROM monthly
)
SELECT month_start, user_id, watched_minutes::int AS watched_minutes
FROM ranked
WHERE rn <= 3
ORDER BY month_start, watched_minutes DESC, user_id;

title: Two Calls by the Same Policy_id Within 7 Days Count as a Single…
question: Schema: call_log(policy_id INT, called_at TIMESTAMP). Two calls by the SAME policy_id within 7 days count as a single 'callback cluster' — i.e. a cluster boundary is created whenever the gap from the previous call exceeds 7 days OR there's no previous call. Return the number of policy holders who created AT LEAST 3 callback clusters. Return one row: column policy_holders.
solution:
WITH flagged AS (
  SELECT policy_id, called_at,
         CASE WHEN called_at - LAG(called_at) OVER (PARTITION BY policy_id ORDER BY called_at)
                   > INTERVAL '7 days' OR LAG(called_at) OVER (PARTITION BY policy_id ORDER BY called_at) IS NULL
              THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS is_cluster_start
  FROM call_log
),
cluster_counts AS (
  SELECT policy_id, SUM(is_cluster_start) AS clusters
  FROM flagged
  GROUP BY policy_id
)
SELECT COUNT(*) AS policy_holders
FROM cluster_counts
WHERE clusters >= 3;

title: A Part Is 'finished' When Every Step Recorded for It Has a Non-NULL…
question: Schema: parts_assembly(part_name TEXT, assembly_step INT, finish_at TIMESTAMP). A part is 'finished' when EVERY step recorded for it has a non-NULL finish_at. Return the parts that are NOT finished (have at least one step with NULL finish_at). Also return how many steps remain incomplete per part. Output columns: part_name, incomplete_steps. Sort by part_name.
schema:
CREATE TABLE parts_assembly (part_name TEXT, assembly_step INT, finish_at TIMESTAMP);
INSERT INTO parts_assembly VALUES
  ('wheel',      1,'2025-01-01 09:00'),
  ('wheel',      2,'2025-01-01 10:00'),
  ('wheel',      3, NULL),
  ('battery',    1,'2025-01-02 09:00'),
  ('battery',    2, NULL),
  ('battery',    3, NULL),
  ('charger',    1,'2025-01-03 09:00'),
  ('charger',    2,'2025-01-03 10:00'),
  ('seat',       1, NULL);
solution:
SELECT part_name, COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE finish_at IS NULL) AS incomplete_steps
FROM parts_assembly
GROUP BY part_name
HAVING COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE finish_at IS NULL) > 0
ORDER BY part_name;

title: For Each Date That Appears in the Data, Compute the Cumulative…
question: Schema: event(user_id INT, event_date DATE). For each date that appears in the data, compute the cumulative number of DISTINCT users seen UP TO AND INCLUDING that date (across all dates in the table). A user counted on an earlier date is NOT counted again on later dates. Output columns: event_date, cumulative_distinct_users. Order by event_date.
solution:
WITH first_seen AS (
  SELECT user_id, MIN(event_date) AS first_date
  FROM event
  GROUP BY user_id
),
new_per_day AS (
  SELECT first_date AS event_date, COUNT(*) AS new_users
  FROM first_seen
  GROUP BY first_date
),
all_days AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT event_date FROM event
)
SELECT d.event_date,
       SUM(COALESCE(n.new_users, 0)) OVER (ORDER BY d.event_date ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS cumulative_distinct_users
FROM all_days d
LEFT JOIN new_per_day n ON n.event_date = d.event_date
ORDER BY d.event_date;

title: Funnel Conversion Percentages for Users Who Signed Up in October 2025
question: Schema: signup_event(user_id INT, signed_up_at TIMESTAMP) and confirm_event(user_id INT, confirmed_at TIMESTAMP) and purchase_event(user_id INT, purchased_at TIMESTAMP). Compute funnel conversion percentages for users who signed up in October 2025. (Each event must occur AFTER the signup time for the same user.) Output a single row with: signups, confirm_pct, purchase_pct. Percentages rounded to 2 dp, with 100% = 100.00. confirm_pct = 100 * (signups who later confirmed) / signups. purchase_pct = 100 * (signups who confirmed AND later purchased) / signups.
solution:
WITH oct_signups AS (
  SELECT user_id, signed_up_at
  FROM signup_event
  WHERE signed_up_at >= TIMESTAMP '2025-10-01' AND signed_up_at < TIMESTAMP '2025-11-01'
),
confirmed AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT s.user_id
  FROM oct_signups s
  JOIN confirm_event c ON c.user_id = s.user_id AND c.confirmed_at > s.signed_up_at
),
purchased AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT s.user_id
  FROM oct_signups s
  JOIN confirm_event c ON c.user_id = s.user_id AND c.confirmed_at > s.signed_up_at
  JOIN purchase_event p ON p.user_id = s.user_id AND p.purchased_at > c.confirmed_at
)
SELECT (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM oct_signups)::int AS signups,
       ROUND(100.0 * (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM confirmed) / NULLIF((SELECT COUNT(*) FROM oct_signups),0), 2) AS confirm_pct,
       ROUND(100.0 * (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM purchased) / NULLIF((SELECT COUNT(*) FROM oct_signups),0), 2) AS purchase_pct;

title: Identify All Duplicate Emails Ignoring Case AND Surrounding…
question: Schema: customer(customer_id INT, email TEXT, signed_up_at TIMESTAMP). Identify all DUPLICATE emails ignoring case and surrounding whitespace, and for each group return the canonical (lowercased, trimmed) email, the EARLIEST signup time among the dupes, and the LATEST signup time. Output columns: email_norm, earliest, latest, n_accounts. Only include groups with > 1 account. Sort by email_norm.
schema:
CREATE TABLE customer (customer_id INT, email TEXT, signed_up_at TIMESTAMP);
INSERT INTO customer VALUES
  (1,'Alice@Example.com','2025-01-01 09:00'),
  (2,'alice@example.com ','2025-03-01 09:00'),
  (3,' ALICE@example.com','2025-06-01 09:00'),
  (4,'bob@x.com','2025-02-01 09:00'),
  (5,'carol@y.com','2025-05-01 09:00'),
  (6,'carol@y.com','2025-04-01 09:00');
solution:
SELECT LOWER(TRIM(email)) AS email_norm,
       MIN(signed_up_at) AS earliest,
       MAX(signed_up_at) AS latest,
       COUNT(*) AS n_accounts
FROM customer
GROUP BY LOWER(TRIM(email))
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
ORDER BY email_norm;

title: Users Who Have Purchased Every Item in Top_items
question: Schema: top_items(item_id INT) (small reference list of items) and purchase(user_id INT, item_id INT). Find users who have purchased EVERY item in top_items. (Order of purchase, duplicates, and items outside the top list do not matter.) Output user_id, sorted ascending.
solution:
SELECT p.user_id
FROM purchase p
WHERE p.item_id IN (SELECT item_id FROM top_items)
GROUP BY p.user_id
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT p.item_id) = (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM top_items)
ORDER BY p.user_id;

title: For Each Department, Compute the Median Base_salary, Ignoring Rows…
question: Schema: salary(emp_id INT, dept_id INT, base_salary NUMERIC). For each department, compute the median base_salary, IGNORING rows where base_salary IS NULL. Departments where every row has NULL salary should NOT appear in the output. Output columns: dept_id, median_salary. Round to 2 dp. Sort by dept_id.
solution:
SELECT dept_id,
       ROUND(PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY base_salary)::numeric, 2) AS median_salary
FROM salary
WHERE base_salary IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY dept_id
ORDER BY dept_id;

title: For Each Month Present in the Data, Pivot the Two Metrics Into…
question: Schema: monthly_metric(month_start DATE, metric TEXT, value NUMERIC) where metric is one of 'revenue', 'cost'. For each month present in the data, pivot the two metrics into separate columns and add a profit (revenue - cost) and mom_profit_delta (profit current month - profit previous month) column. Output columns: month_start, revenue, cost, profit, mom_profit_delta. profit and delta rounded to 2 dp; mom_profit_delta is NULL for the first month. Order by month_start.
solution:
WITH p AS (
  SELECT month_start,
         SUM(value) FILTER (WHERE metric='revenue') AS revenue,
         SUM(value) FILTER (WHERE metric='cost')    AS cost
  FROM monthly_metric
  GROUP BY month_start
)
SELECT month_start, revenue, cost,
       ROUND((revenue - cost)::numeric, 2) AS profit,
       ROUND(((revenue - cost) - LAG(revenue - cost) OVER (ORDER BY month_start))::numeric, 2) AS mom_profit_delta
FROM p
ORDER BY month_start;

title: For Each Trade, Return the Latest Quote Price for the Same Symbol…
question: Schema: quote(symbol TEXT, quoted_at TIMESTAMP, price NUMERIC) and trade(trade_id INT, symbol TEXT, traded_at TIMESTAMP). For each trade, return the latest quote price for the SAME symbol whose quoted_at <= traded_at. If no such quote exists, return NULL. Output columns: trade_id, symbol, traded_at, last_price. Sort by trade_id. Solve using a LATERAL subquery (no self-join row-explosion).
solution:
SELECT t.trade_id, t.symbol, t.traded_at, q.price AS last_price
FROM trade t
LEFT JOIN LATERAL (
  SELECT price
  FROM quote
  WHERE quote.symbol = t.symbol AND quote.quoted_at <= t.traded_at
  ORDER BY quote.quoted_at DESC
  LIMIT 1
) q ON TRUE
ORDER BY t.trade_id;

title: For Each Category, Rank Products by Units_sold Desc
question: Schema: product(product_id INT, category TEXT, units_sold INT, is_discontinued BOOLEAN). For each category, rank products by units_sold DESC. DISCONTINUED products must appear in the output but must NOT participate in the ranking — they should all have rank_in_category = NULL. All active products should be densely ranked (1, 2, 3, ... ties share a rank, no gaps). Output columns: product_id, category, units_sold, is_discontinued, rank_in_category. Sort by category, units_sold DESC, product_id.
schema:
CREATE TABLE product (product_id INT, category TEXT, units_sold INT, is_discontinued BOOLEAN);
INSERT INTO product VALUES
  (1,'A',100,FALSE),
  (2,'A', 80,FALSE),
  (3,'A', 80,FALSE),
  (4,'A',999,TRUE),
  (5,'B', 50,TRUE),
  (6,'B', 60,FALSE),
  (7,'B', 40,FALSE),
  (8,'C', 10,FALSE);
solution:
SELECT product_id, category, units_sold, is_discontinued,
       CASE WHEN is_discontinued THEN NULL
            ELSE DENSE_RANK() OVER (
              PARTITION BY category
              ORDER BY CASE WHEN is_discontinued THEN NULL ELSE units_sold END DESC NULLS LAST
            ) END AS rank_in_category
FROM product
ORDER BY category, units_sold DESC, product_id;

title: You Run a Tennis-stats System
question: You run a tennis-stats system. Table championships(year, wimbledon, fr_open, us_open, au_open) stores the player_id who won each of the four Grand Slams that year (one row per year). Table players(player_id, player_name) is a lookup. Return every player who has won at least one Grand Slam title with columns player_id, player_name, grand_slam_count representing the TOTAL number of titles (across all years and all four tournaments). Order by grand_slam_count DESC, then player_id ASC. Players with zero wins must NOT appear.
solution:
WITH wins AS (
  SELECT wimbledon AS player_id FROM championships
  UNION ALL SELECT fr_open  FROM championships
  UNION ALL SELECT us_open  FROM championships
  UNION ALL SELECT au_open  FROM championships
)
SELECT p.player_id, p.player_name, COUNT(*)::int AS grand_slam_count
FROM players p
JOIN wins w ON w.player_id = p.player_id
GROUP BY p.player_id, p.player_name
ORDER BY grand_slam_count DESC, p.player_id;

title: The Three Highest Distinct Salaries in Each Department -- Including…
question: Given employee(emp_id, name, dept_id, salary) and department(dept_id, dept_name), return the THREE highest distinct salaries in each department -- INCLUDING ties (so if four people share the top salary, all four appear). Output columns: dept_name, name, salary, ordered by dept_name ASC, then salary DESC, then name ASC. Hint: this is the case where DENSE_RANK <= 3 behaves correctly but ROW_NUMBER <= 3 does not.
solution:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT e.dept_id, e.name, e.salary,
         DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY e.dept_id ORDER BY e.salary DESC) AS rk
  FROM employee e
)
SELECT d.dept_name, r.name, r.salary
FROM ranked r
JOIN department d ON d.dept_id = r.dept_id
WHERE r.rk <= 3
ORDER BY d.dept_name, r.salary DESC, r.name;

title: You Moderate Streaming Sessions
question: You moderate streaming sessions. Table log_info(account_id, ip_addr, login_ts, logout_ts) stores each session. An account is BANNED if it has two sessions whose intervals overlap (i.e. were active simultaneously) AND those sessions used DIFFERENT IP addresses (concurrent access from two locations). Return the distinct account_ids flagged for banning, ordered ascending. Hint: a window-function approach that only inspects the previous row will fail when three sessions for the same IP are interleaved -- use a self-join over all pairs.
solution:
SELECT DISTINCT l1.account_id
FROM log_info l1
JOIN log_info l2
  ON l1.account_id = l2.account_id
 AND l1.ip_addr <> l2.ip_addr
 AND l1.login_ts BETWEEN l2.login_ts AND l2.logout_ts
ORDER BY l1.account_id;

title: Table Logs(log_id Integer) Contains a Set of Log Identifiers, Each…
question: Table logs(log_id INTEGER) contains a set of log identifiers, each unique, but with gaps. Return all contiguous ranges of integer ids as start_id, end_id, ordered by start_id ASC. A single isolated id forms a range where start = end.
schema:
CREATE TABLE logs (log_id INTEGER);
INSERT INTO logs VALUES (1),(2),(3),(7),(8),(10),(15),(16),(17),(18);
solution:
WITH numbered AS (
  SELECT log_id, log_id - ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY log_id) AS grp
  FROM logs
)
SELECT MIN(log_id) AS start_id, MAX(log_id) AS end_id
FROM numbered
GROUP BY grp
ORDER BY start_id;

title: Table User_visits(user_id Int, Visit_date Date) Records Every Visit…
question: Table user_visits(user_id INT, visit_date DATE) records every visit a user makes. For each user, return the largest number of days between TWO CONSECUTIVE visits, or between their last visit and a fixed '2025-04-01' cap date if no later visit exists. Columns: user_id, biggest_window. Order by user_id ASC. Each (user, date) row is unique.
solution:
WITH paired AS (
  SELECT user_id, visit_date,
         LEAD(visit_date, 1, DATE '2025-04-01') OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY visit_date) AS next_date
  FROM user_visits
)
SELECT user_id, MAX(next_date - visit_date)::int AS biggest_window
FROM paired
GROUP BY user_id
ORDER BY user_id;

title: Table Customer_purchases(customer_id Int, Product Char(1)) Lists…
question: Table customer_purchases(customer_id INT, product CHAR(1)) lists items each customer bought (product is 'A','B', or 'C'). Return customers who bought BOTH 'A' AND 'B' but NEVER bought 'C'. Output customer_id only, ordered ascending.
schema:
CREATE TABLE customer_purchases (customer_id INTEGER, product CHAR(1));
INSERT INTO customer_purchases VALUES
  (1,'A'),(1,'B'),(1,'C'),
  (2,'A'),(2,'B'),
  (3,'A'),(3,'B'),(3,'A'),
  (4,'A'),(4,'C'),
  (5,'B'),(5,'B'),
  (6,'A'),(6,'B'),(6,'A'),(6,'B');
solution:
SELECT customer_id
FROM customer_purchases
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE product = 'A') >= 1
   AND COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE product = 'B') >= 1
   AND COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE product = 'C') = 0
ORDER BY customer_id;

title: Table Tree_node(id Int, P_id Int) Represents a Tree Where P_id Is…
question: Table tree_node(id INT, p_id INT) represents a tree where p_id is the parent of id, NULL for the root. Classify each node as 'Root' (no parent), 'Inner' (has both a parent and at least one child), or 'Leaf' (has a parent but no children). Return columns id, node_type ordered by id ASC. Use a single query.
schema:
CREATE TABLE tree_node (id INTEGER, p_id INTEGER);
INSERT INTO tree_node VALUES (1,NULL),(2,1),(3,1),(4,2),(5,2);
solution:
SELECT id,
  CASE
    WHEN p_id IS NULL THEN 'Root'
    WHEN id IN (SELECT p_id FROM tree_node WHERE p_id IS NOT NULL) THEN 'Inner'
    ELSE 'Leaf'
  END AS node_type
FROM tree_node
ORDER BY id;

title: Table Point(x Int) Stores Positions on a Number Line (rows Are…
question: Table point(x INT) stores positions on a number line (rows are unordered, all x are distinct). Return the SHORTEST distance between any two distinct points as a single row with column shortest. Naive solutions cross-join in O(n^2); aim for the O(n log n) approach using a window function on the sorted list.
schema:
CREATE TABLE point (x INTEGER);
INSERT INTO point VALUES (-1),(0),(2),(5),(10),(11),(20);

title: Table Person(name Text, Phone_number Text) Stores International…
question: Table person(name TEXT, phone_number TEXT) stores international phone numbers where each number begins with a country code: 1, 86, 91, or 880. Table calls(caller TEXT, callee TEXT, duration_sec INT) logs calls. Return, for each country code that ever appeared in a call (caller OR callee), the average call duration in seconds rounded to 2 decimals. Columns: country_code, avg_duration, ordered by country_code ASC. Note: each call contributes ONCE to the caller's country and ONCE to the callee's country (so the same call may bump two country averages).
solution:
WITH legs AS (
  SELECT caller AS who, duration_sec FROM calls
  UNION ALL
  SELECT callee, duration_sec FROM calls
)
SELECT split_part(p.phone_number, '-', 1) AS country_code,
       ROUND(AVG(l.duration_sec)::numeric, 2) AS avg_duration
FROM legs l
JOIN person p ON p.name = l.who
GROUP BY split_part(p.phone_number, '-', 1)
ORDER BY country_code;

title: The Median Salary per Department Without Using PERCENTILE_CONT Or…
question: Compute the MEDIAN salary per department WITHOUT using PERCENTILE_CONT or PERCENTILE_DISC. Schema: employee(emp_id, dept_id, salary INTEGER). For odd group size, median is the middle value; for even group size, median is the average of the two middle values. Output columns: dept_id, median_salary (numeric with 1 decimal). Order by dept_id. Use window functions.
solution:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT dept_id, salary,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY dept_id ORDER BY salary) AS rn_asc,
         COUNT(*)   OVER (PARTITION BY dept_id) AS cnt
  FROM employee
)
SELECT dept_id,
       ROUND(AVG(salary)::numeric, 1) AS median_salary
FROM ranked
WHERE rn_asc IN (FLOOR((cnt + 1) / 2.0)::int, CEIL((cnt + 1) / 2.0)::int)
GROUP BY dept_id
ORDER BY dept_id;

title: Table Transactions(transaction_id Int, Ts Timestamp, Amount Numeric)…
question: Table transactions(transaction_id INT, ts TIMESTAMP, amount NUMERIC) records purchases. For each calendar day, return the transaction_id of the row with the largest amount that day. Ties are broken by the EARLIER timestamp (and then by the smaller transaction_id). Columns: day, transaction_id, amount ordered by day ASC.
solution:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT transaction_id, ts::date AS day, amount, ts,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY ts::date ORDER BY amount DESC, ts, transaction_id) AS rn
  FROM transactions
)
SELECT day, transaction_id, amount
FROM ranked
WHERE rn = 1
ORDER BY day;

title: Table Orders(order_id Int, Customer_id Int, Order_date Date, Total…
question: Table orders(order_id INT, customer_id INT, order_date DATE, total NUMERIC) and customers(customer_id INT, name TEXT). Return EACH customer's THREE most recent orders (by date). If a customer has fewer than three orders, return all of them. Columns: name, order_id, order_date, total. Order by name ASC, then order_date DESC, then order_id ASC.
solution:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT o.order_id, o.customer_id, o.order_date, o.total,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY o.customer_id ORDER BY o.order_date DESC, o.order_id DESC) AS rn
  FROM orders o
)
SELECT c.name, r.order_id, r.order_date, r.total
FROM ranked r
JOIN customers c ON c.customer_id = r.customer_id
WHERE r.rn <= 3
ORDER BY c.name, r.order_date DESC, r.order_id;

title: Table Daily_sales(date_id Date, Product Text, Qty Integer) Has…
question: Table daily_sales(date_id DATE, product TEXT, qty INTEGER) has multiple rows per date. Reshape it into a wide pivot so each date is ONE row with columns date_id, apples, bananas, cherries (lower-case). Missing combinations become 0. Order by date_id.
schema:
CREATE TABLE daily_sales (date_id DATE, product TEXT, qty INTEGER);
INSERT INTO daily_sales VALUES
  ('2025-06-01','apples',10),
  ('2025-06-01','bananas',3),
  ('2025-06-02','apples',7),
  ('2025-06-02','cherries',5),
  ('2025-06-03','bananas',8);
solution:
SELECT date_id,
       COALESCE(SUM(qty) FILTER (WHERE product = 'apples'),0)::int   AS apples,
       COALESCE(SUM(qty) FILTER (WHERE product = 'bananas'),0)::int  AS bananas,
       COALESCE(SUM(qty) FILTER (WHERE product = 'cherries'),0)::int AS cherries
FROM daily_sales
GROUP BY date_id
ORDER BY date_id;

title: Table Tasks(task_id Int, Subtask_count Int) Declares That Task X Has…
question: Table tasks(task_id INT, subtask_count INT) declares that task X has subtasks numbered 1..subtask_count. Table executed(task_id INT, subtask_id INT) records which subtasks have run. Return the (task_id, subtask_id) pairs that have NOT been executed, ordered by task_id, subtask_id. Generate the universe of expected subtasks using a recursive CTE -- do NOT use generate_series.
solution:
WITH RECURSIVE expand AS (
  SELECT task_id, subtask_count AS subtask_id FROM tasks
  UNION ALL
  SELECT task_id, subtask_id - 1 FROM expand WHERE subtask_id > 1
)
SELECT e.task_id, e.subtask_id
FROM expand e
LEFT JOIN executed x
  ON x.task_id = e.task_id AND x.subtask_id = e.subtask_id
WHERE x.task_id IS NULL
ORDER BY e.task_id, e.subtask_id;

title: Table Employees(employee_id Int, Employee_name Text, Manager_id Int)…
question: Table employees(employee_id INT, employee_name TEXT, manager_id INT) is a manager hierarchy where manager_id is the employee's direct manager. Return EVERY employee (excluding employee 1 themself) who reports DIRECTLY OR TRANSITIVELY to employee_id 1. Output employee_id, employee_name ordered by employee_id. Use a recursive CTE -- the hierarchy depth is unknown in advance.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (employee_id INTEGER, employee_name TEXT, manager_id INTEGER);
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Boss',NULL),
  (2,'V1',1),
  (3,'V2',1),
  (4,'M1',2),
  (5,'M2',2),
  (6,'M3',3),
  (7,'IC1',4),
  (8,'IC2',5),
  (9,'Outsider',NULL),
  (10,'OutsiderReport',9);
solution:
WITH RECURSIVE downstream AS (
  SELECT employee_id, employee_name FROM employees WHERE manager_id = 1
  UNION ALL
  SELECT e.employee_id, e.employee_name
  FROM employees e
  JOIN downstream d ON e.manager_id = d.employee_id
)
SELECT employee_id, employee_name
FROM downstream
ORDER BY employee_id;

title: Two Tables, Failed(fail_date Date) AND Succeeded(success_date Date),…
question: Two tables, failed(fail_date DATE) and succeeded(success_date DATE), record per-day outcomes. Dates may appear in only one table. Build a 'state-changes' report: every maximal contiguous run of days that share the same status. Output columns period_state ('failed' or 'succeeded'), start_date, end_date. Restrict to dates in calendar year 2025. Order by start_date. Each date appears in at most one of the two tables.
solution:
WITH unioned AS (
  SELECT fail_date AS d, 'failed' AS state FROM failed
  UNION ALL
  SELECT success_date, 'succeeded' FROM succeeded
),
filtered AS (
  SELECT d, state FROM unioned WHERE d BETWEEN DATE '2025-01-01' AND DATE '2025-12-31'
),
grouped AS (
  SELECT d, state,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY d)
           - ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY state ORDER BY d) AS grp
  FROM filtered
)
SELECT state AS period_state, MIN(d) AS start_date, MAX(d) AS end_date
FROM grouped
GROUP BY state, grp
ORDER BY start_date;

title: Table Activity(player_id Int, Event_date Date, Games_played Int) Has…
question: Table activity(player_id INT, event_date DATE, games_played INT) has one row per player per day. Return a running total of games played by each player up to and including that date. Columns: player_id, event_date, games_played_so_far. Order by player_id, event_date.
schema:
CREATE TABLE activity (player_id INTEGER, event_date DATE, games_played INTEGER);
INSERT INTO activity VALUES
  (1,'2025-01-01',5),
  (1,'2025-01-02',3),
  (1,'2025-01-04',2),
  (2,'2025-01-01',10),
  (2,'2025-01-03',4);
solution:
SELECT player_id, event_date,
       SUM(games_played) OVER (PARTITION BY player_id ORDER BY event_date
                                ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)::int AS games_played_so_far
FROM activity
ORDER BY player_id, event_date;

title: Table Employees(emp_id Int, Event_day Date, In_time Int, Out_time…
question: Table employees(emp_id INT, event_day DATE, in_time INT, out_time INT) records minutes-since-midnight for clock-in and clock-out. An employee may clock in/out multiple times per day. Return total minutes worked per (day, employee). Columns: event_day, emp_id, total_time. Order by event_day, emp_id.
solution:
SELECT event_day, emp_id, SUM(out_time - in_time)::int AS total_time
FROM employees
GROUP BY event_day, emp_id
ORDER BY event_day, emp_id;

title: Tables Seller(seller_id Int, Seller_name Text) AND Orders(order_id…
question: Tables seller(seller_id INT, seller_name TEXT) and orders(order_id INT, seller_id INT, sale_date DATE). Return seller_names that had NO orders during calendar year 2025. Sort by seller_name. Note: a seller might have orders in OTHER years -- those are still considered 'no 2025 sales'.
solution:
SELECT s.seller_name
FROM seller s
LEFT JOIN orders o
  ON o.seller_id = s.seller_id
 AND o.sale_date >= DATE '2025-01-01'
 AND o.sale_date <  DATE '2026-01-01'
WHERE o.seller_id IS NULL
ORDER BY s.seller_name;

title: Tables Enrolled_employee(emp_id Int, Name Text) And…
question: Tables enrolled_employee(emp_id INT, name TEXT) and paid_salary(emp_id INT, paid NUMERIC). The two tables should normally be in sync. Find employee IDs that appear in exactly ONE of the two tables (employees missing from one side). Return one column emp_id ordered ascending. Use a FULL OUTER JOIN.
solution:
SELECT COALESCE(e.emp_id, p.emp_id) AS emp_id
FROM enrolled_employee e
FULL OUTER JOIN paid_salary p ON e.emp_id = p.emp_id
WHERE e.emp_id IS NULL OR p.emp_id IS NULL
ORDER BY emp_id;

title: Tables Actor_director(actor_id Int, Director_id Int, Movie_id Int)
question: Tables actor_director(actor_id INT, director_id INT, movie_id INT). Return pairs of (actor_id, director_id) who have worked together on AT LEAST 3 distinct movies. Columns: actor_id, director_id, ordered by both ascending.
schema:
CREATE TABLE actor_director (actor_id INTEGER, director_id INTEGER, movie_id INTEGER);
INSERT INTO actor_director VALUES
  (1,1,10),(1,1,11),(1,1,12),
  (1,2,20),(1,2,21),
  (2,1,30),(2,1,31),(2,1,32),(2,1,33),
  (3,3,40),(3,3,41),(3,3,42),
  (3,3,42);
solution:
SELECT actor_id, director_id
FROM actor_director
GROUP BY actor_id, director_id
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT movie_id) >= 3
ORDER BY actor_id, director_id;

title: Table Cinema(seat_id Int, Free Char(1)) Lists Every Seat in a Row…
question: Table cinema(seat_id INT, free CHAR(1)) lists every seat in a row with a 'Y'/'N' availability flag. Seat IDs are consecutive integers but not necessarily starting at 1. Return all seat_ids that are FREE AND have at least one ADJACENT free seat (immediately before or after). Order by seat_id. No duplicate output.
solution:
SELECT DISTINCT a.seat_id
FROM cinema a
JOIN cinema b
  ON ABS(a.seat_id - b.seat_id) = 1
 AND a.free = 'Y' AND b.free = 'Y'
ORDER BY a.seat_id;

title: Tables Friendship(user1_id Int, User2_id Int) (mutual; Stored Only…
question: Tables friendship(user1_id INT, user2_id INT) (mutual; stored only one direction) and likes(user_id INT, page_id INT). Recommend pages to user 1: every page that any of user 1's FRIENDS has liked, EXCLUDING pages user 1 already likes. Output one column recommended_page of DISTINCT page ids, ordered ascending.
solution:
WITH friends AS (
  SELECT user2_id AS friend FROM friendship WHERE user1_id = 1
  UNION
  SELECT user1_id        FROM friendship WHERE user2_id = 1
)
SELECT DISTINCT l.page_id AS recommended_page
FROM friends f
JOIN likes l ON l.user_id = f.friend
WHERE l.page_id NOT IN (SELECT page_id FROM likes WHERE user_id = 1)
ORDER BY recommended_page;

title: Table Activity(player_id Int, Device_id Int, Event_date Date)…
question: Table activity(player_id INT, device_id INT, event_date DATE) records every game session. For each player, return the device_id they used on their EARLIEST event_date. If a player used multiple devices on that earliest date, return the SMALLEST device_id. Columns: player_id, device_id, ordered by player_id.
schema:
CREATE TABLE activity (player_id INTEGER, device_id INTEGER, event_date DATE);
INSERT INTO activity VALUES
  (1,10,'2025-01-01'),
  (1,20,'2025-01-02'),
  (2,30,'2025-02-01'),
  (2,40,'2025-02-01'),
  (3,50,'2025-03-05');
solution:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT player_id, device_id, event_date,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY player_id ORDER BY event_date, device_id) AS rn
  FROM activity
)
SELECT player_id, device_id
FROM ranked
WHERE rn = 1
ORDER BY player_id;

title: Table Match(match_id Int, Host_team Int, Guest_team Int, Host_goals…
question: Table match(match_id INT, host_team INT, guest_team INT, host_goals INT, guest_goals INT). Scoring: a win = 3 pts, a draw = 1 pt, a loss = 0 pts (a team gets points for each match it played, whether as host or guest). Compute total points per team. Output columns: team_id, points, ordered by points DESC then team_id ASC. Include teams that never won (zero/draw-only) but EXCLUDE team ids that never played.
schema:
CREATE TABLE match (match_id INTEGER, host_team INTEGER, guest_team INTEGER, host_goals INTEGER, guest_goals INTEGER);
INSERT INTO match VALUES
  (1,10,20,3,1),
  (2,10,30,2,2),
  (3,20,30,0,4),
  (4,40,10,1,1),
  (5,30,20,1,0);
solution:
WITH legs AS (
  SELECT host_team AS team,
         CASE WHEN host_goals > guest_goals THEN 3
              WHEN host_goals = guest_goals THEN 1
              ELSE 0 END AS pts
  FROM match
  UNION ALL
  SELECT guest_team,
         CASE WHEN guest_goals > host_goals THEN 3
              WHEN host_goals = guest_goals THEN 1
              ELSE 0 END
  FROM match
)
SELECT team AS team_id, SUM(pts)::int AS points
FROM legs
GROUP BY team
ORDER BY points DESC, team_id;

title: Table Products(product_id Int, Store1 Numeric, Store2 Numeric,…
question: Table products(product_id INT, store1 NUMERIC, store2 NUMERIC, store3 NUMERIC) stores prices for the SAME product in three stores. Some store prices can be NULL (the product isn't sold there). UNPIVOT into long format: columns product_id, store, price. Skip rows where price IS NULL. Order by product_id, store.
solution:
SELECT product_id, 'store1' AS store, store1 AS price FROM products WHERE store1 IS NOT NULL
UNION ALL
SELECT product_id, 'store2',        store2        FROM products WHERE store2 IS NOT NULL
UNION ALL
SELECT product_id, 'store3',        store3        FROM products WHERE store3 IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY product_id, store;

title: Table Exam(exam_id Int, Student_id Int, Score Int) And…
question: Table exam(exam_id INT, student_id INT, score INT) and student(student_id INT, student_name TEXT). A 'quiet' student is one who is NEVER the maximum scorer and NEVER the minimum scorer in any exam they took -- AND has taken at least one exam. Return student_id, student_name, ordered by student_id. If two students tie for top in an exam, they BOTH disqualify.
solution:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT exam_id, student_id,
         DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY exam_id ORDER BY score DESC) AS rk_high,
         DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY exam_id ORDER BY score ASC)  AS rk_low
  FROM exam
),
loud AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT student_id FROM ranked WHERE rk_high = 1 OR rk_low = 1
)
SELECT s.student_id, s.student_name
FROM student s
JOIN exam e ON e.student_id = s.student_id
WHERE s.student_id NOT IN (SELECT student_id FROM loud)
GROUP BY s.student_id, s.student_name
ORDER BY s.student_id;

title: Tables Country(country_id Int, Country_name Text, Country_code…
question: Tables country(country_id INT, country_name TEXT, country_code TEXT), person(person_id INT, phone_number TEXT) -- phone numbers begin with the country_code followed by '-'. calls(caller_id INT, callee_id INT, duration INT). Return country names where the AVERAGE call duration of calls touching that country (either caller or callee in that country) STRICTLY exceeds the GLOBAL average across all call legs. Output one column country_name ordered ascending.
solution:
WITH legs AS (
  SELECT caller_id AS pid, duration FROM calls
  UNION ALL
  SELECT callee_id, duration FROM calls
),
tagged AS (
  SELECT c.country_name, l.duration
  FROM legs l
  JOIN person p ON p.person_id = l.pid
  JOIN country c ON split_part(p.phone_number, '-', 1) = c.country_code
)
SELECT country_name
FROM tagged
GROUP BY country_name
HAVING AVG(duration) > (SELECT AVG(duration) FROM legs)
ORDER BY country_name;

title: Table Employee(emp_id Int, Name Text, Dept_id Int, Salary Int)
question: Table employee(emp_id INT, name TEXT, dept_id INT, salary INT). For each department, return the row(s) where the salary equals the department's MAX salary. Use a tuple-match subquery (no window functions). Output columns: dept_id, name, salary. Multiple winners per department all appear. Order by dept_id, then name.

title: Table Customers(customer_id Int, Name Text) AND Orders(order_id Int,…
question: Table customers(customer_id INT, name TEXT) and orders(order_id INT, customer_id INT, order_date DATE). Return the customer (or customers, if tied) with the LARGEST number of orders. Columns: customer_id, name, order_count. Order by customer_id. Use a window function so ties surface naturally.
solution:
WITH counts AS (
  SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS order_count
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY customer_id
),
ranked AS (
  SELECT customer_id, order_count,
         RANK() OVER (ORDER BY order_count DESC) AS rk
  FROM counts
)
SELECT r.customer_id, c.name, r.order_count::int AS order_count
FROM ranked r
JOIN customers c ON c.customer_id = r.customer_id
WHERE rk = 1
ORDER BY r.customer_id;

title: Table Student_courses(student_id Int, Course_id Int, Grade Int)
question: Table student_courses(student_id INT, course_id INT, grade INT). For each student, return the course where they got the HIGHEST grade. Ties: prefer the SMALLEST course_id. Columns: student_id, course_id, grade, ordered by student_id.
solution:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT student_id, course_id, grade,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY student_id ORDER BY grade DESC, course_id ASC) AS rn
  FROM student_courses
)
SELECT student_id, course_id, grade
FROM ranked
WHERE rn = 1
ORDER BY student_id;

title: Table Users(user_id Int, Email Text)
question: Table users(user_id INT, email TEXT). Return DUPLICATE email addresses (emails that appear two or more times) -- compare case-INSENSITIVELY (so 'A@x.com' and 'a@x.com' are the same). Columns: email_lower, occurrences. Order by email_lower.
schema:
CREATE TABLE users (user_id INTEGER, email TEXT);
INSERT INTO users VALUES
  (1,'a@x.com'),
  (2,'A@X.com'),
  (3,'b@x.com'),
  (4,'c@x.com'),
  (5,'C@X.COM'),
  (6,'C@x.com');
solution:
SELECT LOWER(email) AS email_lower, COUNT(*)::int AS occurrences
FROM users
GROUP BY LOWER(email)
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
ORDER BY email_lower;

title: Table Events(event_id Int, Ts Timestamp) Logs User Events
question: Table events(event_id INT, ts TIMESTAMP) logs user events. Bucket each event into a 15-minute window starting at the top of the hour. For each bucket, return the event count. Format the bucket as the START timestamp of the 15-minute window. Columns: bucket_start, event_count. Order by bucket_start.
solution:
SELECT date_trunc('hour', ts)
       + (EXTRACT(MINUTE FROM ts)::int / 15) * INTERVAL '15 minutes' AS bucket_start,
       COUNT(*)::int AS event_count
FROM events
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1;

title: Table Daily_leads(date_id Date, Make_name Text, Lead_id Int,…
question: Table daily_leads(date_id DATE, make_name TEXT, lead_id INT, partner_id INT). For each (date, make), return the number of DISTINCT leads and the number of DISTINCT partners. Columns: date_id, make_name, unique_leads, unique_partners, ordered by date_id, make_name.
schema:
CREATE TABLE daily_leads (date_id DATE, make_name TEXT, lead_id INTEGER, partner_id INTEGER);
INSERT INTO daily_leads VALUES
  ('2025-01-01','Toyota',1,10),
  ('2025-01-01','Toyota',1,10),
  ('2025-01-01','Toyota',2,11),
  ('2025-01-01','Honda',5,20),
  ('2025-01-02','Toyota',1,12);
solution:
SELECT date_id, make_name,
       COUNT(DISTINCT lead_id)::int    AS unique_leads,
       COUNT(DISTINCT partner_id)::int AS unique_partners
FROM daily_leads
GROUP BY date_id, make_name
ORDER BY date_id, make_name;

title: Table Login(user_id Int, Login_date Date) Records Distinct Daily Logins
question: Table login(user_id INT, login_date DATE) records distinct daily logins. Find users who have logged in on 3 OR MORE CONSECUTIVE calendar days. Use a LAG-BASED 3-row pattern: check whether (this_date - 1) and (this_date - 2) both appear as preceding logins for the same user. Return distinct user_ids, ordered ascending.
solution:
WITH dt AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT user_id, login_date FROM login
),
shifted AS (
  SELECT user_id, login_date,
         LAG(login_date, 1) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY login_date) AS d1,
         LAG(login_date, 2) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY login_date) AS d2
  FROM dt
)
SELECT DISTINCT user_id
FROM shifted
WHERE login_date - d1 = 1 AND login_date - d2 = 2
ORDER BY user_id;

title: Table Orders(order_id Int, Customer_id Int, Product_id Int,…
question: Table orders(order_id INT, customer_id INT, product_id INT, order_date DATE). For each customer, return the product they ORDERED MOST OFTEN. If a customer has multiple products tied for most-ordered, return ALL tied products. Columns: customer_id, product_id, times, ordered by customer_id, product_id. The same customer-product combo can appear on different dates.
solution:
WITH counts AS (
  SELECT customer_id, product_id, COUNT(*) AS times
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY customer_id, product_id
),
ranked AS (
  SELECT customer_id, product_id, times,
         RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY times DESC) AS rk
  FROM counts
)
SELECT customer_id, product_id, times::int AS times
FROM ranked
WHERE rk = 1
ORDER BY customer_id, product_id;

title: Table Logins(user_id Int, Time_stamp Timestamp)
question: Table logins(user_id INT, time_stamp TIMESTAMP). Return the LATEST login timestamp for each user that occurred during the year 2025. Columns: user_id, last_stamp. Users with no 2025 logins must NOT appear. Order by user_id.
solution:
SELECT user_id, MAX(time_stamp) AS last_stamp
FROM logins
WHERE time_stamp >= TIMESTAMP '2025-01-01' AND time_stamp < TIMESTAMP '2026-01-01'
GROUP BY user_id
ORDER BY user_id;

title: Table Employees(employee_id Int, Name Text, Salary Numeric)
question: Table employees(employee_id INT, name TEXT, salary NUMERIC). Pay a 'special bonus' equal to the employee's salary IF AND ONLY IF: (a) employee_id is ODD AND (b) name does not start with letter 'M' (case-sensitive). Everyone else gets bonus = 0. Return columns: employee_id, bonus, ordered by employee_id.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employees (employee_id INTEGER, name TEXT, salary NUMERIC);
INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,'Alice',100),
  (2,'Bob',200),
  (3,'Maria',300),
  (4,'Mike',400),
  (5,'Cara',500),
  (6,'Diana',600),
  (7,'Mira',700),
  (9,'mike',900);
solution:
SELECT employee_id,
       CASE WHEN employee_id % 2 = 1 AND name NOT LIKE 'M%' THEN salary ELSE 0 END AS bonus
FROM employees
ORDER BY employee_id;

title: Reconcile by Computing Calculated_balance = Sum(ord_amount) -…
question: Schema: customer(cust_code, recorded_outstanding) (the snapshot value stored on the customer row), orders(ord_num, cust_code, ord_amount), payment(pay_id, cust_code, paid_amount). Reconcile by computing calculated_balance = SUM(ord_amount) - SUM(paid_amount) and discrepancy = recorded_outstanding - calculated_balance. Return only customers whose discrepancy is non-zero (i.e. their stored value disagrees with the derived value). Return cust_code, recorded_outstanding, total_orders, total_payments, calculated_balance, discrepancy. NULLs from missing orders/payments treated as 0. Order by ABS(discrepancy) DESC, then cust_code.
solution:
WITH o AS (
  SELECT cust_code, SUM(ord_amount) AS total_orders FROM orders GROUP BY cust_code
),
p AS (
  SELECT cust_code, SUM(paid_amount) AS total_payments FROM payment GROUP BY cust_code
)
SELECT c.cust_code,
       c.recorded_outstanding,
       COALESCE(o.total_orders,0)   AS total_orders,
       COALESCE(p.total_payments,0) AS total_payments,
       COALESCE(o.total_orders,0) - COALESCE(p.total_payments,0) AS calculated_balance,
       c.recorded_outstanding - (COALESCE(o.total_orders,0) - COALESCE(p.total_payments,0)) AS discrepancy
FROM customer c
LEFT JOIN o ON o.cust_code = c.cust_code
LEFT JOIN p ON p.cust_code = c.cust_code
WHERE c.recorded_outstanding - (COALESCE(o.total_orders,0) - COALESCE(p.total_payments,0)) <> 0
ORDER BY ABS(c.recorded_outstanding - (COALESCE(o.total_orders,0) - COALESCE(p.total_payments,0))) DESC, c.cust_code;

title: Purchase(customer_id, Product_id) Records Who Bought What…
question: Schema: product(product_id, name) is the FULL product catalog. purchase(customer_id, product_id) records who bought what (duplicates allowed). Find customers who have bought EVERY product in the catalog at least once (classic relational division). Return customer_id, ordered ascending. Edge case: if the catalog is empty, every customer trivially qualifies.
solution:
SELECT customer_id
FROM purchase
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT product_id) = (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM product)
ORDER BY customer_id;

title: Enrollment(student_id, Course_id, Grade) Records Enrollments Where…
question: Schema: course(course_id, name) lists all required courses. enrollment(student_id, course_id, grade) records enrollments where grade is a letter ('A','B','C','D','F') or NULL (in progress). Find students who have COMPLETED (i.e. grade IS NOT NULL AND grade <> 'F') every required course. A student who has merely enrolled in but not passed a course does not qualify. Return student_id, ordered ascending.
solution:
SELECT student_id
FROM enrollment
WHERE grade IS NOT NULL AND grade <> 'F'
GROUP BY student_id
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT course_id) = (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM course)
ORDER BY student_id;

title: Customers Who Have Never Placed an Order
question: Schema: customer(cust_code, cust_name, cust_country) and orders(ord_num, cust_code). Return customers who have NEVER placed an order. Use the LEFT JOIN / IS NULL anti-join pattern (NOT IN is unsafe with NULLs and NOT EXISTS is fine but not what we want to test here). Return cust_code, cust_name, cust_country, ordered by cust_code.
solution:
SELECT c.cust_code, c.cust_name, c.cust_country
FROM customer c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.cust_code = c.cust_code
WHERE o.cust_code IS NULL
ORDER BY c.cust_code;

title: For Each Working Area, Compute Num_agents, Avg_commission (rounded…
question: Schema: agent(agent_code, working_area, commission). For each working area, compute num_agents, avg_commission (rounded to 4 decimals), min_commission, max_commission, and commission_spread = max-min. Then RANK areas by avg_commission DESC (ties get the same rank, no gaps — use DENSE_RANK). Return only areas where num_agents >= 1 (i.e. all of them) with columns working_area, num_agents, avg_commission, min_commission, max_commission, commission_spread, rk, ordered by rk then working_area.
schema:
CREATE TABLE agent (agent_code TEXT, working_area TEXT, commission NUMERIC);
INSERT INTO agent VALUES
  ('A001','Bangalore',0.14),
  ('A002','Mumbai',0.11),
  ('A003','Bangalore',0.13),
  ('A004','Toronto',0.15),
  ('A005','London',0.13),
  ('A006','London',0.15),
  ('A007','Bangalore',0.15),
  ('A008','New York',0.12),
  ('A009','Hampshair',0.11),
  ('A010','Chennai',0.14),
  ('A011','Brisban',0.13),
  ('A012','San Jose',0.12);
solution:
WITH g AS (
  SELECT working_area,
         COUNT(*) AS num_agents,
         ROUND(AVG(commission)::numeric, 4) AS avg_commission,
         MIN(commission) AS min_commission,
         MAX(commission) AS max_commission,
         MAX(commission) - MIN(commission) AS commission_spread
  FROM agent
  GROUP BY working_area
)
SELECT working_area, num_agents, avg_commission, min_commission, max_commission, commission_spread,
       DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY avg_commission DESC) AS rk
FROM g
ORDER BY rk, working_area;

title: For Each Agent, Return the Top 2 Orders by Amount Desc
type: SQL / Top-N Per Group with Multi-key Tie-break
subtopic: Top-N Per Group with Multi-key Tie-break
question: Schema: orders(ord_num, agent_code, ord_amount, ord_date). For each agent, return the TOP 2 orders by amount DESC. Tie-break: earlier ord_date wins; further ties broken by lower ord_num. If an agent has only one order, return that one row. Return agent_code, ord_num, ord_date, ord_amount, rk, ordered by agent_code, rk. Use ROW_NUMBER (no ties surface a duplicate rank).
solution:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT agent_code, ord_num, ord_date, ord_amount,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY agent_code ORDER BY ord_amount DESC, ord_date ASC, ord_num ASC) AS rk
  FROM orders
)
SELECT agent_code, ord_num, ord_date, ord_amount, rk
FROM ranked
WHERE rk <= 2
ORDER BY agent_code, rk;

title: Product(product_id, Category_id, Sales) Links Products to Leaf…
question: Schema: category(category_id, name, parent_id) forms a tree (parent_id is NULL at the root). product(product_id, category_id, sales) links products to LEAF categories only. For every category in the tree (leaf AND internal), compute the SUM of sales of all products in its subtree (itself + every descendant). Return category_id, name, total_sales, ordered by category_id. Categories with no products in their subtree get 0.
schema:
CREATE TABLE category (category_id INT, name TEXT, parent_id INT);
INSERT INTO category VALUES
  (1,'All',NULL),
  (2,'Electronics',1),
  (3,'Phones',2),
  (4,'Laptops',2),
  (5,'Apparel',1),
  (6,'Shoes',5),
  (7,'Shirts',5);
CREATE TABLE product (product_id INT, category_id INT, sales NUMERIC);
INSERT INTO product VALUES
  (101,3,500),(102,3,300),
  (103,4,1000),
  (104,6,200),
  (105,7,400),(106,7,100);
solution:
WITH RECURSIVE descendants(ancestor_id, descendant_id) AS (
  SELECT category_id, category_id FROM category
  UNION ALL
  SELECT d.ancestor_id, c.category_id
  FROM descendants d JOIN category c ON c.parent_id = d.descendant_id
)
SELECT c.category_id, c.name,
       COALESCE(SUM(p.sales), 0) AS total_sales
FROM category c
LEFT JOIN descendants d ON d.ancestor_id = c.category_id
LEFT JOIN product p ON p.category_id = d.descendant_id
GROUP BY c.category_id, c.name
ORDER BY c.category_id;

title: For Every Employee, Return the Full Path from the Ceo Down to Them…
question: Schema: employee(emp_id, name, manager_id) where the CEO has manager_id = NULL. For every employee, return the FULL path from the CEO down to them as a >-separated string of names, plus their depth (CEO is 0). Return emp_id, name, depth, path, ordered by depth then emp_id.
solution:
WITH RECURSIVE tree AS (
  SELECT emp_id, name, manager_id, 0 AS depth, name AS path
  FROM employee WHERE manager_id IS NULL
  UNION ALL
  SELECT e.emp_id, e.name, e.manager_id, t.depth + 1, t.path || ' > ' || e.name
  FROM employee e JOIN tree t ON e.manager_id = t.emp_id
)
SELECT emp_id, name, depth, path FROM tree ORDER BY depth, emp_id;

title: From Node 1, Return Every Node Reachable Via Any Directed Path…
question: Schema: edge(src, dst) is a DIRECTED graph that MAY contain cycles. From node 1, return every node reachable via any directed path (excluding node 1 itself), with the MINIMUM hop count to reach it. The traversal must terminate even if the graph has cycles — track the visited path. Return node, min_hops, ordered by node.
solution:
WITH RECURSIVE walk(node, hops, path) AS (
  SELECT 1, 0, ARRAY[1]
  UNION ALL
  SELECT e.dst, w.hops + 1, w.path || e.dst
  FROM walk w JOIN edge e ON e.src = w.node
  WHERE NOT (e.dst = ANY(w.path))
)
SELECT node, MIN(hops) AS min_hops
FROM walk
WHERE node <> 1
GROUP BY node
ORDER BY node;

title: The Graph Is a Dag (no Cycles), Rooted at 'Bike'
question: Schema: bom(parent, child, qty) describes how each parent assembly is composed of child parts (with quantities). The graph is a DAG (no cycles), rooted at 'Bike'. For ONE bike, recursively compute the total quantity of EACH leaf material required. A leaf is any child that never appears as a parent. Return leaf_material, total_qty, ordered by leaf_material.
schema:
CREATE TABLE bom (parent TEXT, child TEXT, qty NUMERIC);
INSERT INTO bom VALUES
  ('Bike','Frame',1),
