  ('Bike','Wheel',2),
  ('Bike','Handlebar',1),
  ('Frame','SteelTube',5),
  ('Frame','Bolt',12),
  ('Wheel','Tire',1),
  ('Wheel','Spoke',32),
  ('Wheel','Bolt',6),
  ('Handlebar','Grip',2),
  ('Handlebar','SteelTube',1);
solution:
WITH RECURSIVE explode(part, qty) AS (
  SELECT child, qty FROM bom WHERE parent = 'Bike'
  UNION ALL
  SELECT b.child, e.qty * b.qty
  FROM explode e JOIN bom b ON b.parent = e.part
),
leaves AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT child FROM bom
  EXCEPT
  SELECT DISTINCT parent FROM bom
)
SELECT e.part AS leaf_material, SUM(e.qty) AS total_qty
FROM explode e JOIN leaves l ON l.child = e.part
GROUP BY e.part
ORDER BY e.part;

title: Aggregate Revenue by Fiscal Quarter, Where the Fiscal Year Starts On…
question: Schema: revenue(d, amount) — daily revenue. Aggregate revenue by FISCAL QUARTER, where the fiscal year STARTS ON APRIL 1 (common UK/India/Japan calendar). FQ1 = Apr–Jun, FQ2 = Jul–Sep, FQ3 = Oct–Dec, FQ4 = Jan–Mar of the FOLLOWING calendar year. fiscal_year is the calendar year in which the fiscal year STARTS (so revenue in March 2026 belongs to fiscal_year 2025, FQ4). Return fiscal_year, fiscal_quarter, total_amount, ordered by fiscal_year, fiscal_quarter.
solution:
SELECT
  CASE WHEN EXTRACT(MONTH FROM d) >= 4 THEN EXTRACT(YEAR FROM d)::INT
       ELSE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM d)::INT - 1
  END AS fiscal_year,
  CASE
    WHEN EXTRACT(MONTH FROM d) BETWEEN 4 AND 6  THEN 1
    WHEN EXTRACT(MONTH FROM d) BETWEEN 7 AND 9  THEN 2
    WHEN EXTRACT(MONTH FROM d) BETWEEN 10 AND 12 THEN 3
    ELSE 4
  END AS fiscal_quarter,
  SUM(amount) AS total_amount
FROM revenue
GROUP BY 1, 2
ORDER BY fiscal_year, fiscal_quarter;

title: Pivot to One Row per Respondent, with One Column per Question…
question: Schema: survey(question_id, respondent_id, answer). Pivot to one row per respondent, with one column per question containing that respondent's answer. The set of questions is small and known: q1, q2, q3. Use conditional aggregation. If a respondent didn't answer a question, the cell should be NULL. Return respondent_id, q1, q2, q3 ordered by respondent_id.
solution:
SELECT respondent_id,
       MAX(CASE WHEN question_id = 'q1' THEN answer END) AS q1,
       MAX(CASE WHEN question_id = 'q2' THEN answer END) AS q2,
       MAX(CASE WHEN question_id = 'q3' THEN answer END) AS q3
FROM survey
GROUP BY respondent_id
ORDER BY respondent_id;

title: For Each Agent, Compute the Total Order Amount Placed by Their…
question: Schema: agent(agent_code, commission), customer(cust_code, agent_code), orders(ord_num, cust_code, ord_amount). For each agent, compute the TOTAL order amount placed by THEIR customers, the agent's commission earned (total * commission rate), and the AVERAGE order size across those customers (rounded to 2 dp). Return agent_code, num_orders, total_amount, commission_earned, avg_order, ordered by total_amount DESC then agent_code. Exclude agents with zero orders.
solution:
SELECT a.agent_code,
       COUNT(o.ord_num) AS num_orders,
       SUM(o.ord_amount) AS total_amount,
       ROUND((SUM(o.ord_amount) * a.commission)::numeric, 2) AS commission_earned,
       ROUND(AVG(o.ord_amount)::numeric, 2) AS avg_order
FROM agent a
JOIN customer c ON c.agent_code = a.agent_code
JOIN orders o ON o.cust_code = c.cust_code
GROUP BY a.agent_code, a.commission
ORDER BY total_amount DESC, a.agent_code;

title: Employees Who Earn More Than the Average Salary of Their Own Department
question: Schema: salary(emp_id, dept, amount). Find employees who earn MORE than the AVERAGE salary OF THEIR OWN DEPARTMENT. Return emp_id, dept, amount, dept_avg, ordered by dept then emp_id. Round dept_avg to 2 decimals.
solution:
WITH d AS (
  SELECT dept, AVG(amount) AS dept_avg FROM salary GROUP BY dept
)
SELECT s.emp_id, s.dept, s.amount, ROUND(d.dept_avg::numeric, 2) AS dept_avg
FROM salary s JOIN d ON d.dept = s.dept
WHERE s.amount > d.dept_avg
ORDER BY s.dept, s.emp_id;

title: For Each Row, Return D, V, Sum_to_date, Count_to_date,…
question: Schema: metric(d, v) is a daily metric (one row per day). For each row, return d, v, sum_to_date, count_to_date, avg_to_date_2dp where each metric is computed over all rows with d <= current d INCLUDING the current row. Round avg_to_date_2dp to 2 decimals. Use window functions (the modern, correct approach), NOT a self-join (which the old SQL pre-2003 approach would have used).
solution:
SELECT d, v,
       SUM(v)   OVER (ORDER BY d ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) AS sum_to_date,
       COUNT(*) OVER (ORDER BY d ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) AS count_to_date,
       ROUND(AVG(v) OVER (ORDER BY d ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING)::numeric, 2) AS avg_to_date_2dp
FROM metric
ORDER BY d;

title: For Each Customer, Return Their Largest Order (by Amount, Ties…
type: SQL / Top N Per Group with Outer Join
subtopic: Top N Per Group with Outer Join
question: Schema: customer(cust_code, cust_name) and orders(ord_num, cust_code, ord_amount, ord_date). For each customer, return their LARGEST order (by amount, ties broken by earliest date, then ord_num). For customers with no orders, return them with NULL ord fields. Return cust_code, cust_name, ord_num, ord_amount, ord_date, ordered by cust_code.
solution:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT cust_code, ord_num, ord_amount, ord_date,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY cust_code ORDER BY ord_amount DESC, ord_date ASC, ord_num ASC) AS rk
  FROM orders
)
SELECT c.cust_code, c.cust_name, r.ord_num, r.ord_amount, r.ord_date
FROM customer c
LEFT JOIN ranked r ON r.cust_code = c.cust_code AND r.rk = 1
ORDER BY c.cust_code;

title: For Each User, Return Their First AND Last Event (by Ts) in One Row
type: SQL / First and Last Per Group
question: Schema: event(user_id, ts, action). For each user, return their FIRST and LAST event (by ts) in ONE row. Return user_id, first_ts, first_action, last_ts, last_action, ordered by user_id. Use FIRST_VALUE / LAST_VALUE correctly with explicit frames (the default RANGE frame trap).
schema:
CREATE TABLE event (user_id INT, ts TIMESTAMP, action TEXT);
INSERT INTO event VALUES
  (1,'2025-01-01 10:00','signup'),
  (1,'2025-01-02 11:00','view'),
  (1,'2025-01-05 12:00','purchase'),
  (2,'2025-02-01 09:00','signup'),
  (2,'2025-02-10 14:00','churn');
solution:
SELECT DISTINCT
  user_id,
  FIRST_VALUE(ts)     OVER w AS first_ts,
  FIRST_VALUE(action) OVER w AS first_action,
  LAST_VALUE(ts)      OVER w AS last_ts,
  LAST_VALUE(action)  OVER w AS last_action
FROM event
WINDOW w AS (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING)
ORDER BY user_id;

title: Group Events Into Sessions Where a New Session Begins After a Gap Of…
question: Schema: event(user_id, ts). Group events into sessions where a NEW session begins after a gap of MORE THAN 60 minutes from the previous event of the same user. Return user_id, session_id, session_start, session_end, event_count where session_id is the 1-based ordinal within each user. Order by user_id, session_id.
solution:
WITH flagged AS (
  SELECT user_id, ts,
         CASE WHEN ts - LAG(ts) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts) > INTERVAL '60 minutes'
              OR LAG(ts) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts) IS NULL
              THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS is_new
  FROM event
),
numbered AS (
  SELECT user_id, ts,
         SUM(is_new) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts) AS session_id
  FROM flagged
)
SELECT user_id, session_id,
       MIN(ts) AS session_start,
       MAX(ts) AS session_end,
       COUNT(*) AS event_count
FROM numbered
GROUP BY user_id, session_id
ORDER BY user_id, session_id;

title: Generate One Row per Calendar Day from 2025-01-01 Through 2025-01-10…
question: Generate one row per CALENDAR DAY from 2025-01-01 through 2025-01-10 inclusive, then LEFT JOIN to signup(user_id, signup_date) and return d, signups (count of signups on that date, 0 if none). Order by d. Use Postgres generate_series.
solution:
SELECT g.d::date AS d,
       COUNT(s.user_id) AS signups
FROM generate_series('2025-01-01'::date, '2025-01-10'::date, INTERVAL '1 day') AS g(d)
LEFT JOIN signup s ON s.signup_date = g.d
GROUP BY g.d
ORDER BY g.d;

title: The Median Value per Category Using PERCENTILE_CONT (interpolated…
question: Schema: score(category, value). Compute the MEDIAN value per category using PERCENTILE_CONT (interpolated median for even-count groups). Return category, median rounded to 2 decimals, ordered by category.
solution:
SELECT category,
       ROUND(PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY value)::numeric, 2) AS median
FROM score
GROUP BY category
ORDER BY category;

title: Each Customer's 2 Most-recent Orders Using a Postgres Lateral…
question: Schema: customer(cust_code, cust_name) and orders(ord_num, cust_code, ord_amount, ord_date). Return each customer's 2 most-recent orders using a Postgres LATERAL subquery (not a window function). Return cust_code, cust_name, ord_num, ord_amount, ord_date, ordered by cust_code, ord_date DESC, ord_num. Customers with no orders are omitted.
solution:
SELECT c.cust_code, c.cust_name, o.ord_num, o.ord_amount, o.ord_date
FROM customer c
JOIN LATERAL (
  SELECT ord_num, ord_amount, ord_date
  FROM orders
  WHERE cust_code = c.cust_code
  ORDER BY ord_date DESC, ord_num DESC
  LIMIT 2
) o ON TRUE
ORDER BY c.cust_code, o.ord_date DESC, o.ord_num;

title: For Each Row, Attach the Same-month-last-year Revenue AND Compute…
question: Schema: monthly(region, month_start, revenue) (one row per region per first-of-month). For each row, attach the SAME-MONTH-LAST-YEAR revenue and compute YoY growth percent (this/last - 1) * 100, rounded to 2 dp. If there is no same-month-last-year row, both prev_year_revenue and yoy_pct are NULL. Return region, month_start, revenue, prev_year_revenue, yoy_pct, ordered by region, month_start.
solution:
SELECT m.region, m.month_start, m.revenue,
       p.revenue AS prev_year_revenue,
       CASE WHEN p.revenue IS NULL OR p.revenue = 0 THEN NULL
            ELSE ROUND(((m.revenue / p.revenue - 1) * 100)::numeric, 2)
       END AS yoy_pct
FROM monthly m
LEFT JOIN monthly p
  ON p.region = m.region
 AND p.month_start = (m.month_start - INTERVAL '1 year')::date
ORDER BY m.region, m.month_start;

title: In One Row per Region, Return Total Revenue Split by Status
question: Schema: orders(order_id, status, region, amount). In ONE row per region, return total revenue split by status. Statuses are 'paid', 'pending', 'refunded'. Return region, paid, pending, refunded, total. Each status column is the SUM of amounts of orders in that status; missing combinations get 0 (not NULL). Order by region.
solution:
SELECT region,
       COALESCE(SUM(amount) FILTER (WHERE status = 'paid'), 0) AS paid,
       COALESCE(SUM(amount) FILTER (WHERE status = 'pending'), 0) AS pending,
       COALESCE(SUM(amount) FILTER (WHERE status = 'refunded'), 0) AS refunded,
       SUM(amount) AS total
FROM orders
GROUP BY region
ORDER BY region;

title: Correctly Returns Users Who Are Not Banned
question: Schema: users(user_id) and banned(user_id) where banned.user_id MAY contain NULLs (a known data-quality issue). Write a query that correctly returns users who are NOT banned. Demonstrate the NOT-IN NULL pitfall by ALSO returning a count column naive_count from the broken query alongside the correct count safe_count. Return ONE row with safe_count, naive_count.

title: For Each Item, Return the Comma-separated List of Tags Ordered By…
question: Schema: tag(item_id, tag, weight). For each item, return the comma-separated list of tags ORDERED BY weight DESC (so the most-important tag appears first), with NULL tags skipped. Return item_id, tag_list, ordered by item_id.
solution:
SELECT item_id,
       STRING_AGG(tag, ', ' ORDER BY weight DESC) AS tag_list
FROM tag
WHERE tag IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY item_id
ORDER BY item_id;

title: Monthly Cohort Retention: for Each Cohort (= Signup_month), AND For…
question: Schema: customer(cust_code, signup_month) (signup_month is a DATE first-of-month) and orders(ord_num, cust_code, ord_month) (ord_month also first-of-month). Compute monthly cohort retention: for each cohort (= signup_month), and for each subsequent month, how many customers from that cohort placed at least one order. Return cohort, ord_month, retained_users, sorted by cohort then ord_month. Only emit (cohort, ord_month) cells with retained_users >= 1.
solution:
SELECT c.signup_month AS cohort,
       o.ord_month,
       COUNT(DISTINCT c.cust_code) AS retained_users
FROM customer c
JOIN orders o ON o.cust_code = c.cust_code
GROUP BY c.signup_month, o.ord_month
ORDER BY cohort, o.ord_month;

title: For Each Employee, Return Their Longest Streak of Consecutive…
question: Schema: attendance(emp_id, work_date). For each employee, return their LONGEST streak of consecutive workdays (where 'consecutive' means calendar-adjacent dates). Ties: just return the longest length. Return emp_id, longest_streak, ordered by emp_id.
solution:
WITH dt AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT emp_id, work_date FROM attendance
),
grouped AS (
  SELECT emp_id, work_date,
         work_date - (ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY emp_id ORDER BY work_date))::int AS streak_key
  FROM dt
),
streaks AS (
  SELECT emp_id, streak_key, COUNT(*) AS streak_len
  FROM grouped
  GROUP BY emp_id, streak_key
)
SELECT emp_id, MAX(streak_len) AS longest_streak
FROM streaks
GROUP BY emp_id
ORDER BY emp_id;

title: The Pareto Running Percentage: Order Products by Sales Desc, AND For…
question: Schema: product(product_id, name, sales). Compute the Pareto running percentage: order products by sales DESC, and for each row return cumulative_pct (running sum / overall total * 100) rounded to 2 dp. Also include a boolean in_pareto_80 = TRUE for rows that belong to the 'top 80%' set, defined as: every row whose previous cumulative_pct was strictly less than 80 (i.e. the row that takes the curve INTO the 80% zone or further is included, but no row beyond it). Return product_id, name, sales, cumulative_pct, in_pareto_80. Order by sales DESC then product_id.
schema:
CREATE TABLE product (product_id INT, name TEXT, sales NUMERIC);
INSERT INTO product VALUES
  (1,'A',500),
  (2,'B',300),
  (3,'C',100),
  (4,'D',50),
  (5,'E',30),
  (6,'F',20);
solution:
WITH cum AS (
  SELECT product_id, name, sales,
         SUM(sales) OVER () AS grand_total,
         SUM(sales) OVER (ORDER BY sales DESC, product_id ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) AS running
  FROM product
),
with_pct AS (
  SELECT product_id, name, sales, grand_total, running,
         ROUND((running / grand_total * 100)::numeric, 2) AS cumulative_pct,
         LAG(running, 1, 0) OVER (ORDER BY sales DESC, product_id) AS prev_running
  FROM cum
)
SELECT product_id, name, sales, cumulative_pct,
       (prev_running / grand_total * 100 < 80) AS in_pareto_80
FROM with_pct
ORDER BY sales DESC, product_id;

title: Product(product_id, Category_id, Name)
question: Schema: category(category_id, parent_id, name) (a tree). product(product_id, category_id, name). Find all products that belong DIRECTLY OR INDIRECTLY (via descendant category) to the category named 'Electronics'. Return product_id, name, ordered by product_id.
schema:
CREATE TABLE category (category_id INT, parent_id INT, name TEXT);
INSERT INTO category VALUES
  (1,NULL,'All'),
  (2,1,'Electronics'),
  (3,2,'Phones'),
  (4,2,'Laptops'),
  (5,1,'Apparel'),
  (6,5,'Shoes');
CREATE TABLE product (product_id INT, category_id INT, name TEXT);
INSERT INTO product VALUES
  (101,3,'iPhone'),
  (102,3,'Pixel'),
  (103,4,'MacBook'),
  (104,2,'Charger'),
  (105,6,'Sneaker'),
  (106,5,'Tee');
solution:
WITH RECURSIVE elec AS (
  SELECT category_id FROM category WHERE name = 'Electronics'
  UNION ALL
  SELECT c.category_id FROM category c JOIN elec e ON c.parent_id = e.category_id
)
SELECT p.product_id, p.name
FROM product p
JOIN elec e ON e.category_id = p.category_id
ORDER BY p.product_id;

title: Users Whose Cart Contains Every Product in Target_products
question: Schema: target_products(product_id) (a curated short list of must-have products) and cart(user_id, product_id). Find users whose cart contains EVERY product in target_products. Use the double-NOT-EXISTS formulation (classic SQL relational division). Return user_id, ordered ascending.

title: Two Bookings Overlap If Their Half-open Intervals [start_ts, End_ts)…
question: Schema: booking(id, room_id, start_ts, end_ts). Two bookings overlap if their half-open intervals [start_ts, end_ts) intersect. Find pairs of OVERLAPPING bookings for the SAME room. Return each pair only ONCE (with id_a < id_b). Return room_id, id_a, id_b, ordered by room_id, id_a, id_b.
solution:
SELECT a.room_id, a.id AS id_a, b.id AS id_b
FROM booking a JOIN booking b
  ON a.room_id = b.room_id
 AND a.id < b.id
 AND a.start_ts < b.end_ts
 AND b.start_ts < a.end_ts
ORDER BY a.room_id, a.id, b.id;

title: For Each Given Instant in Probe_ts(ts), Count How Many Sessions Were…
question: Schema: session(id, login_ts, logout_ts). For each given INSTANT in probe_ts(ts), count how many sessions were ACTIVE at that instant (i.e. login_ts <= probe AND logout_ts > probe). Return ts, active_users, ordered by ts. Include probe rows with zero active users.
solution:
SELECT p.ts, COUNT(s.id) AS active_users
FROM probe_ts p
LEFT JOIN session s
  ON s.login_ts <= p.ts AND s.logout_ts > p.ts
GROUP BY p.ts
ORDER BY p.ts;

title: The Top 3 Most-frequent Unordered Pairs of Distinct Items That…
question: Schema: basket_item(basket_id, item). Find the TOP 3 most-frequent unordered pairs of distinct items that appear together in the same basket. The pair (A, B) where A < B is the same as (B, A). Return item_a, item_b, frequency, ordered by frequency DESC then item_a, item_b. LIMIT 3.
schema:
CREATE TABLE basket_item (basket_id INT, item TEXT);
INSERT INTO basket_item VALUES
  (1,'bread'),(1,'milk'),(1,'eggs'),
  (2,'bread'),(2,'milk'),
  (3,'bread'),(3,'butter'),
  (4,'milk'),(4,'cookies'),
  (5,'bread'),(5,'milk'),(5,'butter'),
  (6,'bread'),(6,'jam'),
  (7,'bread'),(7,'milk');
solution:
SELECT a.item AS item_a, b.item AS item_b, COUNT(*) AS frequency
FROM basket_item a JOIN basket_item b
  ON a.basket_id = b.basket_id AND a.item < b.item
GROUP BY a.item, b.item
ORDER BY frequency DESC, a.item, b.item
LIMIT 3;

title: A User 'completed the Funnel' If They Did Each Step in Strict…
question: Schema: event(user_id, event_type, ts) where event_type is 'view', 'cart', or 'purchase'. A user 'completed the funnel' if they did each step IN STRICT CHRONOLOGICAL ORDER — i.e. their earliest 'view' came BEFORE their earliest 'cart', which came BEFORE their earliest 'purchase'. Return user_id of users who completed the funnel, ordered ascending.
solution:
WITH firsts AS (
  SELECT user_id,
         MIN(ts) FILTER (WHERE event_type = 'view')     AS first_view,
         MIN(ts) FILTER (WHERE event_type = 'cart')     AS first_cart,
         MIN(ts) FILTER (WHERE event_type = 'purchase') AS first_purchase
  FROM event
  GROUP BY user_id
)
SELECT user_id FROM firsts
WHERE first_view IS NOT NULL
  AND first_cart IS NOT NULL
  AND first_purchase IS NOT NULL
  AND first_view < first_cart
  AND first_cart < first_purchase
ORDER BY user_id;

title: Bucket Customers Into Deciles (10 Quantile Buckets) by LTV — Bucket…
question: Schema: customer(cust_code, ltv). Bucket customers into DECILES (10 quantile buckets) by LTV — bucket 1 = lowest 10% LTV, bucket 10 = highest 10% LTV. Return cust_code, ltv, decile, ordered by decile DESC then ltv DESC then cust_code.
solution:
SELECT cust_code, ltv,
       NTILE(10) OVER (ORDER BY ltv) AS decile
FROM customer
ORDER BY decile DESC, ltv DESC, cust_code;

title: For Each Entity, Return Its Current (latest by Ts) Status
question: Schema: status_log(entity_id, ts, status) is an append-only log of state changes. For each entity, return its CURRENT (latest by ts) status. Tie-break: later status alphabetically wins (unlikely in practice but deterministic). Use Postgres DISTINCT ON. Return entity_id, ts, status, ordered by entity_id.

title: For Each Department, Compute the Gap Between the Highest And…
question: Schema: employee(emp_id INT, dept TEXT, salary INT). For EACH department, compute the gap between the HIGHEST and SECOND-HIGHEST distinct salary. Departments where only one distinct salary exists (i.e. no genuine second-place value) must surface a NULL gap rather than 0 or being dropped. Return columns dept, top_salary, second_salary, gap, ordered by dept. Use DENSE_RANK so ties at #1 still allow the next distinct salary to be #2.
solution:
WITH d AS (
  SELECT dept, salary,
         DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rk
  FROM employee
  GROUP BY dept, salary
)
SELECT dept,
       MAX(salary) FILTER (WHERE rk = 1) AS top_salary,
       MAX(salary) FILTER (WHERE rk = 2) AS second_salary,
       (MAX(salary) FILTER (WHERE rk = 1) - MAX(salary) FILTER (WHERE rk = 2)) AS gap
FROM d
WHERE rk <= 2
GROUP BY dept
ORDER BY dept;

title: Customers Who Have at Least One Run of Three OR More Strictly…
type: SQL / Window Functions / Strict Sequence Detection
question: Schema: orders(customer_id INT, order_date DATE, amount NUMERIC). Find customers who have at least one run of THREE OR MORE STRICTLY INCREASING consecutive orders (ordered by order_date). That means there exists some position N for the customer such that amount[N] < amount[N+1] < amount[N+2]. Return distinct customer_id ordered ascending. Two-LAG comparison only — do not use recursive CTE or self-join.
solution:
WITH w AS (
  SELECT customer_id,
         amount,
         LAG(amount, 1) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date) AS prev1,
         LAG(amount, 2) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date) AS prev2
  FROM orders
)
SELECT DISTINCT customer_id
FROM w
WHERE prev2 IS NOT NULL
  AND prev2 < prev1
  AND prev1 < amount
ORDER BY customer_id;

title: The Lowest Common Manager (the Deepest Node That Is an Ancestor of Both)
type: SQL / Recursive CTE / Lowest Common Manager
question: Schema: employee(emp_id INT PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, manager_id INT) — CEO has manager_id NULL. Given two specific employees with emp_id = 6 and emp_id = 7, return the LOWEST COMMON MANAGER (the deepest node that is an ancestor of BOTH). Use a recursive CTE to compute each employee's full ancestor chain (including themselves), then intersect. Return columns lca_emp_id, lca_name. If one of the employees is itself the ancestor of the other, return that employee.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employee (emp_id INT PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, manager_id INT);
INSERT INTO employee VALUES
  (1,'Alice',NULL),
  (2,'Bob',1),
  (3,'Carla',1),
  (4,'Dan',2),
  (5,'Erin',2),
  (6,'Finn',4),
  (7,'Gita',5),
  (8,'Hugo',3);
solution:
WITH RECURSIVE ancestors AS (
  SELECT emp_id AS start_id, emp_id AS anc_id, 0 AS depth
  FROM employee
  UNION ALL
  SELECT a.start_id, e.manager_id, a.depth + 1
  FROM ancestors a
  JOIN employee e ON e.emp_id = a.anc_id
  WHERE e.manager_id IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT a1.anc_id AS lca_emp_id, e.name AS lca_name
FROM ancestors a1
JOIN ancestors a2 ON a1.anc_id = a2.anc_id
JOIN employee e ON e.emp_id = a1.anc_id
WHERE a1.start_id = 6 AND a2.start_id = 7
ORDER BY a1.depth ASC
LIMIT 1;

title: For Each Employee, Ntile-bucket Their Chronological Salary Points…
question: Schema: salary_history(emp_id INT, effective_date DATE, salary INT) records salary changes over time. For each employee, NTILE-bucket their CHRONOLOGICAL salary points into 3 'compensation tiers' (1=lowest third, 3=highest third). Return only the rows where the tier STRICTLY INCREASED from the previous chronological row for that employee (a tier transition upward). Output columns: emp_id, effective_date, salary, prev_tier, new_tier. Order by emp_id, effective_date.
solution:
WITH t AS (
  SELECT emp_id, effective_date, salary,
         NTILE(3) OVER (PARTITION BY emp_id ORDER BY salary) AS tier
  FROM salary_history
),
lag_t AS (
  SELECT emp_id, effective_date, salary, tier,
         LAG(tier) OVER (PARTITION BY emp_id ORDER BY effective_date) AS prev_tier
  FROM t
)
SELECT emp_id, effective_date, salary, prev_tier, tier AS new_tier
FROM lag_t
WHERE prev_tier IS NOT NULL AND tier > prev_tier
ORDER BY emp_id, effective_date;

title: The First Month (chronologically) at Which MoM Growth Exceeded 20%…
question: Schema: monthly_revenue(month_start DATE, revenue NUMERIC) — one row per first-of-month. Find the FIRST month (chronologically) at which MoM growth EXCEEDED 20% AND the PRIOR month also had positive growth (i.e. two consecutive months of growth, with the second crossing the 20% threshold). Return columns month_start, prev_revenue, revenue, mom_growth_pct for that single row. mom_growth_pct rounded to 2 decimals. If no such month exists, return zero rows.
solution:
WITH g AS (
  SELECT month_start, revenue,
         LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month_start) AS prev_revenue,
         LAG(revenue, 2) OVER (ORDER BY month_start) AS prev2_revenue
  FROM monthly_revenue
)
SELECT month_start,
       prev_revenue,
       revenue,
       ROUND(((revenue - prev_revenue) / NULLIF(prev_revenue, 0) * 100)::numeric, 2) AS mom_growth_pct
FROM g
WHERE prev_revenue IS NOT NULL AND prev2_revenue IS NOT NULL
  AND prev_revenue > prev2_revenue
  AND (revenue - prev_revenue) / NULLIF(prev_revenue, 0) > 0.20
ORDER BY month_start
LIMIT 1;

title: 'underperformers in Elite Departments': Employees Who Are in the Top…
question: Schema: employee(emp_id INT, dept TEXT, salary INT). Find 'underperformers in elite departments': employees who are in the TOP HALF of company-wide salary (CUME_DIST > 0.50) BUT in the BOTTOM 30% of their own department (CUME_DIST <= 0.30 within dept). Return emp_id, dept, salary, company_pct, dept_pct where the two percentiles are rounded to 2 decimals. Order by emp_id. (This catches people who are well-paid by global standards but lagging within an elite team.)
solution:
WITH r AS (
  SELECT emp_id, dept, salary,
         CUME_DIST() OVER (ORDER BY salary) AS company_pct,
         CUME_DIST() OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary) AS dept_pct
  FROM employee
)
SELECT emp_id, dept, salary,
       ROUND(company_pct::numeric, 2) AS company_pct,
       ROUND(dept_pct::numeric, 2) AS dept_pct
FROM r
WHERE company_pct > 0.50 AND dept_pct <= 0.30
ORDER BY emp_id;

title: For Each Manager, Return the Count of Their 'skip-level Reports' —…
question: Schema: employee(emp_id INT, name TEXT, manager_id INT). For each manager, return the count of their 'skip-level reports' — employees who report to someone who reports to them (i.e. EXACTLY 2 levels below them in the tree, NOT direct reports, NOT 3+ levels down). Return manager_id, manager_name, skip_level_count, ordered by manager_id. Only include managers who have at least one skip-level report.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employee (emp_id INT, name TEXT, manager_id INT);
INSERT INTO employee VALUES
  (1,'Alice',NULL),
  (2,'Bob',1),
  (3,'Carla',1),
  (4,'Dan',2),
  (5,'Erin',2),
  (6,'Finn',3),
  (7,'Gita',4),
  (8,'Hugo',5),
  (9,'Iris',7);
solution:
SELECT m.emp_id AS manager_id,
       m.name AS manager_name,
       COUNT(skip.emp_id)::int AS skip_level_count
FROM employee m
JOIN employee mid ON mid.manager_id = m.emp_id
JOIN employee skip ON skip.manager_id = mid.emp_id
GROUP BY m.emp_id, m.name
ORDER BY m.emp_id;

title: A Team_id May Have a 10000 Commission Pool to Split Among Its…
question: Schema: sale(sale_id INT, salesperson_id INT, team_id INT, amount NUMERIC). A team_id may have a 10000 commission pool to split among its salespeople, proportional to each salesperson's TOTAL sales within the team. Compute each salesperson's share of the 10000 pool, rounded to 2 decimals. Output columns team_id, salesperson_id, total_sales, commission_share. Order by team_id, salesperson_id. A salesperson may appear in multiple teams — keep them as separate rows per team.
solution:
WITH per_person AS (
  SELECT team_id, salesperson_id, SUM(amount) AS total_sales
  FROM sale
  GROUP BY team_id, salesperson_id
)
SELECT team_id,
       salesperson_id,
       total_sales,
       ROUND((10000.0 * total_sales / SUM(total_sales) OVER (PARTITION BY team_id))::numeric, 2) AS commission_share
FROM per_person
ORDER BY team_id, salesperson_id;

title: A Cohort Retention Table: Each User's Cohort Month Is Their…
question: Schema: event(user_id INT, event_date DATE). Build a cohort retention table: each user's COHORT MONTH is their first-ever event month. For each (cohort_month, months_since_cohort) pair where months_since_cohort is 0, 1, 2, or 3, return the count of cohort users who had ANY event in the calendar month exactly that many months after their cohort month. Output columns: cohort_month, months_since, active_users. Include zero counts for in-range cells. Order by cohort_month, months_since.
solution:
WITH first_event AS (
  SELECT user_id,
         DATE_TRUNC('month', MIN(event_date))::date AS cohort_month
  FROM event
  GROUP BY user_id
),
active AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT user_id,
         DATE_TRUNC('month', event_date)::date AS active_month
  FROM event
),
grid AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT f.cohort_month, m.months_since
  FROM first_event f
  CROSS JOIN (VALUES (0),(1),(2),(3)) AS m(months_since)
)
SELECT g.cohort_month,
       g.months_since,
       COUNT(DISTINCT a.user_id)::int AS active_users
FROM grid g
LEFT JOIN first_event f ON f.cohort_month = g.cohort_month
LEFT JOIN active a
  ON a.user_id = f.user_id
  AND a.active_month = (g.cohort_month + (g.months_since || ' months')::interval)::date
GROUP BY g.cohort_month, g.months_since
ORDER BY g.cohort_month, g.months_since;

title: For Each Sale, Compute Its Running Average Up to AND Including That…
type: SQL / Window Functions / Running vs Static Aggregate
subtopic: Window Functions / Running vs Static Aggregate
question: Schema: sale(sale_id INT, sale_date DATE, amount NUMERIC). For each sale, compute its RUNNING average up to and including that row, AND the GLOBAL average across the whole table. Then return ONLY the sales where the running average is FOR THE FIRST TIME (chronologically) at or above the global average. There is at most one such row by definition of 'first time'. Columns: sale_date, amount, running_avg, global_avg. running_avg and global_avg rounded to 2 decimals.
solution:
WITH base AS (
  SELECT sale_date, amount,
         AVG(amount) OVER (ORDER BY sale_date ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS running_avg,
         AVG(amount) OVER () AS global_avg
  FROM sale
),
flagged AS (
  SELECT sale_date, amount, running_avg, global_avg,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY (running_avg >= global_avg) ORDER BY sale_date) AS first_above_rn
  FROM base
  WHERE running_avg >= global_avg
)
SELECT sale_date,
       amount,
       ROUND(running_avg::numeric, 2) AS running_avg,
       ROUND(global_avg::numeric, 2) AS global_avg
FROM flagged
WHERE first_above_rn = 1
ORDER BY sale_date;

title: The Department(s) That Contain the Largest Number of Employees Whose…
type: SQL / Window Functions / Ranked Top-1 With Department Tie
subtopic: Window Functions / Ranked Top-1 With Department Tie
question: Schema: employee(emp_id INT, dept TEXT, salary INT). Find the department(s) that contain the LARGEST number of employees whose salary equals the highest salary in their own department. (For each dept, count how many people TIE for the dept's max salary — return the dept(s) with the largest such count; tie at the dept level keeps all winners.) Return columns: dept, top_earner_count, ordered by dept.
solution:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT dept, salary,
         RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rk
  FROM employee
),
per_dept AS (
  SELECT dept, COUNT(*) AS top_earner_count
  FROM ranked
  WHERE rk = 1
  GROUP BY dept
)
SELECT dept, top_earner_count
FROM per_dept
WHERE top_earner_count = (SELECT MAX(top_earner_count) FROM per_dept)
ORDER BY dept;

title: For Each Employee, Compute Their PERCENT_RANK Within Their…
question: Schema: employee(emp_id INT, dept TEXT, salary INT). For each employee, compute their PERCENT_RANK within their department (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary). Return employees whose PERCENT_RANK is EXACTLY 0 (the lowest-paid in their dept) — but ONLY IF their department has more than 2 employees (so PERCENT_RANK is meaningful). For single-member or two-member departments, exclude the rows entirely. Output emp_id, dept, salary, ordered by dept, emp_id.
solution:
WITH r AS (
  SELECT emp_id, dept, salary,
         PERCENT_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary) AS pr,
         COUNT(*) OVER (PARTITION BY dept) AS dept_size
  FROM employee
)
SELECT emp_id, dept, salary
FROM r
WHERE pr = 0 AND dept_size > 2
ORDER BY dept, emp_id;

title: For Customers Who Have Made at Least 2 Purchases, Return The…
type: SQL / Window Functions / Second-Time Buyer Latency
question: Schema: purchase(customer_id INT, purchased_at TIMESTAMP). For customers who have made AT LEAST 2 purchases, return the customer_id, their first purchase timestamp, their second purchase timestamp, and the latency in DAYS between them (as INTEGER, truncated toward zero). Return only customers whose latency is BELOW the median latency across all qualifying customers. Output columns: customer_id, first_at, second_at, latency_days, ordered by customer_id.
solution:
WITH numbered AS (
  SELECT customer_id, purchased_at,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY purchased_at) AS rn
  FROM purchase
),
paired AS (
  SELECT n1.customer_id,
         n1.purchased_at AS first_at,
         n2.purchased_at AS second_at,
         FLOOR(EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (n2.purchased_at - n1.purchased_at)) / 86400)::int AS latency_days
  FROM numbered n1
  JOIN numbered n2
    ON n1.customer_id = n2.customer_id
    AND n1.rn = 1 AND n2.rn = 2
),
m AS (
  SELECT PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY latency_days) AS med FROM paired
)
SELECT customer_id, first_at, second_at, latency_days
FROM paired, m
WHERE latency_days < m.med
ORDER BY customer_id;

title: Rank Departments by Their Average Salary, But First Exclude The…
question: Schema: employee(emp_id INT, dept TEXT, salary INT). Rank departments by their AVERAGE salary, but FIRST exclude the highest-paid and lowest-paid employee in each department (trimmed dept average). Departments with fewer than 3 employees produce a NULL trimmed_avg and rank — keep them in the output. Return columns: dept, headcount, trimmed_avg, dept_rank. trimmed_avg rounded to 2 decimals. Order by dept.
solution:
WITH r AS (
  SELECT emp_id, dept, salary,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rn_desc,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary ASC)  AS rn_asc,
         COUNT(*) OVER (PARTITION BY dept) AS headcount
  FROM employee
),
trimmed AS (
  SELECT dept, headcount,
         CASE WHEN headcount >= 3
              THEN ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN rn_desc <> 1 AND rn_asc <> 1 THEN salary END)::numeric, 2)
              ELSE NULL
         END AS trimmed_avg
  FROM r
  GROUP BY dept, headcount
)
SELECT dept, headcount, trimmed_avg,
       CASE WHEN trimmed_avg IS NULL THEN NULL
            ELSE RANK() OVER (ORDER BY trimmed_avg DESC NULLS LAST)
       END AS dept_rank
FROM trimmed
ORDER BY dept;

title: For Each Row, Compute the 7-day Trailing Average Revenue for That…
type: SQL / Window Functions / Rolling Window Per Group
subtopic: Window Functions / Rolling Window Per Group
question: Schema: daily_sales(store_id INT, sale_date DATE, revenue NUMERIC) — one row per (store, day) on days where the store sold anything (sparse). For each row, compute the 7-DAY trailing AVERAGE REVENUE for that store (including the current day, looking back EXACTLY 6 prior calendar days, regardless of whether the store had sales on those days — missing days count as 0). Use a RANGE-based date frame, NOT a ROWS frame. Output store_id, sale_date, revenue, rolling_7d_avg rounded to 2 decimals, ordered by store_id, sale_date.
solution:
SELECT store_id,
       sale_date,
       revenue,
       ROUND((SUM(revenue) OVER (
          PARTITION BY store_id
          ORDER BY sale_date
          RANGE BETWEEN INTERVAL '6 days' PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
       ) / 7.0)::numeric, 2) AS rolling_7d_avg
FROM daily_sales
ORDER BY store_id, sale_date;

title: Activity(user_id Int, Activity_month Date) Where Activity_month Is…
type: SQL / Conditional Aggregation / Cohort Retention Rate
question: Schema: signup(user_id INT, signup_month DATE) where signup_month is always first-of-month. activity(user_id INT, activity_month DATE) where activity_month is first-of-month and one row per (user, month-with-activity). For each signup_month, return: cohort_size (users in that cohort), retained_m1 (users active exactly 1 month after signup), and retention_pct_m1 (retained_m1 / cohort_size * 100, 2 dp). Output signup_month, cohort_size, retained_m1, retention_pct_m1 ordered by signup_month. Months with zero retention still appear.
solution:
WITH cohort AS (
  SELECT signup_month, COUNT(*) AS cohort_size FROM signup GROUP BY signup_month
),
retained AS (
  SELECT s.signup_month,
         COUNT(DISTINCT a.user_id) AS retained_m1
  FROM signup s
  LEFT JOIN activity a
    ON a.user_id = s.user_id
    AND a.activity_month = (s.signup_month + INTERVAL '1 month')::date
  GROUP BY s.signup_month
)
SELECT c.signup_month,
       c.cohort_size,
       r.retained_m1,
       ROUND((100.0 * r.retained_m1 / c.cohort_size)::numeric, 2) AS retention_pct_m1
FROM cohort c
JOIN retained r USING (signup_month)
ORDER BY c.signup_month;

title: For Each Dept, Return Only the Third-highest Distinct Salary Value
type: SQL / Window Functions / Distinct Salary Ranking
question: Schema: employee(emp_id INT, dept TEXT, salary INT). For each dept, return only the THIRD-HIGHEST DISTINCT salary value. Departments with fewer than 3 distinct salaries should NOT appear in the result. Output columns: dept, third_highest_distinct_salary, ordered by dept.
solution:
WITH d AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT dept, salary FROM employee
),
r AS (
  SELECT dept, salary,
         DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rk
  FROM d
)
SELECT dept, salary AS third_highest_distinct_salary
FROM r
WHERE rk = 3
ORDER BY dept;

title: For Each Pair of Employees (A, B) Where a Is an Ancestor (in the Org…
question: Schema: employee(emp_id INT, name TEXT, manager_id INT). For each pair of employees (A, B) where A is an ANCESTOR (in the org tree) of B, return the chain length — number of manager_id hops needed to go from B up to A. Output columns: ancestor_id, descendant_id, hops, ordered by ancestor_id, descendant_id. Include immediate (hops=1) up to deepest chains. Don't include (X, X) self-pairs.
schema:
CREATE TABLE employee (emp_id INT, name TEXT, manager_id INT);
INSERT INTO employee VALUES
  (1,'Alice',NULL),
  (2,'Bob',1),
  (3,'Carla',1),
  (4,'Dan',2),
  (5,'Erin',4);
solution:
WITH RECURSIVE chains AS (
  SELECT manager_id AS ancestor_id, emp_id AS descendant_id, 1 AS hops
  FROM employee
  WHERE manager_id IS NOT NULL
  UNION ALL
  SELECT e.manager_id, c.descendant_id, c.hops + 1
  FROM chains c
  JOIN employee e ON e.emp_id = c.ancestor_id
  WHERE e.manager_id IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT ancestor_id, descendant_id, hops
FROM chains
ORDER BY ancestor_id, descendant_id;

title: 'Salary Compression' = a Junior Level's Max Salary >= a Senior…
type: SQL / Window Functions / Salary Compression Detection
question: Schema: employee(emp_id INT, dept TEXT, level TEXT, salary INT) where level is one of 'L1', 'L2', 'L3' (junior to senior). 'Salary compression' = a junior level's MAX salary >= a senior level's MIN salary within the same dept. Return department-level rows where compression exists between L1 and L2, OR between L2 and L3. Columns: dept, junior_level, junior_max, senior_level, senior_min. Sort by dept, junior_level.
solution:
WITH agg AS (
  SELECT dept, level, MIN(salary) AS min_s, MAX(salary) AS max_s
  FROM employee
  GROUP BY dept, level
),
pairs AS (
  SELECT j.dept, j.level AS junior_level, j.max_s AS junior_max,
         s.level AS senior_level, s.min_s AS senior_min
  FROM agg j JOIN agg s
    ON j.dept = s.dept
   AND ((j.level = 'L1' AND s.level = 'L2') OR (j.level = 'L2' AND s.level = 'L3'))
)
SELECT dept, junior_level, junior_max, senior_level, senior_min
FROM pairs
WHERE junior_max >= senior_min
ORDER BY dept, junior_level;

title: For Each Dept, Return: Total_headcount, Num_female, Num_male,…
question: Schema: employee(emp_id INT, dept TEXT, gender TEXT, salary INT). For each dept, return: total_headcount, num_female, num_male, female_share_pct (rounded 2dp), female_avg_salary, male_avg_salary, salary_gap_pct = ((male_avg - female_avg) / male_avg) * 100 rounded to 2 dp. If a dept has zero of one gender, the corresponding avg should be NULL and salary_gap_pct should be NULL. Output ordered by dept.
solution:
SELECT dept,
       COUNT(*) AS total_headcount,
       COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE gender = 'F') AS num_female,
       COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE gender = 'M') AS num_male,
       ROUND((100.0 * COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE gender = 'F') / COUNT(*))::numeric, 2) AS female_share_pct,
       AVG(salary) FILTER (WHERE gender = 'F')::numeric(10,2) AS female_avg_salary,
       AVG(salary) FILTER (WHERE gender = 'M')::numeric(10,2) AS male_avg_salary,
       CASE
         WHEN AVG(salary) FILTER (WHERE gender = 'M') IS NULL
           OR AVG(salary) FILTER (WHERE gender = 'F') IS NULL
         THEN NULL
         ELSE ROUND(((AVG(salary) FILTER (WHERE gender = 'M') - AVG(salary) FILTER (WHERE gender = 'F'))
               / AVG(salary) FILTER (WHERE gender = 'M') * 100)::numeric, 2)
       END AS salary_gap_pct
FROM employee
GROUP BY dept
ORDER BY dept;

title: Growth Accounting — Daily New / Retained / Resurrected / Churned
question:
Growth accounting — daily new / retained / resurrected / churned.

Schema: dau(dt, user_id, inc_amt) — one row per active user per day; inc_amt is the value they created that day.

For each day, classify the active-user base into four buckets:
- new — user's FIRST-ever active day is this day
- retained — user was also active the PREVIOUS day
- resurrected — user is not new, and was NOT active the previous day (came back from dormancy)
- churned — user was active the previous day but NOT this day (report as a negative number)

Return columns: day, dau, new_users, retained, resurrected, churned, ordered by day. Note: a trailing row appears for the day AFTER the last activity, capturing the final day's churn.

Hint: a FULL OUTER self-join of the activity table to itself, offset by one day (tm.dt = lm.dt + 1), puts each user's today-row next to their yesterday-row. Churned users exist only on the lm side.
solution:
WITH first_dt AS (
  SELECT user_id, MIN(dt) AS first_dt FROM dau GROUP BY user_id
),
dau_decorated AS (
  SELECT d.dt, d.user_id, f.first_dt
  FROM dau d JOIN first_dt f ON d.user_id = f.user_id
  WHERE d.inc_amt > 0
)
SELECT
  COALESCE(tm.dt, lm.dt + 1) AS day,
  COUNT(DISTINCT tm.user_id) AS dau,
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN tm.first_dt = tm.dt THEN tm.user_id END) AS new_users,
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN lm.user_id IS NOT NULL THEN tm.user_id END) AS retained,
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN tm.first_dt <> tm.dt AND lm.user_id IS NULL THEN tm.user_id END) AS resurrected,
  -1 * COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN tm.user_id IS NULL THEN lm.user_id END) AS churned
FROM dau_decorated tm
FULL OUTER JOIN dau_decorated lm
  ON tm.user_id = lm.user_id AND tm.dt = lm.dt + 1
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1;

title: Cumulative LTV by Weekly Cohort
question:
Cumulative LTV by weekly cohort.

Schema: wau(week, user_id, inc_amt) — one row per active user per week, inc_amt is value created that week.

For each weekly signup cohort (a user's cohort = the week of their first activity), produce the cumulative-LTV curve: for each weeks_since_first, how many users were active, what % of the cohort that is, the cumulative value through that week, and cumulative value per cohort member.

Return columns: first_week, weeks_since_first, users, cohort_num_users, retained_pctg, cum_amt, cum_amt_per_user, ordered by first_week, weeks_since_first.

Hint: build a per-cohort-per-offset cohorts table, a cohort_sizes table (offset 0), then a semi-cartesian self-join of cohorts to itself on the same cohort where c2.weeks_since_first <= c1.weeks_since_first — summing c2.inc_amt gives the running cumulative.
solution:
WITH first_week AS (
  SELECT user_id, MIN(week) AS first_week FROM wau GROUP BY user_id
),
wau_d AS (
  SELECT w.week, w.user_id, w.inc_amt, f.first_week
  FROM wau w JOIN first_week f ON w.user_id = f.user_id
),
cohorts AS (
  SELECT first_week, (week - first_week)/7 AS weeks_since_first,
         COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS users, SUM(inc_amt) AS inc_amt
  FROM wau_d GROUP BY 1,2
),
cohort_sizes AS (
  SELECT first_week, users FROM cohorts WHERE weeks_since_first = 0
)
SELECT c1.first_week, c1.weeks_since_first, c1.users,
       cs.users AS cohort_num_users,
       ROUND(1.0*c1.users/cs.users, 3) AS retained_pctg,
       SUM(c2.inc_amt) AS cum_amt,
       ROUND(1.0*SUM(c2.inc_amt)/cs.users, 2) AS cum_amt_per_user
FROM cohorts c1
JOIN cohort_sizes cs ON cs.first_week = c1.first_week
JOIN cohorts c2 ON c1.first_week = c2.first_week AND c2.weeks_since_first <= c1.weeks_since_first
GROUP BY 1,2,3,4
ORDER BY 1,2;

title: Monthly Cohort Retention Triangle
question:
Monthly cohort retention triangle.

Schema: activity(activity_date, user_id) — one row per user per day they were active (a user may appear many times).

Produce the classic retention heatmap dataset: for each monthly signup cohort (cohort = month of a user's first activity), and each calendar month they were subsequently active, the count of distinct active users and the month-offset.

Return columns: first_month, month, months_since_first, active_users, ordered by first_month, month. All dates as the first day of the month.

Hint: derive first_month per user, derive the set of distinct (month, user) pairs, join, and compute the offset as a difference of (year*12 + month) values — NOT a raw date subtraction, which gives days.
solution:
WITH first_month AS (
  SELECT user_id, DATE_TRUNC('month', MIN(activity_date))::date AS first_month
  FROM activity GROUP BY user_id
),
monthly AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT DATE_TRUNC('month', activity_date)::date AS month, user_id FROM activity
)
SELECT f.first_month, m.month,
       (EXTRACT(YEAR FROM m.month) * 12 + EXTRACT(MONTH FROM m.month))
       - (EXTRACT(YEAR FROM f.first_month) * 12 + EXTRACT(MONTH FROM f.first_month)) AS months_since_first,
       COUNT(DISTINCT m.user_id) AS active_users
FROM monthly m JOIN first_month f ON m.user_id = f.user_id
GROUP BY 1,2,3
ORDER BY 1,2;

title: Produce a Report with One Subtotal Row per Region Plus a Single…
question: Schema: sales(region TEXT, product TEXT, amount INT). Produce a report with one subtotal row per region plus a single grand-total row, using GROUP BY ROLLUP. The catch: the grand-total row would otherwise have NULL in region, indistinguishable from a real NULL region. Use the GROUPING() function to print the literal text Grand Total in the region column of the total row (and Region Total is NOT needed — only per-region rows and the one grand total). Return columns region, total, ordered so the grand total sorts last.
solution:
SELECT
  CASE WHEN GROUPING(region) = 1 THEN 'Grand Total' ELSE region END AS region,
  SUM(amount) AS total
FROM sales
GROUP BY ROLLUP (region)
ORDER BY GROUPING(region), region;

title: You Must Report Monthly Totals for Every (region, Month) Combination…
question: Schema: sales(region TEXT, sold_on DATE, amount INT). You must report monthly totals for EVERY (region, month) combination across the first quarter of 2025 (Jan, Feb, Mar) — including combinations with zero sales, which must show 0 rather than being absent. Build the complete grid with a CROSS JOIN of the distinct regions against a generate_series of month starts, then LEFT JOIN the sales. Return region, month_start, total, ordered by region, month_start.
solution:
SELECT
  r.region,
  gs::date AS month_start,
  COALESCE(SUM(s.amount), 0) AS total
FROM (SELECT DISTINCT region FROM sales) r
CROSS JOIN generate_series(DATE '2025-01-01', DATE '2025-03-01', INTERVAL '1 month') AS gs
LEFT JOIN sales s
  ON s.region = r.region
 AND date_trunc('month', s.sold_on) = gs
GROUP BY r.region, gs
ORDER BY r.region, month_start;

title: The Project_id of Every Project That Is Fully Complete — Meaning All…
question: Schema: task(project_id INT, task_id INT, done BOOLEAN). Return the project_id of every project that is fully complete — meaning ALL of its tasks have done = true — and also include any project that currently has zero tasks (an empty project is trivially complete). Use a boolean aggregate. Return one column project_id, ordered ascending. Note: projects with no tasks are listed in a separate project table.
solution:
SELECT p.project_id
FROM project p
LEFT JOIN task t ON t.project_id = p.project_id
GROUP BY p.project_id
HAVING BOOL_AND(COALESCE(t.done, true))
ORDER BY p.project_id;

title: Extract the Level Token (the Value After Level=, Up to the Next…
question: Schema: log_line(id INT, raw TEXT) where raw looks like "[2025-03-04] level=WARN msg=disk full". Extract the level token (the value after level=, up to the next space) into a column level, using regexp_replace (or substring with a regex) — pure SQL, no application code. Return id, level, ordered by id.
solution:
SELECT
  id,
  regexp_replace(raw, '^.*level=([A-Z]+).*$', '\1') AS level
FROM log_line
ORDER BY id;

title: Using PostgreSQL's Generate_series, Produce the 5 Month-start Dates…
question: Using PostgreSQL's generate_series, produce the 5 month-start dates of 2025 from January through May, each paired with its 1-based position in the series. Use WITH ORDINALITY so the position comes from the set-returning function itself rather than a window function. Return columns month_num, month_start, ordered by month_num.
solution:
SELECT
  ord AS month_num,
  m::date AS month_start
FROM generate_series(DATE '2025-01-01', DATE '2025-05-01', INTERVAL '1 month')
     WITH ORDINALITY AS t(m, ord)
ORDER BY ord;

title: For Each Product, Return Its Single Most Recent Price
question: Schema: price_change(product_id INT, changed_at TIMESTAMP, price NUMERIC, source TEXT). For each product, return its single most recent price. If two changes share the exact same changed_at for a product, break the tie by taking the row whose source is alphabetically last. Use DISTINCT ON. Return product_id, price, source, ordered by product_id.
solution:
SELECT DISTINCT ON (product_id)
  product_id, price::float8 AS price, source
FROM price_change
ORDER BY product_id, changed_at DESC, source DESC;

title: The Top-scoring Players Using the Standard Fetch First
question: Schema: score(player TEXT, points INT). Return the top-scoring players using the standard FETCH FIRST ... ROWS WITH TIES clause so that, instead of an arbitrary cutoff, anyone tied at the boundary is also included. Request the top 2 rows WITH TIES. Return player, points ordered by points DESC then player ASC.
solution:
SELECT player, points
FROM score
ORDER BY points DESC
FETCH FIRST 2 ROWS WITH TIES;

title: The Region Value Can Legitimately Be NULL (meaning 'unspecified'),…
question: Schema: two staging snapshots snap_a(region TEXT, metric INT) and snap_b(region TEXT, metric INT). The region value can legitimately be NULL (meaning 'unspecified'), and a NULL region in A should match a NULL region in B. Join the two snapshots on region using a NULL-safe predicate so NULL pairs with NULL, and return region, a_metric, b_metric for matched rows, ordered with the NULL region last. Use IS NOT DISTINCT FROM.
solution:
SELECT a.region AS region, a.metric AS a_metric, b.metric AS b_metric
FROM snap_a a
JOIN snap_b b ON a.region IS NOT DISTINCT FROM b.region
ORDER BY a.region NULLS LAST;

title: Most_common_word(text, K=1) That Returns the K Most Frequent…
question: Write most_common_word(text, k=1) that returns the k most frequent lowercase 'words' (alphabetic runs) in text. Ties broken by first-seen order. Return a list of strings of length up to k. Use only the standard library.
solution:
import re
from collections import Counter

def most_common_word(text, k=1):
    words = re.findall(r'[a-zA-Z]+', text.lower())
    counts = Counter(words)
    return [w for w, _ in counts.most_common(k)]

title: Coroutine Gather_with_concurrency(limit, *coros) That Runs the Given…
question: Write a coroutine gather_with_concurrency(limit, *coros) that runs the given coroutines concurrently, but with at most limit running at any time. Return a list of results in the SAME ORDER as the input coroutines. Use only asyncio from the stdlib (no third-party libs).
solution:
import asyncio

async def gather_with_concurrency(limit, *coros):
    sem = asyncio.Semaphore(limit)
    async def run(c):
        async with sem:
            return await c
    return await asyncio.gather(*(run(c) for c in coros))

title: _snake_case(s) That Converts a CamelCase OR PascalCase String To…

solution:
import re

_PASS1 = re.compile(r'(.)([A-Z][a-z]+)')
_PASS2 = re.compile(r'([a-z0-9])([A-Z])')

def to_snake_case(s):
    s = _PASS1.sub(r'\1_\2', s)
    s = _PASS2.sub(r'\1_\2', s)
    return s.lower()

title: P_n_frequent(items, N) That Returns the N Most Frequent Items as A…
question: Write top_n_frequent(items, n) that returns the n most frequent items as a list of (item, count) tuples sorted by count descending. Ties broken alphabetically by the item value. If n exceeds the number of distinct items, return all of them.
solution:
from collections import Counter

def top_n_frequent(items, n):
    c = Counter(items)
    # sort by (-count, item) — descending count, ascending item for ties
    pairs = sorted(c.items(), key=lambda kv: (-kv[1], kv[0]))
    return pairs[:n]

title: Is_valid_email(s) That Returns True If S Looks Like a Valid Email…
question:
Write is_valid_email(s) that returns True if s looks like a valid email address, False otherwise. Reasonable rules (NOT full RFC 5322):
- Exactly one @
- Non-empty local part containing only letters, digits, ., _, +, -
- Domain has at least one ., each label is letters/digits/hyphens, no leading/trailing hyphen, TLD is at least 2 letters.

Use a single compiled regex. Assume s is always a string.
solution:
import re

_EMAIL_RE = re.compile(
    r'^[A-Za-z0-9._+\-]+'  # local
    r'@'
    r'(?:[A-Za-z0-9](?:[A-Za-z0-9\-]*[A-Za-z0-9])?\.)+'  # one or more labels
    r'[A-Za-z]{2,}$'  # TLD
)

def is_valid_email(s):
    if not isinstance(s, str):
        return False
    return _EMAIL_RE.match(s) is not None

title: Is_valid_card(number) That Validates a Credit-card Number Using The…

1. Strip non-digits.
2. From the rightmost digit, double every SECOND digit; if the doubled value is >= 10, sum its digits (or equivalently subtract 9).
3. Sum everything. Number is valid iff the total is divisible by 10.

Reject inputs whose stripped digit count is not in [13, 19].

title: Sanitize_filename(name) That Returns a Cross-platform-safe Filename
question:
Write sanitize_filename(name) that returns a cross-platform-safe filename. Rules:
- Strip leading/trailing whitespace and dots from the input.
- Replace any character in < > : " / \\ | ? * and ASCII control chars (0-31) with _.
- Collapse runs of internal whitespace to a single underscore.
- Reject (return '_') Windows reserved names: CON, PRN, AUX, NUL, COM1..COM9, LPT1..LPT9 (case-insensitive, with or without an extension).
- Truncate to 255 characters.
- If result is empty after cleaning, return '_'.
solution:
import re

_BAD = re.compile(r'[<>:"/\\|?*\x00-\x1f]')
_WS = re.compile(r'\s+')
_RESERVED = {'CON','PRN','AUX','NUL'} | {f'COM{i}' for i in range(1,10)} | {f'LPT{i}' for i in range(1,10)}

def sanitize_filename(name):
    s = name.strip(' .\t\n\r')
    s = _BAD.sub('_', s)
    s = _WS.sub('_', s)
    if not s:
        return '_'
    base = s.split('.', 1)[0].upper()
    if base in _RESERVED:
        return '_'
    return s[:255]

title: Canonical_url(url) That Returns a Canonical Form of the URL: -…
question:
Write canonical_url(url) that returns a canonical form of the URL:
- Lowercase the scheme and host.
- Strip default port (:80 for http, :443 for https).
- Remove URL fragment (#...).
- Sort query parameters by key (stable on duplicate keys — preserve original relative order among duplicates).
- If path is empty, use /.

Use urllib.parse from the stdlib.
solution:
from urllib.parse import urlsplit, urlunsplit, parse_qsl, urlencode

def canonical_url(url):
    parts = urlsplit(url)
    scheme = parts.scheme.lower()
    host = parts.hostname or ''
    port = parts.port
    # Strip default port
    if (scheme == 'http' and port == 80) or (scheme == 'https' and port == 443):
        port = None
    netloc = host
    if port is not None:
        netloc = f'{host}:{port}'
    path = parts.path or '/'
    # Sort query params by key (stable on dup keys)
    pairs = parse_qsl(parts.query, keep_blank_values=True)
    pairs = sorted(pairs, key=lambda kv: kv[0])
    query = urlencode(pairs)
    return urlunsplit((scheme, netloc, path, query, ''))

title: Dedupe_ignore(records, Ignore_keys) That Returns the Deduplicated List
question: Given a list of dicts representing rows, write dedupe_ignore(records, ignore_keys) that returns the deduplicated list. Two records are duplicates iff they agree on every key EXCEPT those in ignore_keys. Keep the FIRST occurrence (preserve input order).
solution:
def dedupe_ignore(records, ignore_keys):
    ignore = set(ignore_keys)
    seen = set()
    out = []
    for r in records:
        # Build a hashable signature of non-ignored fields
        sig = tuple(sorted((k, v) for k, v in r.items() if k not in ignore))
        if sig not in seen:
            seen.add(sig)
            out.append(r)
    return out

solution:
def under_budget(attempts, max_per_hour, now):
    cutoff = now - 3600
    recent = sum(1 for t in attempts if cutoff <= t <= now)
    return (recent + 1) <= max_per_hour

title: Async Function Batch_collect(items_q, Batch_size, Max_wait_s)
question:
Write an async function batch_collect(items_q, batch_size, max_wait_s). items_q is an asyncio.Queue. Yield lists of items, flushing when EITHER:
- the batch reaches batch_size items, OR
- max_wait_s seconds elapsed since the FIRST item in the current batch.

The producer signals end-of-stream by putting a special END sentinel on the queue. Flush the final partial batch (if any) before returning. Use only asyncio.
solution:
import asyncio

END = object()

async def batch_collect(items_q, batch_size, max_wait_s):
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    batch = []
    deadline = None
    while True:
        if not batch:
            item = await items_q.get()
            if item is END:
                return
            batch.append(item)
            deadline = loop.time() + max_wait_s
        else:
            remaining = deadline - loop.time()
            if remaining <= 0:
                yield batch
                batch = []
                deadline = None
                continue
            try:
                item = await asyncio.wait_for(items_q.get(), timeout=remaining)
            except asyncio.TimeoutError:
                yield batch
                batch = []
                deadline = None
                continue
            if item is END:
                if batch:
                    yield batch
                return
            batch.append(item)
        if len(batch) >= batch_size:
            yield batch
            batch = []
            deadline = None

- Strip trailing commas before } or ].

import json, re

_LINE_COMMENT = re.compile(r'(?m)//[^\n]*')
_TRAILING_COMMA = re.compile(r',(\s*[}\]])')

def parse_loose_json(text):
    text = _LINE_COMMENT.sub('', text)
    text = _TRAILING_COMMA.sub(r'\1', text)
    return json.loads(text)

title: Requires Numpy — Loads on First Run
question:
Requires numpy — loads on first Run.

Given two 1-D numpy arrays x and y of equal length, write rolling_corr(x, y, window) that returns an array of Pearson correlations computed over each window of window consecutive points. Output length is len(x) - window + 1. If len(x) < window, return an empty array. Use numpy operations (no Python loop over each window).
solution:
import numpy as np

def rolling_corr(x, y, window):
    x = np.asarray(x, dtype=float)
    y = np.asarray(y, dtype=float)
    n = len(x)
    if n < window or window <= 1:
        return np.array([])
    # Cumulative sums for O(n) window stats
    cx = np.concatenate([[0.0], np.cumsum(x)])
    cy = np.concatenate([[0.0], np.cumsum(y)])
    cxx = np.concatenate([[0.0], np.cumsum(x * x)])
    cyy = np.concatenate([[0.0], np.cumsum(y * y)])
    cxy = np.concatenate([[0.0], np.cumsum(x * y)])
    starts = np.arange(0, n - window + 1)
    ends = starts + window
    sum_x = cx[ends] - cx[starts]
    sum_y = cy[ends] - cy[starts]
    sum_xx = cxx[ends] - cxx[starts]
    sum_yy = cyy[ends] - cyy[starts]
    sum_xy = cxy[ends] - cxy[starts]
    mean_x = sum_x / window
    mean_y = sum_y / window
    cov = (sum_xy / window) - mean_x * mean_y
    var_x = (sum_xx / window) - mean_x ** 2
    var_y = (sum_yy / window) - mean_y ** 2
    denom = np.sqrt(var_x * var_y)
    with np.errstate(divide='ignore', invalid='ignore'):
        out = np.where(denom > 0, cov / denom, 0.0)
    return out

solution:
from collections import defaultdict

def anagram_groups(words):
    groups = defaultdict(list)
    for w in words:
        key = ''.join(sorted(w))
        groups[key].append(w)
    out = [sorted(v) for v in groups.values()]
    out.sort(key=lambda g: g[0])
    return out

title: Requires Pandas — Loads ~10 Mb on First Run

solution:
import pandas as pd

def outliers(df, col, k=1.5):
    q1 = df[col].quantile(0.25)
    q3 = df[col].quantile(0.75)
    iqr = q3 - q1
    lo = q1 - k * iqr
    hi = q3 + k * iqr
    mask = (df[col] < lo) | (df[col] > hi)
    return df[mask]

title: Product_except_self(nums) That, Given a List of Integers, Returns A…
question: Write product_except_self(nums) that, given a list of integers, returns a list where each position i contains the product of every element EXCEPT nums[i]. Constraints: do NOT use division (so the obvious total // nums[i] trick is banned), and run in O(n) time with O(1) auxiliary space beyond the output list. Input domain: a list of ints with len >= 1. For a single-element input the answer is [1] (empty product). For an empty list return [].
solution:
def product_except_self(nums):
    n = len(nums)
    if n == 0:
        return []
    out = [1] * n
    # First pass: out[i] = product of nums[0..i-1] (prefix)
    prefix = 1
    for i in range(n):
        out[i] = prefix
        prefix *= nums[i]
    # Second pass: multiply by product of nums[i+1..n-1] (suffix)
    suffix = 1
    for i in range(n - 1, -1, -1):
        out[i] *= suffix
        suffix *= nums[i]
    return out

title: Reverse_int_safe(x) That Reverses the Digits of a Signed 32-bit Integer
question: Write reverse_int_safe(x) that reverses the digits of a signed 32-bit integer. If the reversed result overflows the signed 32-bit range (less than -231 or greater than 231 - 1), return 0 instead. Sign is preserved. Examples: 123 -> 321, -123 -> -321, 120 -> 21, 1534236469 -> 0 (overflows). Input is always an int.
solution:
def reverse_int_safe(x):
    INT_MIN, INT_MAX = -(2 ** 31), 2 ** 31 - 1
    sign = -1 if x < 0 else 1
    rev = int(str(abs(x))[::-1]) * sign
    if rev < INT_MIN or rev > INT_MAX:
        return 0
    return rev

question: Write median_two_sorted(a, b) that returns the median of two pre-sorted lists of numbers, considering them as a single sorted sequence. Aim for O(m + n) time using a merge-style two-pointer walk (do NOT just concatenate-and-sort; that would be O((m+n) log (m+n))). For an even total length return the average of the two middle elements as a float; for odd return the single middle element. Both inputs are non-empty lists of numbers and individually sorted in ascending order.

title: Daily_temperatures(temps) That, Given a List of Daily Temperatures…
question: Write daily_temperatures(temps) that, given a list of daily temperatures (ints), returns a list answer of the same length where answer[i] is the number of days you must wait AFTER day i to see a strictly warmer temperature. If no future day is warmer, answer[i] is 0. Run in O(n) using a monotonic decreasing stack of indices. Example: [73, 74, 75, 71, 69, 72, 76, 73] returns [1, 1, 4, 2, 1, 1, 0, 0]. Empty input returns [].

title: Largest_rectangle_histogram(heights) That, Given a List Of…
question: Write largest_rectangle_histogram(heights) that, given a list of non-negative integer bar heights, returns the area of the largest axis-aligned rectangle that can be inscribed in the histogram (each bar has width 1, bars sit on a common baseline). Run in O(n) using a monotonic stack. Examples: [2, 1, 5, 6, 2, 3] returns 10 (the 5,6 pair gives a 2-wide rectangle of height 5 = 10). Empty input returns 0. Single bar [h] returns h.
solution:
def largest_rectangle_histogram(heights):
    stack = []  # indices of bars with strictly increasing heights
    best = 0
    # Append a sentinel zero to flush the stack at the end.
    for i, h in enumerate(heights + [0]):
        while stack and heights[stack[-1]] > h:
            top = stack.pop()
            left = stack[-1] if stack else -1
            width = i - left - 1
            best = max(best, heights[top] * width)
        stack.append(i)
    return best

title: Subset_sum_exists(nums, Target) That Returns True If Some Subset Of…

title: Eval_basic_expr(expr) That Evaluates a String Arithmetic Expression…

title: A Simple Observer Pattern
question: Implement a simple observer pattern. Define class EventBus with: subscribe(event, handler) registers a callable for a named event; unsubscribe(event, handler) removes it; publish(event, *args, kwargs) calls every registered handler for that event in subscription order, passing the args through. title: A Deterministic Finite-state Machine
question: Implement a deterministic finite-state machine. Define class StateMachine(initial, transitions) where transitions is a dict mapping (state, event) -> (new_state, action_callable_or_None). title: A Lazy Pipeline Class Pipe(iterable) That Supports Method Chaining…
question: Implement a lazy pipeline class Pipe(iterable) that supports method chaining for map(fn), filter(predicate), and take(n), then a terminal to_list() that materializes the result. title: Range_sum_query(nums) That Returns a Function Query(left, Right)…

title: Two_sum_sorted(nums, Target) That Returns the Tuple (i, J) of Two…

Use sorting + two-pointer for O(n^2) time. Skip duplicate values to keep the result unique.

title: Max_subarray_sum(nums) That Returns the Maximum Sum of Any…
question:

Use Kadane's algorithm — O(n) time, O(1) space.

title: Longest_at_most_k_distinct(s, K) That Returns the Length of The…

title: The Stock-span Problem: for Each Day's Price, Count the Maximum…
question:

Write stock_span(prices) that returns the list of spans. Use a monotonic decreasing stack of (price, span) for amortized O(n) total time — naive nested loops are O(n^2).

title: Next_greater(nums) That for Each Element Returns the Next Strictly…

Use a monotonic stack for O(n) total time.

title: Longest_common_prefix(strs) That Returns the Longest String That Is…

Hint: comparing the lexicographically-smallest and largest strings is a clean trick — characters that match between those two must match in every string in between.

title: Count_palindromes(s) That Returns the Total Number of Palindromic…
question:
Write count_palindromes(s) that returns the TOTAL number of palindromic substrings of s (counting different starting positions as different — 'aaa' has 6: a, a, a, aa, aa, aaa). Single characters count as palindromes.

Use the 'expand around center' technique — try every possible center (odd-length AND even-length) and count each successful expansion. O(n^2) time, O(1) space.
solution:
def count_palindromes(s):
    count = 0
    n = len(s)
    for i in range(n):
        # Odd-length centered at i
        L, R = i, i
        while L >= 0 and R < n and s[L] == s[R]:
            count += 1
            L -= 1
            R += 1
        # Even-length centered between i and i+1
        L, R = i, i + 1
        while L >= 0 and R < n and s[L] == s[R]:
            count += 1
            L -= 1
            R += 1
    return count

title: Class DiffArray(n) for Fast Range Updates + Point Queries (the…
question:
Implement class DiffArray(n) for fast RANGE UPDATES + point queries (the inverse of a prefix-sum range-query):
- __init__(n) — initializes an array of n zeros
- update(i, j, val) — adds val to every position from i to j inclusive in O(1)
- value_at(i) — returns the current value at index i

Use a difference array internally — store deltas, materialize on read via prefix-sum. Multiple updates should be cheap. Assume valid indices 0 <= i <= j < n.

title: Subarray_sum_count(nums, K) That Returns the Number of Contiguous…
question:
Write subarray_sum_count(nums, k) that returns the NUMBER of contiguous subarrays whose elements sum to exactly k. Subarrays may overlap.

Use the prefix-sum + hash-map technique for O(n) time. The brute force O(n^2) (or O(n^3)) won't scale.
solution:
from collections import defaultdict

def subarray_sum_count(nums, k):
    counts = defaultdict(int)
    counts[0] = 1  # empty prefix sums to 0
    total = 0
    cur = 0
    for x in nums:
        cur += x
        total += counts[cur - k]
        counts[cur] += 1
    return total

title: Rotate_array(arr, K) That Rotates the Elements of Arr to the Right…
question:
Write rotate_array(arr, k) that rotates the elements of arr to the RIGHT by k positions IN PLACE (mutate the input list). After return, arr[i] should equal the OLD arr[(i - k) % n]. The function may also return arr for convenience.

k may be negative, zero, or larger than len(arr) — handle via modular arithmetic. Empty list and single-element list are no-ops.

Use the 'three reversals' trick: reverse whole, then reverse first k, then reverse the rest. O(n) time, O(1) extra space.
solution:
def rotate_array(arr, k):
    n = len(arr)
    if n == 0:
        return arr
    k = k % n
    if k == 0:
        return arr
    arr[:] = arr[::-1]
    arr[:k] = arr[:k][::-1]
    arr[k:] = arr[k:][::-1]
    return arr

title: The Chain-of-responsibility Pattern for a Request-handler Pipeline

question:

title: Trap_rain(heights) That Returns the Total Volume of Water That Would…

title: K_closest(points, K) That Returns the K Points (as a List of (x, Y)…

Write k_closest(points, k) that returns the k points (as a list of (x, y) tuples) closest to the origin (0, 0) by Euclidean distance. Return them sorted ascending by distance. If k <= 0 or points is empty, return []. If k >= len(points), return all points sorted by distance.

title: Longest_consecutive(nums) That Returns the Length of the Longest Run…
